topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 : Elementary Signals

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Elementary Signals

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Introduction to Signal Processing for Robotics* (OERAT831), **Elementary Signals** are defined as the fundamental, mathematically well-defined, canonical building-block functions that serve as the atomic vocabulary for representing, analyzing, and synthesizing any arbitrary signal encountered in robotic systems. These signals form the orthogonal basis of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** system analysis, sensor data interpretation, and actuator command synthesis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> "Elementary signals are standardized mathematical functions — such as the unit step, unit impulse, ramp, exponential, and sinusoidal signals — whose properties (continuity, differentiability, integrability, and transform characteristics) are exhaustively tabulated, and which can be linearly combined to construct any real-world signal in robotics such as LiDAR returns, encoder pulses, or motor velocity profiles."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building sentences in the English language. You do not invent a new letter for every new word — instead, you reuse the **26 letters of the alphabet** in different combinations. In exactly the same way, **elementary signals are the "alphabet" of signal processing**.

For instance, when a robot's ultrasonic sensor receives an echo, that complex echo waveform is actually a **sum of simpler signals** (a step, an impulse, an exponential decay). If you understand each letter (elementary signal) in isolation, you can decipher any sentence (any real robotic signal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 Anchor):**
> Every robot perception signal — from a single encoder tick (impulse), to a battery voltage ramp-up (ramp), to a sinusoidal servo dither (sine) — can be decomposed into elementary components. Mastering these components is the **prerequisite for Module 2 (LTI Systems)** and **Module 3 (Z-Transform / Sampling)**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following **standardized signal metrics** must be memorized verbatim for KTU valuation:

- **Signal Energy** $E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ — measured in **Joules (J)**.
- **Signal Power** $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ — measured in **Watts (W)**.
- **Period** $T_0$ — measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Angular Frequency** $\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_0}$ — measured in **radians per second (rad/s)**.
- **Fundamental Period** $N_0$ — for discrete-time signals, measured in **samples**.

### 1.4 Classification of Elementary Signals

For the KTU board examination, elementary signals are classified along **three axes**:

| **Axis** | **Class 1** | **Class 2** | **Class 3** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Domain** | Continuous-Time $x(t)$ | Discrete-Time $x[n]$ | — |
| **Energy** | Energy Signals ($0 < E < \infty$, $P = 0$) | Power Signals ($0 < P < \infty$, $E = \infty$) | Neither |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic | Random | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:** A signal is an **Energy Signal** if its total energy is finite and average power is zero. It is a **Power Signal** if its average power is finite and non-zero. The unit step $u(t)$ and sinusoid are power signals; the unit impulse $\delta(t)$ and decaying exponential are energy signals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Mandatory Elementary Signals

The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 mandates mastery of the following five canonical signals. We dissect each one in terms of its **mathematical form**, **graphical shape**, and **robotics application**.

### 2.1.1 The Unit Step Signal $u(t)$

**Why it matters in Robotics:** A step function models the **instantaneous "ON" command** sent to a DC motor, the sudden engagement of a robotic gripper, or the binary thresholding of a proximity sensor.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Key Properties (Board Favorites):**
- It is a **discontinuous** signal at $t = 0$ (jump discontinuity of magnitude **1**).
- The signal is a **Power Signal** with $P = \frac{1}{2}$ (for $t \in (-\infty, \infty)$) and infinite energy.
- Time-shifted form: $u(t - t_0)$ represents a step occurring at $t = t_0$.

### 2.1.2 The Unit Impulse Signal $\delta(t)$

**Why it matters in Robotics:** Impulses model **instantaneous shocks** — wheel impacts on a tile edge, a sudden collision, a single encoder tick, or the Dirac-delta sampling of a continuous LiDAR scan.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$
\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t = 0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{with} \quad \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t) \, dt = 1
$$

**Key Properties (Board Favorites):**
- The **Sifting Property**: $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0)$.
- It is the **derivative of the unit step**: $\delta(t) = \frac{du(t)}{dt}$.
- It is an **Energy Signal** with $E = 1$.

### 2.1.3 The Unit Ramp Signal $r(t)$

**Why it matters in Robotics:** Ramps model **constant-acceleration velocity profiles** of robot joints at the start of motion, or the linear voltage sweep of a function generator driving a servo.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$
r(t) = \begin{cases} t, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases} \quad \equiv \quad t \cdot u(t)
$$

**Key Properties:**
- It is the **integral of the unit step**: $r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau) \, d\tau$.
- It is a **Power Signal** (energy is infinite, power is infinite too — formally classified as "neither" for $t \in (-\infty, \infty)$, but a power signal for $t \in [0, \infty)$).

### 2.1.4 The Exponential Signal $x(t) = A e^{at}$

**Why it matters in Robotics:** Exponentials model the **discharge of a robot's capacitor bank**, the **thermal decay** of an overheating motor, or the **exponential smoothing** filter used in low-pass sensor fusion.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$
x(t) = A e^{at}, \quad a \in \mathbb{R}
$$

**Sub-Cases (Must Memorize for KTU):**
- If $a > 0$: **Growing exponential** (unstable system response).
- If $a < 0$: **Decaying exponential** (stable system response).
- If $a$ is purely imaginary $a = j\omega_0$: reduces to a **sinusoid**.
- If $a = 0$: reduces to a **DC constant** $A$.

### 2.1.5 The Sinusoidal Signal $x(t) = A \sin(\omega_0 t + \phi)$

**Why it matters in Robotics:** Sinusoids are the **backbone of AC motor control**, **vibration analysis** in robotic arms, and the **Fourier basis** for decomposing any periodic sensor signal.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$
x(t) = A \sin(\omega_0 t + \phi) = A \cos\left(\omega_0 t + \phi - \frac{\pi}{2}\right)
$$

**Key Properties:**
- **Periodicity:** $x(t + T_0) = x(t)$, where $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$.
- **Power:** $P = \frac{A^2}{2}$ for a pure sinusoid.
- **Energy:** Infinite (over infinite time), so it is a **Power Signal**.

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following **high-yield formula table** is the single most important reference for solving Module 1 problems. Memorize every cell.

| **Signal** | **Mathematical Form** | **Energy $E$** | **Power $P$** | **Period $T_0$** | **Signal Class** | **Robotic Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unit Step $u(t)$ | $1$ for $t \geq 0$, else $0$ | $\infty$ | $\frac{1}{2}$ | Non-periodic | Power | Motor ON command |
| Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$ | $\infty$ at $0$, $\int = 1$ | $1$ | $\infty$ | Non-periodic | Energy | Collision / Tick |
| Unit Ramp $r(t)$ | $t$ for $t \geq 0$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Non-periodic | Neither | Constant Accel. |
| Exponential $A e^{at}$ | $A e^{at}$ | $\frac{A^2}{2\vert a \vert}$ (decaying) | $0$ (decaying) | Non-periodic | Energy (if $a < 0$) | Capacitor Discharge |
| Sinusoid $A \sin(\omega_0 t)$ | $A \sin(\omega_0 t)$ | $\infty$ | $\frac{A^2}{2}$ | $\frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$ | Power | AC Servo / Vibration |
| Signum $\text{sgn}(t)$ | $1$ for $t>0$, $-1$ for $t<0$ | $\infty$ | $1$ | Non-periodic | Power | Direction Polarity |
| Rectangular Pulse | $1$ for $\vert t \vert \leq \tau/2$, else $0$ | $\tau$ | $0$ | Non-periodic | Energy | Gating Window |
| Triangular Pulse | $1 - \frac{2\vert t \vert}{\tau}$ for $\vert t \vert \leq \tau/2$ | $\frac{\tau}{3}$ | $0$ | Non-periodic | Energy | Smoothing Kernel |
| Sinc $\text{sinc}(t)$ | $\frac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$ | $1$ | $0$ | Non-periodic | Energy | Sampling Theorem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala University Valuation Tip:** When asked "Is the unit step a power signal or energy signal?", examiners **deduct marks** if you say "energy signal". The correct answer is **Power Signal with $P = 1/2$**. The energy integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} 1^2 dt = \infty$, but the time-averaged power over infinite observation is $\frac{1}{2}$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern robotics, elementary signals are not merely textbook abstractions — they are the operational language of:

- **Digital-to-Analog Converters (DAC):** The output stage produces a **staircase approximation** (sum of delayed step functions) of an ideal analog waveform.
- **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM):** Motor drivers generate **rectangular pulses** (sums of shifted step functions) at high frequency to simulate a DC voltage.
- **LiDAR & Radar:** The received echo is a **train of impulses** convolved with the target's impulse response.
- **Kalman Filtering:** The state-prediction step uses **exponential decay** of covariance matrices.
- **Fourier Analysis:** Any periodic robotic vibration signal decomposes into a sum of **sine and cosine** elementary signals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Showing the Unit Impulse is the Derivative of the Unit Step

This is a **high-frequency KTU 2024 question** worth up to **7 marks** in Part B. We derive the relationship rigorously.

**Starting Point:** Define the unit step function as:

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the Discontinuity:**
The function $u(t)$ is **constant** ($u = 0$) for $t < 0$ and **constant** ($u = 1$) for $t \geq 0$. The only place where the slope (derivative) is non-zero is precisely at the **jump point** $t = 0$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate Each Region:**
Since $u(t)$ is constant in each region, the ordinary derivative is zero everywhere except at $t = 0$:

$$
\frac{du(t)}{dt} = 0 \quad \text{for } t \neq 0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Generalized Derivative:**
To rigorously handle the jump, we use the **distribution theory** or consider a limiting sequence. A standard approach is the rectangular pulse limit. Define a sequence of rectangular pulses $p_\epsilon(t)$ of width $\epsilon$ and height $\frac{1}{\epsilon}$:

$$
p_\epsilon(t) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{\epsilon}, & \vert t \vert < \frac{\epsilon}{2} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the Limit:**
As $\epsilon \to 0$, the pulse becomes infinitely tall and infinitely narrow, while its area remains:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} p_\epsilon(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{\epsilon} \cdot \epsilon = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the Identity:**
In the limit, this sequence converges to the unit impulse:

$$
\delta(t) = \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} p_\epsilon(t) = \frac{du(t)}{dt}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalently (in the integral sense):** The derivative of the step at $t = 0$ is a function that integrates to $1$ over any interval containing $0$, and to $0$ otherwise — this is precisely $\delta(t)$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Signal Energy of the Decaying Exponential

This is a **standard 7-mark derivation** in the KTU 2024 ESE pattern.

**Problem:** Find the energy of $x(t) = A e^{-at} u(t)$, where $a > 0$.

**Step 1 — Recall the Energy Definition:**
For a continuous-time signal, the total energy is:

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Given Signal:**
Since $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, the lower limit is $0$. The integrand becomes:

$$
E = \int_{0}^{+\infty} \vert A e^{-at} \vert^2 \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Squared Modulus:**
For a real-valued exponential, $\vert A e^{-at} \vert^2 = A^2 e^{-2at}$. This gives:

$$
E = A^2 \int_{0}^{+\infty} e^{-2at} \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral:**
Apply the standard integral $\int e^{kt} \, dt = \frac{e^{kt}}{k}$:

$$
E = A^2 \left[ \frac{e^{-2at}}{-2a} \right]_{0}^{+\infty}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Limits:**
As $t \to +\infty$ and $a > 0$, the numerator $e^{-2at} \to 0$. At $t = 0$, $e^0 = 1$. Therefore:

$$
E = A^2 \left( 0 - \frac{1}{-2a} \right) = A^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2a}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
E = \frac{A^2}{2a}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for Robotics:** The factor $\frac{1}{2a}$ tells us that a **faster-decaying** exponential (larger $a$) has **less energy** — this is why RC low-pass filters with small time constants are used in high-speed robotic sensors to suppress high-frequency noise efficiently.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time-Shifting, Time-Scaling, and Time-Reversal

This is a **must-know transformation skill** for KTU graphs and signals questions.

**Step 1 — Time Shifting $x(t - t_0)$:**
Replacing $t$ with $(t - t_0)$ shifts the entire graph to the **right** by $t_0$ seconds. For example, $u(t - 3)$ is the unit step starting at $t = 3$. The KTU board explicitly tests this in graph-sketch questions (typically 4-7 marks).

**Step 2 — Time Scaling $x(at)$:**
Replacing $t$ with $at$ compresses (if $\vert a \vert > 1$) or expands (if $\vert a \vert < 1$) the graph. For example, $x(2t)$ is **twice as fast**.

**Step 3 — Time Reversal $x(-t)$:**
Replacing $t$ with $-t$ mirrors the graph about the **vertical axis**. For example, $u(-t)$ is a step that turns ON at $t = 0$ going backwards in time — it equals $1$ for $t \leq 0$ and $0$ for $t > 0$.

**Step 4 — Combined Operation $x(-at + b)$:**
The standard KTU procedure is:

$$
x(-at + b) = x\left(-a\left(t - \frac{b}{a}\right)\right)
$$

First apply **time reversal**, then **time scaling by $a$**, then **time shift by $\frac{b}{a}$**.

**Step 5 — Example Application:**
For $x(t) = u(t)$, find $y(t) = u(-2t + 4)$:
- Rewrite: $y(t) = u\left(-2\left(t - 2\right)\right)$.
- Step A (Shift): $u(t - 2)$ → step ON at $t = 2$.
- Step B (Reverse): $u(-(t - 2)) = u(2 - t)$ → step ON for $t < 2$.
- Step C (Scale): $u(-2(t - 2))$ → step ON for $t < 2$, with a **sharper** jump (slope doubled).

$$
u(-2t + 4) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \leq 2 \\ 0, & t > 2 \end{cases}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Signal Identification Checklist

The following matrix is the **valuation key** examiners use to award partial credit in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions. Use it to structure your answer.

| **Identification Criterion** | **Question to Ask** | **Expected Answer Format** |
|---|---|---|
| Energy or Power? | Compute $E$ and $P$ | "$x(t)$ is an energy signal because $E = \frac{A^2}{2a} < \infty$ and $P = 0$." |
| Periodic or Aperiodic? | Does $x(t + T_0) = x(t)$ for some $T_0 > 0$? | "Non-periodic (or periodic with period $T_0 = \ldots$)" |
| Even or Odd? | Does $x(-t) = x(t)$ or $x(-t) = -x(t)$? | "Even: $x(-t) = x(t)$" or "Odd: $x(-t) = -x(t)$" |
| Causal or Non-Causal? | Is $x(t) = 0$ for all $t < 0$? | "Causal, since $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$" |
| Continuous or Discrete? | Is the domain $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{Z}$? | "Continuous-time signal" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Elementary Signal Generation in a Robotic Sensor Pipeline

The following Mermaid block illustrates how a **raw LiDAR echo** in a robot is decomposed into a sum of elementary signals. This kind of "system-level intuition" diagram is often asked in **14-mark KTU problems** as a setup for convolution and LTI analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw LiDAR Echo Signal x-of-t] --> B[Analog Front End]
    B --> C[Sampler]
    C --> D[Digital Signal Processor]
    D --> E[Signal Decomposition Engine]
    E --> F1[Unit Impulse Component]
    E --> F2[Exponential Decay Component]
    E --> F3[Step Edge Component]
    E --> F4[Sinusoidal Carrier Component]
    F1 --> G[Object Distance Estimator]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Robot Obstacle Avoidance Output]

    A -.-> A1[Time Domain Waveform]
    A -.-> A2[Frequency Domain Spectrum]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#FF6347,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF
    style F1 fill:#FFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F2 fill:#FFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F3 fill:#FFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F4 fill:#FFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Interpretation for KTU Examination:**
The diagram conveys that **every complex robotic sensor signal** is a linear combination of the four elementary signal types we have studied. The DSP block performs the decomposition, and the estimator uses the **parameters** of each component (timing of impulse, decay rate of exponential, edge of step, frequency of sinusoid) to deduce physical meaning (distance, material, motion, signature).

## 4.2 State-Transition Diagram — Energy / Power Classification Logic

The following Mermaid flow chart provides a **deterministic decision procedure** that examiners expect students to demonstrate when classifying a given signal in a 3-mark KTU Part A question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start with Signal x-of-t] --> Q1{Is Total Energy E Finite?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is Average Power P Equal to 0?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> E1[Result: ENERGY SIGNAL]
    Q2 -- No --> P1[Result: NEITHER ENERGY NOR POWER]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is Average Power P Finite and Non-Zero?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> P2[Result: POWER SIGNAL]
    Q3 -- No --> P3[Result: NEITHER ENERGY NOR POWER]

    style S0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Decision Logic (Verbal Form for Board Answer):**

> "Given $x(t)$, I first compute $E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$. If $0 < E < \infty$, it is an **energy signal** with $P = 0$. If $E = \infty$, I compute $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$. If $0 < P < \infty$, it is a **power signal**. Otherwise, it is **neither**."

## 4.3 Graphical Schematic of All Five Elementary Signals

The following Mermaid block represents the **sketching template** KTU examiners use as the model answer for 7-mark "sketch and label" questions.


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]

**Define the unit step signal $u(t)$ and the unit impulse signal $\delta(t)$. State two properties of each.**

**Model Answer (Board Standard):**

**Unit Step Signal:** The unit step signal is defined as:

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}
$$

It represents a signal that switches instantaneously from **0** to **1** at $t = 0$. It is a **discontinuous** signal with a jump of magnitude $1$ at the origin.

**Properties of $u(t)$:**
1. It is a **causal** signal (zero for $t < 0$).
2. It is a **power signal** with average power $P = \frac{1}{2}$ and infinite energy.

**Unit Impulse Signal:** The unit impulse signal is defined as:

$$
\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t = 0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{with} \quad \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t) \, dt = 1
$$

It is the **derivative of the unit step** and represents an idealized instantaneous event.

**Properties of $\delta(t)$:**
1. **Sifting property:** $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0)$.
2. It is an **energy signal** with $E = 1$.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper 1]

**Determine whether the signal $x(t) = 5 e^{-3t} u(t)$ is an energy signal, a power signal, or neither. Justify your answer with calculations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute Energy:**

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} (5)^2 e^{-6t} \, dt = 25 \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t} \, dt
$$

$$
E = 25 \cdot \left[ \frac{e^{-6t}}{-6} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 25 \cdot \left( 0 - \frac{1}{-6} \right) = \frac{25}{6} \approx 4.17 \text{ Joules}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Power:**

$$
P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \cdot E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{25}{12T} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude:** Since $0 < E = \frac{25}{6} < \infty$ and $P = 0$, the signal $x(t)$ is an **Energy Signal**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, With Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Question Paper Module 1, Q.1(a) Internal Choice]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Sketch the following signals for $t \in [-3, 3]$: (i) $x_1(t) = u(t) - u(t-2)$ (ii) $x_2(t) = r(t) - 2r(t-1) + r(t-2)$. Identify whether each is an energy or power signal.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Rectangular Pulse $x_1(t)$:**

Step 1 — Analyze the expression. $u(t)$ is **1** for $t \geq 0$ and **0** otherwise. $u(t-2)$ is **1** for $t \geq 2$ and **0** otherwise.

Step 2 — Subtract them:

$$
x_1(t) = u(t) - u(t-2) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1, & 0 \leq t < 2 \\ 0, & t \geq 2 \end{cases}
$$

Step 3 — Sketch: A rectangular pulse of height **1** spanning $t \in [0, 2)$.

Step 4 — Energy/Power:

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x_1(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{2} 1^2 \, dt = 2 \text{ J}
$$

Since $0 < E < \infty$, it is an **Energy Signal**.

**Part (ii) — Triangular Pulse $x_2(t)$:**

Step 1 — Analyze each ramp:
- $r(t) = t$ for $t \geq 0$, else $0$.
- $r(t-1) = (t-1)$ for $t \geq 1$, else $0$.
- $r(t-2) = (t-2)$ for $t \geq 2$, else $0$.

Step 2 — Build piecewise:

- For $t < 0$: $x_2(t) = 0$.
- For $0 \leq t < 1$: $x_2(t) = t - 0 + 0 = t$.
- For $1 \leq t < 2$: $x_2(t) = t - 2(t-1) + 0 = -t + 2$.
- For $t \geq 2$: $x_2(t) = t - 2(t-1) + (t-2) = 0$.

Step 3 — Sketch: A **triangular pulse** rising linearly from 0 to 1 over $t \in [0, 1]$, then falling linearly from 1 to 0 over $t \in [1, 2]$.

Step 4 — Energy/Power:

$$
E = \int_{0}^{1} t^2 \, dt + \int_{1}^{2} (-t+2)^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3} \text{ J}
$$

It is an **Energy Signal**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**For the signal $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$, perform the following operations and sketch each result: (i) Time reversal $x(-t)$ (ii) Time shift $x(t-1)$ (iii) Time scale $x(2t)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Time Reversal $x(-t) = e^{-2(-t)} u(-t) = e^{2t} u(-t)$:**

For $t > 0$, $u(-t) = 0$, so $x(-t) = 0$.
For $t \leq 0$, $u(-t) = 1$, so $x(-t) = e^{2t}$.

The signal is a **growing exponential extending to the left** from $t = 0$, reaching $1$ at $t = 0$ and $e^{-2}$ at $t = -1$.

**Part (ii) — Time Shift $x(t-1) = e^{-2(t-1)} u(t-1)$:**

For $t < 1$, the signal is $0$. For $t \geq 1$, it is $e^{-2(t-1)}$. The decaying exponential is **delayed by 1 second**, starting at $t = 1$ with value $1$ and decaying from there.

**Part (iii) — Time Scale $x(2t) = e^{-4t} u(2t)$:**

Since $u(2t) = u(t)$ (the step is invariant to positive scaling), $x(2t) = e^{-4t} u(t)$. The new decay constant is **4** instead of **2**, so the exponential decays **twice as fast** (compressed in time).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Sketch Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to indicate value 0 or 1 at discontinuities.** Always label $u(0) = 1$ explicitly. Some texts define it as 0.5, but KTU expects **1**.
> 2. **Incorrect sign on time-reversed exponentials.** Students often write $e^{2t}$ for $t > 0$ when reversing $e^{-2t}u(t)$ — this is wrong. The reversal mirrors the support to $t \leq 0$.
> 3. **Missing the causal region ($t < 0$ part).** Always draw the zero baseline for $t < 0$ in causal signals.
> 4. **Confusing $r(t-1)$ with $r(t) - r(t-1)$.** The former is a ramp that **starts at 1**, not a triangle.

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Paper Module 1, Q.2(a) Internal Choice]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**For the signal $x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$, (i) find the total energy and average power, (ii) state whether it is periodic and find the fundamental period, (iii) state the conditions for even/odd symmetry.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Energy and Power:**

Energy:

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} A^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) \, dt
$$

Using the identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$
E = A^2 \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2} \, dt = \infty
$$

Average Power:

$$
P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} A^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) \, dt
$$

Since the time-average of $\cos^2$ is $\frac{1}{2}$:

$$
P = A^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{A^2}{2} \text{ W}
$$

**Part (ii) — Periodicity:**
$x(t)$ is **periodic** with fundamental period $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$ because:

$$
x(t + T_0) = A \cos(\omega_0(t + T_0) + \phi) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + 2\pi + \phi) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = x(t)
$$

**Part (iii) — Symmetry:**
- $x(t)$ is **even** if $\phi = 0$ (giving $A \cos(\omega_0 t)$).
- $x(t)$ is **odd** if $\phi = \frac{\pi}{2}$ (giving $-A \sin(\omega_0 t)$).
- For any other $\phi$, the signal is **neither even nor odd**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Express the rectangular pulse $p(t) = u(t+\tau/2) - u(t-\tau/2)$ as a sum of time-shifted unit steps, and hence find its energy.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recognition:** The signal $p(t)$ is already expressed as a sum (with subtraction) of two time-shifted unit steps. We rewrite the subtraction as the sum of a positive step and a negative-shifted step:

$$
p(t) = u(t + \tau/2) + (-1) \cdot u(t - \tau/2)
$$

**Step 2 — Piecewise Form:**

$$
p(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & -\tau/2 \leq t < \tau/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Calculation:**

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert p(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} 1^2 \, dt = \tau
$$

**Step 4 — Classification:** Since $0 < E = \tau < \infty$, the rectangular pulse is an **Energy Signal**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Part B:**
> 1. **Averaging over infinite time:** Students often forget to compute the limit $T \to \infty$ in the power formula. Always write the limit explicitly.
> 2. **Missing factor of 2 in $\cos^2$ identity:** The expansion $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1+\cos(2\theta)}{2}$ is non-negotiable. A common slip is writing $\frac{1+\cos(2\theta)}{1}$ instead.
> 3. **Forgetting to specify the phase for symmetry:** A cosine with $\phi \neq 0, \pi/2$ is **neither** even nor odd. Many students incorrectly label it as even because they "see" a cosine.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 1 — Elementary Signals:**

- **Unit Step $u(t)$:** On at $t=0$, value **1** thereafter. Power signal with $P = 1/2$. The **building block** for constructing all causal signals.
- **Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$:** Infinite at $t=0$, area = 1. The **derivative of the step** and the **identity element of convolution**. Energy $E = 1$.
- **Unit Ramp $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$:** Linear slope of 1 starting at $t=0$. Integral of $u(t)$. Models constant acceleration.
- **Exponential $A e^{at}$:** If $a < 0$ → decaying (energy signal). If $a > 0$ → growing (neither). Pure imaginary $a = j\omega_0$ → sinusoidal.
- **Sinusoid $A \sin(\omega_0 t + \phi)$:** Periodic with $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$. Power = $\frac{A^2}{2}$. Even if $\phi = 0$ (cosine); odd if $\phi = \frac{\pi}{2}$ (sine).
- **Signum $\text{sgn}(t)$:** Equals $1$ for $t > 0$, $-1$ for $t < 0$. Power signal with $P = 1$. Models direction/orientation in robotics.
- **Rectangular Pulse $p(t) = u(t+\tau/2) - u(t-\tau/2)$:** Energy $E = \tau$. Used as a windowing function.
- **Triangular Pulse:** Energy $E = \frac{\tau}{3}$. Used as a smoothing kernel in DSP.
- **Sinc Function $\text{sinc}(t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$:** Energy $E = 1$. The **Fourier transform of the rectangular pulse** (sampling theorem foundation).
- **Energy vs Power Decision Algorithm:** Compute $E$ first. Finite $E$ → energy signal. Infinite $E$ → compute $P$. Finite, non-zero $P$ → power signal. Both infinite → neither.
- **Time-Shift Rule:** $x(t - t_0)$ → shift **right** by $t_0$. $x(t + t_0)$ → shift **left** by $t_0$.
- **Time-Scale Rule:** $x(at)$ with $\vert a \vert > 1$ → compress. $\vert a \vert < 1$ → expand.
- **Time-Reversal Rule:** $x(-t)$ → mirror about the vertical axis.
- **Sifting Property:** $\int x(t) \delta(t - t_0) dt = x(t_0)$ — appears in **every** KTU Module 1 paper.
- **Robotics Context:** Step → motor ON; Impulse → sensor tick; Ramp → acceleration; Exponential → thermal/RC decay; Sinusoid → AC servo and vibration.
- **Common Pitfall:** Confusing $r(t) - r(t-1)$ (a triangle) with $r(t-1)$ (a shifted ramp). The KTU board tests this distinction in 7-mark sketch questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Time and Discrete Time Signals and Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuous Time and Discrete Time Signals and Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Continuous Time and Discrete Time Signals and Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Signal?

In the formal KTU engineering lexicon, a **signal** is a function of one or more independent variables that carries information about the behavior or nature of a physical phenomenon. In robotics, this independent variable is almost always **time ($t$ or $n$)**, and the signal value represents a measurable quantity such as joint angle, motor velocity, voltage, force, or sensor intensity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **signal** $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ is a mathematical description of a physical quantity as a function of an independent variable (most commonly time). Signals are the fundamental objects upon which **systems** operate.

### 1.2 Two Fundamental Axes of Classification

Every signal in this module can be classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

**Axis A — The Nature of the Independent Variable:**
- **Continuous-Time (CT) Signal $x(t)$**: Defined for every real value of $t \in \mathbb{R}$ within some interval. The signal amplitude can also be continuous.
- **Discrete-Time (DT) Signal $x[n]$**: Defined only at discrete instants, typically $n \in \mathbb{Z}$, where $n$ is an integer index.

**Axis B — The Nature of the Amplitude:**
- **Continuous-Amplitude**: The signal value $x$ can take any real value.
- **Discrete-Amplitude (Quantized)**: The signal value is restricted to a finite set (e.g., a digital ADC output).

The four resulting categories are summarized below, and the KTU examiner's favorite is the **Continuous-Time, Continuous-Amplitude** (analog) vs **Discrete-Time, Continuous-Amplitude** (sampled) distinction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A signal is **analog** if it is continuous in both time and amplitude. A signal is **digital** if it is discrete in both time and amplitude. A signal that is discrete in time but continuous in amplitude is simply called a **sampled** or **discrete-time** signal.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Robot's Eyes and Brain

Imagine a robot navigating a corridor. Its **LIDAR sensor** continuously sweeps a laser beam, producing a voltage that is defined for *every* microsecond of operation. This is a **continuous-time signal** $x(t)$. Now imagine a microcontroller sampling that LIDAR voltage every **10 milliseconds** to free up processing resources — it records a sequence of numbers: $x[0]=1.2\text{ V}$, $x[1]=1.4\text{ V}$, $x[2]=1.3\text{ V}, \dots$ This indexed sequence is a **discrete-time signal** $x[n]$.

- The continuous signal is like a **smooth, unbroken film reel** of the corridor.
- The discrete signal is like a **photo album** — a series of still frames captured at regular intervals.

> [!TIP]
> **Robotics Insight:** Almost every real robotic sensor (camera, IMU, encoder) is *continuous* in nature, but every embedded processor (Arduino, STM32, Raspberry Pi) deals with *discrete* data. The art of signal processing for robotics is bridging this gap intelligently.

### 1.4 What is a System?

A **system** is a mathematical operator that maps an input signal $x(\cdot)$ to an output signal $y(\cdot)$. In KTU notation:

$$y(\cdot) = T\{x(\cdot)\}$$

where $T\{\cdot\}$ denotes the **system transformation** (e.g., a filter, a differentiator, a robot's controller, a low-pass amplifier).

| Domain | Input | Output | Transformation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Continuous | $x(t)$ | $y(t)$ | $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$ |
| Discrete | $x[n]$ | $y[n]$ | $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$ |

### 1.5 The Four Canonical Signal/System Pairings in Robotics

1. **CT input → CT output** : A purely analog robot audio amplifier.
2. **CT input → DT output** : A/D conversion of a sensor reading.
3. **DT input → CT output** : D/A conversion feeding a motor driver.
4. **DT input → DT output** : A digital FIR filter inside an STM32 controller.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing *discrete-time* with *digital*. A signal can be **discrete in time** but **continuous in amplitude** (e.g., a sampled analog sensor output before quantization). It only becomes *digital* after the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** quantizes the amplitude.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Representation Standards (KTU 2024 Convention)

| Domain | Independent Variable | Notation | Domain Set |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Continuous-Time (CT) | $t$ | $x(t)$ | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Discrete-Time (DT) | $n$ | $x[n]$ | $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |

The relationship between a CT signal and a DT version obtained by **uniform sampling at period $T_s$** is:

$$x[n] = x(nT_s)$$

The sampling frequency is $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ Hz, and the angular sampling frequency is $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_s}$ rad/s.

## 2.2 The Five Standard Test Signals (Must Memorize for KTU)

These are the "vocabulary" of signal processing. KTU questions almost always feature at least one of them.

### (a) Unit Impulse Signal
The discrete unit impulse (Kronecker delta) and the continuous unit impulse (Dirac delta) are fundamental:

$$\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases} \qquad \delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t = 0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

Key property: $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1$.

### (b) Unit Step Signal
$$u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases} \qquad u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

Relationship: $\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$ and $u[n] = \displaystyle\sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta[k]$.

### (c) Unit Ramp Signal
$$r[n] = n \cdot u[n] \qquad r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$$

### (d) Exponential Signal
$$x(t) = e^{at} \quad (a \in \mathbb{R}) \qquad x[n] = a^n \quad (a \in \mathbb{R})$$

### (e) Sinusoidal Signal
$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega t + \phi) \qquad x[n] = A \cos(\Omega n + \phi)$$

Note the critical distinction: in $x(t)$, $\omega$ is **radians per second** (real), while in $x[n]$, $\Omega$ is **radians per sample** (real, but always interpreted modulo $2\pi$).

## 2.3 Elementary Signal Operations

These operations are bread-and-butter for KTU problems:

1. **Time Shifting:** $x(t-t_0)$ shifts the signal **right** by $t_0$ if $t_0>0$.
2. **Time Reversal (Folding):** $x(-t)$ mirrors the signal about the vertical axis.
3. **Time Scaling:** $x(2t)$ compresses, $x(t/2)$ expands the signal.
4. **Amplitude Scaling:** $A \cdot x(t)$ multiplies every value by constant $A$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** When asked to sketch $y(t) = x(-2t+3)$, always perform the operations in this order: (1) **Shift right by 3** → $x(t+3)$, (2) **Scale by 2** → $x(2t+3)$, (3) **Reverse** → $x(-2t+3)$. Mark these steps clearly in your answer script.

## 2.4 Classification of Signals

| Property | Test | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Deterministic vs Random | Can the signal be described by a closed-form equation? | $x(t) = \sin(t)$ is deterministic; sensor noise is random |
| Periodic vs Aperiodic | Does $x(t+T) = x(t)$ for some $T>0$? | $\sin(t)$ is periodic; $e^{-t}u(t)$ is aperiodic |
| Even vs Odd | Is $x(-t) = x(t)$ or $x(-t) = -x(t)$? | $\cos(t)$ is even; $\sin(t)$ is odd |
| Energy vs Power | Finite $E$ or finite $P$? | Decaying exponentials are energy signals; sinusoids are power signals |

### 2.4.1 Energy and Power — The KTU 14-Mark Favorite

The **total energy** of a signal is:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t)\vert^2\,dt \quad \text{(CT)} \qquad E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n]\vert^2 \quad \text{(DT)}$$

The **average power** is:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t)\vert^2\,dt \quad \text{(CT)} \qquad P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n]\vert^2 \quad \text{(DT)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Energy vs Power Cheat Sheet:** A signal is an **energy signal** if $0 < E < \infty$ (which forces $P = 0$). A signal is a **power signal** if $0 < P < \infty$ (which forces $E = \infty$). A signal is **neither** if both diverge. A signal is **both** only for the trivial zero signal.

## 2.5 Classification of Systems

A **system** $y = T\{x\}$ can be classified by four key properties — these are the core of every KTU 14-mark question on systems:

### 2.5.1 Linearity (Superposition + Homogeneity)
A system is **linear** if, for any two inputs $x_1, x_2$ and scalars $a, b$:

$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a\,T\{x_1\} + b\,T\{x_2\}$$

### 2.5.2 Time Invariance
A system is **time-invariant (TI)** if a time shift in the input produces an identical time shift in the output:

$$\text{If } y(t) = T\{x(t)\}, \text{ then } y(t-t_0) = T\{x(t-t_0)\} \quad \forall t_0$$

### 2.5.3 Causality
A system is **causal** if the output at any time depends only on present and past inputs (never future inputs):

$$y(t) \text{ depends only on } x(\tau), \tau \leq t$$

### 2.5.4 Stability (BIBO — Bounded-Input Bounded-Output)
A system is **BIBO stable** if every bounded input produces a bounded output:

$$\vert x(t)\vert \leq M < \infty \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert y(t)\vert < \infty \quad \forall t$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Continuous-Time Formula | Discrete-Time Formula |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Sampling relationship | $x(t) = x(nT_s)$ | $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ |
| 2 | Periodicity | $x(t) = x(t+T)$ | $x[n] = x[n+N]$ |
| 3 | Fundamental period (sinusoid) | $T = 2\pi \cdot m / \omega$ | $N = 2\pi \cdot m / \Omega$ |
| 4 | Energy | $E = \int \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt$ | $E = \sum \vert x[n]\vert^2$ |
| 5 | Power | $P = \lim \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T}\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt$ | $P = \lim \frac{1}{2N+1}\sum_{-N}^{N}\vert x[n]\vert^2$ |
| 6 | Even decomposition | $x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}[x(t)+x(-t)]$ | $x_e[n] = \frac{1}{2}[x[n]+x[-n]]$ |
| 7 | Odd decomposition | $x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}[x(t)-x(-t)]$ | $x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2}[x[n]-x[-n]]$ |
| 8 | Linearity test | $T\{ax_1+bx_2\} = ay_1+by_2$ | Same form with $n$ |
| 9 | Time invariance test | Compare $T\{x(t-t_0)\}$ vs $y(t-t_0)$ | Compare $T\{x[n-n_0]\}$ vs $y[n-n_0]$ |
| 10 | BIBO Stability (LTI) | $\int \vert h(t)\vert dt < \infty$ | $\sum \vert h[n]\vert < \infty$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in robotics:** A robot arm's velocity controller is typically **linear and time-invariant** under small-signal assumptions. Its stability is determined by impulse-response integrability (formula 10). Path planners must be **causal** to be physically realizable. Mastering these four properties is your gateway to module-2 stability analysis.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LiDAR & Cameras in Robots:** Sampled to discrete-time before any algorithm touches them. Aliasing (covered later) is governed by the sampling theorem.
- **PID Controllers:** The integrator is a linear, time-invariant, **causal but marginally stable** system — knowing these properties is critical for tuning.
- **IMU Drift:** Drift signals are **random, low-power, infinite-energy** signals — they are **power signals**, not energy signals.
- **Voice Assistants:** Speech signals are energy signals with finite duration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always state the **assumptions**, then write the formula, substitute, simplify, and **box the final answer**. Marks are awarded for each visible step.

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy and Power of a Discrete-Time Rectangular Pulse

**Problem.** Consider the discrete-time signal:
$$x[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq n \leq 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Determine whether $x[n]$ is an energy signal, a power signal, both, or neither.

### Step 1 — Compute the Energy $E$

Using the DT energy formula:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n]\vert^2$$

Substitute the definition of $x[n]$:

$$E = \sum_{n=0}^{3} (1)^2 + \sum_{n \notin [0,3]} (0)^2$$

Evaluate both sums:

$$E = 4 \cdot 1 + 0 = 4 \text{ units}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Average Power $P$

Using the DT power formula:

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n]\vert^2$$

The numerator is a fixed finite number (4), but the denominator grows without bound. So:

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{4}{2N+1} = 0 \text{ units}$$

### Step 3 — Classify the Signal

Since $0 < E = 4 < \infty$ and $P = 0$, the signal is an **energy signal only**.

> [!TIP]
> **General result:** Any signal with **finite duration** and **bounded amplitude** is always a **pure energy signal**. This is a useful shortcut to write on the first line of your answer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Periodicity Test of a Discrete-Time Sinusoid

**Problem.** Determine whether the signal $x[n] = \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4} n + \dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$ is periodic. If yes, find the fundamental period $N$.

### Step 1 — Write the Periodicity Condition

By definition, $x[n]$ is periodic with period $N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ if:

$$x[n+N] = x[n] \quad \forall n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $n \to n+N$

$$x[n+N] = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}(n+N) + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}n + \frac{\pi}{4}N + \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Equate to $x[n]$ Using Cosine Periodicity

We need:

$$\frac{\pi}{4}N + \frac{\pi}{6} = 2\pi k + \frac{\pi}{6} \quad \text{for some integer } k$$

Cancel $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ from both sides:

$$\frac{\pi}{4}N = 2\pi k$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Smallest Positive Integer $N$

$$N = 8k$$

The smallest positive integer $N$ is obtained at $k=1$:

$$\boxed{N = 8}$$

Hence $x[n]$ is **periodic with fundamental period $N = 8$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Watchout:** Unlike $x(t) = \cos(\omega t)$, where **any** real $\omega$ gives a periodic signal, a discrete sinusoid $x[n] = \cos(\Omega n)$ is periodic **only if $\dfrac{\Omega}{2\pi}$ is a rational number** $\dfrac{p}{q}$ in lowest terms. The fundamental period is then $N = q$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Time-Shift and Time-Scale Cascade

**Problem.** Given $x(t) = \sin(\pi t) \cdot u(t)$, find and sketch $y(t) = x(-2t + 3)$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Order of Operations

From the argument $-2t+3$, factor out $-2$:

$$-2t + 3 = -2\left(t - \frac{3}{2}\right)$$

This tells us the operations in order:
1. Shift $x(t)$ right by $\frac{3}{2}$: gives $x\left(t - \frac{3}{2}\right)$.
2. Compress the result by factor 2: gives $x\left(2t - \frac{3}{2}\right)$.
3. Reverse the result about the vertical axis: gives $x\left(-2t + 3\right)$.

### Step 2 — Mathematical Substitution

$$y(t) = \sin\left[\pi\left(-2t + 3\right)\right] \cdot u\left(-2t + 3\right)$$

Simplify using angle identities:

$$\sin(-2\pi t + 3\pi) = -\sin(2\pi t - 3\pi) = -\sin(2\pi t - \pi) = \sin(2\pi t)$$

And the support condition $u(-2t+3)=1$ implies $-2t+3 \geq 0 \Rightarrow t \leq \frac{3}{2}$:

$$y(t) = \sin(2\pi t) \cdot u\left(\frac{3}{2} - t\right)$$

### Step 3 — Sketch the Result

The original $x(t) = \sin(\pi t)u(t)$ is a half-sine of duration 2 (from $t=0$ to $t=2$). The transformed $y(t)$ is a **time-reversed, compressed sine burst** that:
- Starts at $t=0$ (peak of the sine).
- Has **twice the frequency** ($2\pi$ vs $\pi$).
- Terminates at $t = \frac{3}{2}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Robotics Interpretation:** This operation is exactly what happens when a robot plays back a **recorded trajectory in reverse at double speed** — a common motion-planning test for hysteresis in motor controllers.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Even/Odd Decomposition

**Problem.** Decompose $x[n] = \{2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ (where the underline indicates the $n=0$ term) into even and odd parts.

### Step 1 — Reverse the Signal

$$x[-n] = \{6, 5, 4, 3, 2\}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Even Part

$$x_e[n] = \frac{1}{2}\left(x[n] + x[-n]\right)$$

Add element-wise:

$$x[n] + x[-n] = \{2+6,\ 3+5,\ 4+4,\ 5+3,\ 6+2\} = \{8, 8, 8, 8, 8\}$$

Divide by 2:

$$\boxed{x_e[n] = \{4, 4, 4, 4, 4\}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Odd Part

$$x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2}\left(x[n] - x[-n]\right)$$

Subtract element-wise:

$$x[n] - x[-n] = \{2-6,\ 3-5,\ 4-4,\ 5-3,\ 6-2\} = \{-4, -2, 0, 2, 4\}$$

Divide by 2:

$$\boxed{x_o[n] = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}}$$

### Step 4 — Verification

$$x_e[n] + x_o[n] = \{4-2,\ 4-1,\ 4+0,\ 4+1,\ 4+2\} = \{2, 3, 4, 5, 6\} = x[n] \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's View:** Always show the **verification step**. It is worth 1 mark on its own and demonstrates command of the procedure.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Linearity Test of a System

**Problem.** Test the system $y[n] = 2x[n] + 3$ for linearity.

### Step 1 — Apply the Superposition Test

Let $x_1[n]$ produce $y_1[n] = 2x_1[n] + 3$.
Let $x_2[n]$ produce $y_2[n] = 2x_2[n] + 3$.

For a linear combination input $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$, the system output is:

$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = 2(a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]) + 3 = 2a x_1[n] + 2b x_2[n] + 3$$

### Step 2 — Apply Linearity Hypothesis

$$a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] = a(2x_1[n] + 3) + b(2x_2[n] + 3) = 2a x_1[n] + 2b x_2[n] + 3(a+b)$$

### Step 3 — Compare the Two Expressions

$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = 2a x_1[n] + 2b x_2[n] + 3$$
$$a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] = 2a x_1[n] + 2b x_2[n] + 3(a+b)$$

These are equal **only if $3 = 3(a+b)$**, i.e., $a+b=1$, which is not generally true.

### Step 4 — Conclusion

The system is **non-linear** (it fails the homogeneity test alone, since $T\{0\} = 3 \neq 0$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Constant terms are the giveaway:** Any system of the form $y = (\text{linear part}) + \text{constant}$ is **non-linear**. This is the fastest way to mark a system non-linear in a KTU exam.

---

## 3.6 Summary of Analytical Traps

| Trap | KTU-Style Question Form | Safe Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Continuous vs Discrete sinusoid periodicity | "Is $\cos(0.3\pi n)$ periodic?" | Convert $\Omega/(2\pi)$ to a fraction; check rationality |
| $E$ vs $P$ confusion | "Is the signal energy or power?" | Compute both; if $E$ finite, $P=0$; if $P$ finite, $E=\infty$ |
| Time-scaling order | "Sketch $x(2t-4)$" | Factor first: $2(t-2)$; shift right by 2, then compress by 2 |
| Linearity test mistake | "Is $y = x^2$ linear?" | Always test $T\{0\}=0$ first; constant or quadratic terms fail |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Signal Classification Master Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SIGNAL] --> B[Continuous-Time x of t]
    A --> C[Discrete-Time x of n]
    B --> D[Analog: Continuous Amp]
    B --> E[Continuous Amplitude Sampled]
    C --> F[Discrete Amp Sampled and Quantized]
    C --> G[Continuous Amplitude Sampled]
    B --> H{Periodic?}
    H --> H1[Yes: x of t plus T equals x of t]
    H --> H2[No: Aperiodic]
    C --> I{Periodic?}
    I --> I1[Yes: x of n plus N equals x of n]
    I --> I2[No: Aperiodic]
    B --> J{Energy or Power?}
    J --> J1[Energy: 0 less than E less than infinity]
    J --> J2[Power: 0 less than P less than infinity]
    C --> K{Energy or Power?}
    K --> K1[Energy: 0 less than E less than infinity]
    K --> K2[Power: 0 less than P less than infinity]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Robotic Signal Processing Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    src1[Physical World: Joint angle, velocity, force] --> sensor[Analog Sensor: Encoder, IMU, Strain gauge]
    sensor --> ct1[Continuous Time Signal x of t]
    ct1 --> adc[Anti-aliasing Filter LPF]
    adc --> adc2[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    adc2 --> dt1[Discrete Time Signal x of n]
    dt1 --> proc[Digital Processor: FIR, IIR, FFT, PID]
    proc --> dt2[Processed Output y of n]
    dt2 --> dac[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    dac --> hold[Zero Order Hold]
    hold --> ct2[Continuous Actuator Signal y of t]
    ct2 --> act[Robot Actuator: Motor, Servo, Speaker]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure Insight:** The shaded conversion blocks (ADC and DAC) are precisely where continuous-time signals become discrete-time, and vice versa. The **anti-aliasing filter** before the ADC is a critically important CT system — without it, high-frequency content will fold back into the processed signal (a phenomenon called aliasing, covered in a later module).

## 4.3 Block Diagram of System Classification Properties

```mermaid
graph TD
    sys[System: y of t equals T of x of t] --> p1{Linear?}
    sys --> p2{Time Invariant?}
    sys --> p3{Causal?}
    sys --> p4{BIBO Stable?}
    p1 --> p1a[Yes: Superposition Holds]
    p1 --> p1b[No: Contains nonlinear ops or constants]
    p2 --> p2a[Yes: Shift Invariant]
    p2 --> p2b[No: Contains time varying coefficients]
    p3 --> p3a[Yes: Depends only on present and past]
    p3 --> p3b[No: Uses future values]
    p4 --> p4a[Yes: Bounded Input yields Bounded Output]
    p4 --> p4b[No: Impulse response not absolutely summable or integrable]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow of Time-Domain Operations

```mermaid
graph LR
    in[Input x of t] --> op1[Step 1: Time Shift by t0]
    op1 --> op2[Step 2: Time Scale by a]
    op2 --> op3[Step 3: Time Reverse]
    op3 --> op4[Step 4: Amplitude Scale by A]
    op4 --> out[Output y of t equals A times x of negative a times t minus t0]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Sketching Strategy:** Always draw four small axes under the main figure — one for each step. Label each with the resulting equation. Examiners award **partial marks for each correctly labeled intermediate step** even if the final sketch is imperfect.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a continuous-time signal and a discrete-time signal with one robotics example each.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **continuous-time signal** $x(t)$ is a function defined for every real value of the independent variable $t \in \mathbb{R}$. *Robotics example:* The continuous voltage output of a **potentiometer** attached to a robot arm's joint — it is defined for every instant of time.

A **discrete-time signal** $x[n]$ is a function defined only at discrete integer values of the index $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. *Robotics example:* A sequence of joint angles measured by a **digital rotary encoder** sampled every 10 ms by a microcontroller.

The mathematical relationships are $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$ (CT) and $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$ (DT).

---

### **Q2. State the conditions for a discrete-time signal to be (a) energy signal, (b) power signal. Give one example of each.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Energy Signal:** A DT signal is an energy signal if its total energy $E$ is finite and non-zero:

$$0 < E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n]\vert^2 < \infty$$

*Example:* $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$. The sum $\sum (0.5)^{2n} = \sum (0.25)^n$ is a convergent geometric series, so $E < \infty$.

**(b) Power Signal:** A DT signal is a power signal if its average power $P$ is finite and non-zero:

$$0 < P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n]\vert^2 < \infty$$

*Example:* $x[n] = A \cos(\Omega n + \phi)$ — the average power is $P = A^2/2$ (finite and non-zero).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) For the discrete-time signal** $x[n] = \{ \underline{2}, 1, 0, -1, -2 \}$, **determine with full derivation:**
**(i)** Whether $x[n]$ is an energy signal, a power signal, both, or neither.
**(ii)** Its even and odd components $x_e[n]$ and $x_o[n]$.
**(iii)** Verify the decomposition. (7 marks)

**(b) For the continuous-time system** $y(t) = t \cdot x(t) + 5$, **test whether it is:**
**(i)** Linear
**(ii)** Time-invariant
**(iii)** Causal
**(iv)** BIBO stable (state the bounded-input assumption explicitly). (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a)(i) — Energy and Power Classification:**

Energy of $x[n]$:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n]\vert^2 = 2^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 + (-1)^2 + (-2)^2 = 4+1+0+1+4 = 10$$

Power of $x[n]$ (assuming it repeats periodically with period 5):

$$P = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{n=0}^{4} \vert x[n]\vert^2 = \frac{10}{5} = 2$$

Since both $E$ and $P$ are finite and non-zero, the signal is **neither** a pure energy nor a pure power signal (in the non-periodic interpretation). If we treat the sequence as one period of a periodic signal, the power is $P=2$.

**Part (a)(ii) — Even/Odd Decomposition:**

Reverse the signal: $x[-n] = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, \underline{2}\}$.

Even part: $x_e[n] = \frac{1}{2}(x[n] + x[-n]) = \frac{1}{2}(\{2,1,0,-1,-2\} + \{-2,-1,0,1,2\}) = \frac{1}{2}\{0,0,0,0,0\} = \{0,0,\underline{0},0,0\}$.

Odd part: $x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2}(x[n] - x[-n]) = \frac{1}{2}\{4,2,0,-2,-4\} = \{2,1,\underline{0},-1,-2\}$.

**Part (a)(iii) — Verification:**

$x_e[n] + x_o[n] = \{2,1,0,-1,-2\} = x[n]$ ✓

**Part (b)(i) — Linearity:**

Test $T\{0\} = t \cdot 0 + 5 = 5 \neq 0$. Fails the homogeneity condition.

**Conclusion: System is NON-LINEAR.**

**Part (b)(ii) — Time Invariance:**

Let $x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$. Then $T\{x_1(t)\} = t \cdot x(t - t_0) + 5$. But $y_1(t) = y(t - t_0) = (t - t_0) x(t - t_0) + 5$.

These differ by the term $t_0 x(t-t_0)$.

**Conclusion: System is TIME-VARIANT.**

**Part (b)(iii) — Causality:**

Output $y(t)$ depends only on $x(t)$ (the present input) and not on any future value. **System is CAUSAL.**

**Part (b)(iv) — BIBO Stability:**

Let $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M < \infty$. Then:

$$\vert y(t) \vert = \vert t \cdot x(t) + 5 \vert \leq \vert t \vert M + 5$$

As $t \to \infty$, the bound grows without limit. Hence a bounded input can produce an unbounded output.

**Conclusion: System is NOT BIBO STABLE.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Sketch the continuous-time signal $x(t) = (t+1) [u(t+1) - u(t-2)]$, and from it obtain the signal $y(t) = x(2t - 3)$. Show every intermediate step. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the discrete-time signal $x[n] = \cos\left(0.4\pi n + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$, determine its fundamental period (if any) and compute its average power. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Sketching the Operations:**

Original signal: $x(t) = (t+1)$ for $-1 \leq t < 2$, zero elsewhere. This is a ramp of slope 1 starting at $x(-1)=0$ and ending at $x(2)=3$.

**Step 1 — Time shift right by $\frac{3}{2}$:**

$$x\left(t - \frac{3}{2}\right) = \left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right) \text{ for } \frac{1}{2} \leq t < \frac{7}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compress by factor 2:**

$$x\left(2t - \frac{3}{2}\right) = (2t - \tfrac{1}{2}) \text{ for } \frac{1}{4} \leq t < \frac{7}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Time-reverse:**

$$y(t) = x(-2t+3) = (-2t+4) \text{ for } \frac{1}{4} \leq t < \frac{7}{4} \text{ (reversed direction)}$$

After reversal, the ramp goes from a peak at $t=\frac{1}{4}$ (value $=\frac{7}{2}$) down to $t=\frac{7}{4}$ (value $=-\frac{1}{2}$).

> [!TIP]
> **Sketching Tip:** Always label the **boundaries** explicitly with dotted lines and the **functional form** on the relevant interval.

**Part (b) — Periodicity and Power:**

Check rationality of $\dfrac{\Omega}{2\pi} = \dfrac{0.4\pi}{2\pi} = \dfrac{0.4}{2} = \dfrac{1}{5}$.

This is rational in lowest terms $\dfrac{p}{q} = \dfrac{1}{5}$, with $p=1, q=5$. Hence the fundamental period is:

$$N = q = 5$$

**Power Computation:**

For a discrete-time sinusoid $x[n] = A \cos(\Omega n + \phi)$ with $A$ being the amplitude, the average power is:

$$P = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

In our case $A = 1$ (no amplitude scaling). So:

$$P = \frac{1^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

This result is **independent of the phase $\phi$** and the frequency $\Omega$, as long as the signal is periodic. **Final answers: $N=5$, $P=0.5$.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Periodicity of Discrete Sinusoids:** A common student error is to claim that $x[n] = \cos(0.3n)$ is periodic with period $T = 2\pi/0.3$. **This is wrong.** The continuous formula $T = 2\pi/\omega$ does **not** apply to discrete time. You must check whether $\Omega/(2\pi)$ is rational.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2 — Energy vs Power Confusion:** Students often compute $E$ for an infinite-duration signal and report it without first checking convergence. For $x[n] = u[n]$, $E = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 1 = \infty$, so the signal is **not** an energy signal. Always check convergence explicitly.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3 — Linearity Test Shortcut:** A student writes "since $y = 2x+3$ contains a constant, it is non-linear" but loses marks by **not showing the homogeneity test** $T\{0\} = 3 \neq 0$. Always show the explicit math.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 4 — Time-Invariant Test Notation:** Confuse $y(t-t_0)$ with $y$ evaluated at $t = t - t_0$. A common error: writing $T\{x(t-t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t-t_0) + 5$ but comparing it to $y(t) = t \cdot x(t) + 5$ evaluated at $t - t_0$, which gives $(t-t_0)x(t-t_0) + 5$. These are **not** equal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 5 — Skipping Intermediate Sketches:** For a 7-mark question involving signal transformations, drawing **only the final sketch** without intermediate steps typically yields 3–4 marks maximum. Always include 2–3 intermediate sketches.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

- [x] A signal is a function of an independent variable (usually time).
- [x] CT signals are defined for $t \in \mathbb{R}$; DT signals are defined for $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- [x] The **five standard test signals**: unit impulse $\delta$, unit step $u$, unit ramp $r$, exponential $e^{at}$ or $a^n$, sinusoid.
- [x] **Time shift $t_0 > 0$** → right shift; **$t_0 < 0$** → left shift.
- [x] **Time scale $|a| > 1$** → compress; **$|a| < 1$** → expand.
- [x] **Energy signal** ↔ finite $E$, zero $P$. **Power signal** ↔ finite $P$, infinite $E$.
- [x] Discrete sinusoid periodic only if $\Omega/(2\pi)$ is rational; fundamental period $N = q$ in lowest terms $\dfrac{p}{q}$.
- [x] **Linearity** test: $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a y_1 + b y_2$. Quick test: $T\{0\} = 0$ is **necessary** (not sufficient).
- [x] **Time invariance** test: output shift = system response to input shift.
- [x] **Causality**: output depends only on present/past inputs.
- [x] **BIBO stability**: bounded input ⇒ bounded output. For LTI systems, equivalent to absolute integrability/summability of impulse response.
- [x] **Even part** $x_e = \frac{1}{2}[x(t) + x(-t)]$; **Odd part** $x_o = \frac{1}{2}[x(t) - x(-t)]$.
- [x] In robotics: sensor signals are CT, processor signals are DT, ADC and DAC are the bridges.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes.** You are now ready to move to **Module 2: Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Systems** where you will use every concept mastered here — linearity, time-invariance, impulse response, and stability — as foundational tools. Master this module thoroughly before proceeding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Classifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Signal Classifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Signal?

In the rigorous context of **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** course *OERAT831 – Introduction to Signal Processing for Robotics*, a **signal** is formally defined as any physical quantity that varies with one or more independent variables (most commonly time) and contains information about the behavior or nature of a phenomenon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **signal** $x(t)$ is a mathematical function that conveys information, where the independent variable (typically time $t$, or in spatial applications, position $s$) maps a domain of values to a range of magnitudes.
> $$x: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \quad \text{or} \quad x: \mathbb{Z} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

In **robotics**, signals are the fundamental medium of perception and control. Examples include:
- A **voltage** from an infrared (IR) proximity sensor on an autonomous floor-cleaning robot.
- A **current** reading from a brushless DC (BLDC) motor driving a robotic arm joint.
- A **digital pulse** from an encoder wheel measuring wheel rotation of a mobile robot.
- An **image intensity** matrix captured by a camera mounted on a humanoid robot.

## 1.2 Why Do We Classify Signals?

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):**
> Classification is the *first cognitive step* before any analysis (Fourier, Laplace, Z-transform) or system design (filter, controller). Each class of signal has a **unique mathematical toolset** that applies to it. Misclassifying a signal leads to incorrect transform selection and erroneous robotic responses.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body of a Robot

Imagine a robot as a living organism. Signals are its **nervous system**.

| Robot Organ | Signal Type | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Camera (eyes) | 2-D Image Matrix $I(m,n)$ | Continuous, multidimensional visual stream |
| Microphone (ears) | Audio $x(t)$ | Continuous-time, energy-bounded, aperiodic |
| Wheel Encoder (proprioception) | Pulse train $x[n]$ | Discrete-time, periodic, digital |
| Temperature Sensor (skin) | DC voltage $V$ | Constant (DC) signal |
| Battery Monitor | Step change $u(t)$ | Discontinuous, deterministic, one-sided |

Just as a doctor must know whether a heartbeat signal is **periodic** (normal) or **aperiodic** (arrhythmia) to diagnose a patient, a robotics engineer must classify a sensor signal before processing it.

## 1.4 The Five Fundamental Independent Variables in Robotics

1. **Time ($t$)** – most common (sensor readings, motor commands).
2. **Space ($s$)** – image pixels, LiDAR point clouds.
3. **Frequency ($f$ or $\omega$)** – spectral domain (post-Fourier analysis).
4. **Wavelength ($\lambda$)** – optical and ultrasonic sensors.
5. **Discrete index ($n$ or $k$)** – sampled/digital signals.

## 1.5 The Master Classification Tree

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Classification Scheme:**
> Signals are broadly classified along **seven orthogonal axes**:
> 1. **Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time** (domain nature)
> 2. **Analog vs Digital** (amplitude nature)
> 3. **Periodic vs Aperiodic** (repetition)
> 4. **Energy vs Power** (norm finiteness)
> 5. **Deterministic vs Random** (predictability)
> 6. **Even vs Odd** (symmetry)
> 7. **Causal vs Non-causal vs Anti-causal** (time support)

Each axis is independent; a single signal can be classified along **all seven** simultaneously (e.g., *a continuous-time, periodic, power, deterministic, even, causal signal* is a cosine wave $x(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Axis 1 — Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time Signals

### 2.1.1 Continuous-Time (CT) Signal $x(t)$
- Independent variable $t \in \mathbb{R}$ is **continuous**.
- Defined for **every** instant in an interval.
- Naturally produced by analog sensors (thermistor, microphone, gyroscope analog output).

### 2.1.2 Discrete-Time (DT) Signal $x[n]$
- Independent variable $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ takes only **discrete** values.
- Usually obtained by **sampling** a CT signal at uniform intervals $T_s$.
- Convention: $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ where $T_s$ is the sampling period.
- **Nyquist Criterion (KTU High-Yield):** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} \quad ; \quad f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotics Insight:** A LiDAR sensor on a mobile robot uses discrete-time sampling — every $T_s = 50\,\mu s$ it produces one range measurement. A PID controller in a robotic arm operates in discrete time, computing $u[n]$ from $e[n]$ at fixed clock cycles.

---

## 2.2 Axis 2 — Analog vs Digital Signals

| Property | Analog Signal | Digital Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Amplitude** | Continuous real-valued | Quantized to finite levels |
| **Time** | Continuous | Usually discrete |
| **Noise immunity** | Poor | Excellent (regeneration) |
| **Storage** | Difficult (tape/vinyl) | Easy (binary memory) |
| **Robotic use** | Sensor front-end | Microprocessor, FPGA buses |

A signal can be **discrete-time but not digital** (e.g., sampled but amplitude-unquantized). A **digital signal** is *both* discrete-time *and* quantized.

---

## 2.3 Axis 3 — Periodic vs Aperiodic Signals

### 2.3.1 Continuous-Time Periodic Signal
A signal $x(t)$ is **periodic** with fundamental period $T_0 > 0$ if:
$$x(t + T_0) = x(t) \quad \forall\, t \in \mathbb{R}$$
The **smallest positive** $T_0$ is the *fundamental period*. The fundamental frequency:
$$f_0 = \frac{1}{T_0} \quad ; \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_0}$$

### 2.3.2 Discrete-Time Periodic Signal
$$x[n + N] = x[n] \quad \forall\, n \in \mathbb{Z}$$
where $N$ is a positive integer period. **Important:** A DT signal periodic with period $N$ is *also* periodic with period $kN$ for any integer $k$, but the **fundamental period** is the smallest such $N$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Trap:** A DT sinusoid $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ is periodic **only if** $\omega_0 / 2\pi$ is a **rational number** $p/q$ (in lowest terms). Then the period is $N = q$.

### 2.3.3 Aperiodic Signals
Signals that do not repeat. Includes transient responses, one-shot impulses, exponentially decaying signals, and most real-world sensor noise.

**Sum of two periodic signals** with periods $T_1$ and $T_2$ is periodic **iff** the ratio $T_1/T_2$ is rational.

---

## 2.4 Axis 4 — Energy vs Power Signals

This classification is the **most important** for KTU exam questions and robotics applications.

### 2.4.1 Continuous-Time Definitions
**Total Energy:**
$$E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt \quad \text{[Joules, J]}$$

**Average Power:**
$$P_x = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt \quad \text{[Watts, W]}$$

**Relationship (Fundamental Identity):**
$$P_x = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{E_x}{2T}$$

### 2.4.2 Discrete-Time Definitions
$$E_x = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

$$P_x = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{+N} \vert x[n] \vert^2 \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

### 2.4.3 Classification Rules
| Case | Condition | Class |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $0 < E_x < \infty$ (and thus $P_x = 0$) | **Energy signal** |
| 2 | $0 < P_x < \infty$ (and thus $E_x = \infty$) | **Power signal** |
| 3 | $E_x = \infty$ and $P_x = \infty$ | **Neither** (rare) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A signal cannot be both an energy signal and a power signal (except the trivial zero signal).**

**Robotic Examples:**
- A **single pulse** from a robot's whisker sensor hitting an obstacle → **energy signal** (finite total energy, zero average power).
- A **sinusoidal motor command** $u(t) = 5\sin(100\pi t)$ → **power signal** (infinite energy, finite average power $= 12.5$ W if $1\,\Omega$ load).

---

## 2.5 Axis 5 — Deterministic vs Random Signals

| Aspect | Deterministic | Random (Stochastic) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Completely specified by an explicit mathematical function | Modeled via probability distributions / statistics |
| **Past/Future** | Known exactly | Known only in a statistical sense |
| **Example** | Robot arm position $q(t) = q_0 + 0.5t^2$ | IMU sensor noise $n(t)$ in a walking humanoid |

A **random signal** is described by ensemble averages (mean, autocorrelation, PSD) rather than a closed-form expression.

---

## 2.6 Axis 6 — Even vs Odd Signals

**Even Signal:**
$$x(t) = x(-t) \quad \text{(symmetric about vertical axis)}$$

**Odd Signal:**
$$x(t) = -x(-t) \quad \text{(anti-symmetric)}$$

**Key Theorem (KTU High-Yield):**
> Every signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely decomposed into an even part $x_e(t)$ and an odd part $x_o(t)$:
> $$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$

where
$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \quad ; \quad x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotic Insight:** Even signals are essential in designing **linear-phase FIR filters** for image processing in computer vision pipelines of autonomous robots.

---

## 2.7 Axis 7 — Causal vs Non-Causal vs Anti-Causal Signals

### 2.7.1 Causal Signal
A signal is **causal** if it is zero for all negative time:
$$x(t) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad t < 0$$
$$x[n] = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad n < 0$$

This is the *physical realizability* condition — a robot's output at time $t$ **cannot depend on the future**. **All real-time robotic systems must be causal.**

### 2.7.2 Non-Causal Signal
Non-zero for some $t < 0$. Used in **offline processing** (e.g., post-mission log analysis of a robot's trajectory).

### 2.7.3 Anti-Causal Signal
Non-zero for $t > 0$, zero for $t < 0$. Used in **two-sided Z-transforms** and digital filter design (e.g., moving-average smoothing of pre-recorded IMU data).

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEEP THIS TABLE FOR QUICK REVISION — Pipes escaped as `\vert` for Markdown safety.**

| $\#$ | Quantity / Property | Continuous-Time Formula | Discrete-Time Formula | Units / Class |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Signal notation | $x(t)$ | $x[n]$ | — |
| 2 | Sampling period | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | — | seconds (s) |
| 3 | Nyquist rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | — | Hz |
| 4 | Total energy | $E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $E_x = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Joules (J) |
| 5 | Average power | $P_x = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $P_x = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Watts (W) |
| 6 | Energy signal | $0 < E_x < \infty \Rightarrow P_x = 0$ | same | finite total energy |
| 7 | Power signal | $0 < P_x < \infty \Rightarrow E_x = \infty$ | same | finite avg. power |
| 8 | Periodic condition | $x(t+T_0)=x(t)$ | $x[n+N]=x[n]$ | $T_0$ smallest, $N$ smallest integer |
| 9 | Fundamental frequency | $f_0 = 1/T_0$ | $\omega_0 = 2\pi k/N$ | Hz or rad |
| 10 | Even decomposition | $x_e(t) = (x(t)+x(-t))/2$ | $x_e[n] = (x[n]+x[-n])/2$ | symmetric |
| 11 | Odd decomposition | $x_o(t) = (x(t)-x(-t))/2$ | $x_o[n] = (x[n]-x[-n])/2$ | antisymmetric |
| 12 | Causal condition | $x(t)=0$ for $t<0$ | $x[n]=0$ for $n<0$ | physical realizability |
| 13 | RMS value | $x_{rms} = \sqrt{P_x}$ | $x_{rms} = \sqrt{P_x}$ | same units as $x$ |

---

## 2.9 Standard Test Signals in Robotics (KTU High-Yield)

These appear in nearly every KTU exam and form the basis of every system analysis.

| Signal | Formula | CT/DT | Period | Energy | Power | Robotics Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unit step | $u(t) = 1$ for $t \geq 0$, else $0$ | CT | Aperiodic | $\infty$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}$ | Motor ON command |
| Unit impulse | $\delta(t)$ | CT | Aperiodic | **1** | $\infty$ | Impact / collision |
| Unit ramp | $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$ | CT | Aperiodic | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Constant-acceleration trajectory |
| Exponential | $e^{-at} u(t)$ | CT | Aperiodic | $\tfrac{1}{2a}$ | $0$ | Decaying sensor response |
| Sinusoid | $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ | CT | $T_0 = 2\pi/\omega_0$ | $\infty$ | $A^2/2$ | AC motor drive |
| Rectangular pulse | $p_T(t)$ of width $T$ | CT | Aperiodic | $T$ | $0$ | Ultrasonic burst |
| Sinusoid DT | $A\cos(\omega_0 n)$ | DT | $N = 2\pi/\omega_0$ if rational | $\infty$ | $A^2/2$ | Encoder reference |
| Signum | $\text{sgn}(t)$ | CT | Aperiodic | $\infty$ | $1$ | Direction polarity |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Power of a Sinusoidal Signal (High-Yield KTU Problem)

**Problem:** Find the total energy $E_x$ and average power $P_x$ of $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$, where $A, \omega_0, \phi$ are real constants.

**Step 1 — Set up the energy integral.**
$$E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Expand the magnitude squared.**
$$E_x = A^2 \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Use the trigonometric half-angle identity $\cos^2\theta = (1 + \cos 2\theta)/2$.**
$$E_x = A^2 \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Split the integral.**
$$E_x = \frac{A^2}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} dt \; + \; \frac{A^2}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate each integral.**
- The first integral diverges: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} dt = \infty$.
- The second integral averages to zero over symmetric limits.

$$E_x = \frac{A^2}{2} \cdot \infty + 0 = \infty$$

**Conclusion:** $E_x = \infty$ → the signal is **not an energy signal**.

**Step 6 — Set up the average power integral over a finite window $[-T, T]$.**
$$P_x = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} A^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) \, dt$$

**Step 7 — Apply the half-angle identity again.**
$$P_x = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2} \, dt$$

$$P_x = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T} \left[ \int_{-T}^{T} dt + \int_{-T}^{T} \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \, dt \right]$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the cosine integral over the symmetric window.**
$$\int_{-T}^{T} \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \, dt = \left[ \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2\omega_0} \right]_{-T}^{T} = \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 T + 2\phi) + \sin(2\omega_0 T - 2\phi)}{2\omega_0}$$

**Step 9 — Bound the second term.** Since $\vert \sin(\cdot) \vert \leq 1$, we have:
$$\left\vert \int_{-T}^{T} \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \, dt \right\vert \leq \frac{2}{2\omega_0} = \frac{1}{\omega_0}$$

This is a **bounded** quantity independent of $T$.

**Step 10 — Take the limit $T \to \infty$.**
$$P_x = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T} \left[ 2T + \underbrace{\text{bounded}}_{\to 0 \text{ after division by } T} \right] = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T} \cdot 2T = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{E_x = \infty, \quad P_x = \frac{A^2}{2}}$$

Hence, $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ is a **power signal** with $P_x = A^2/2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotic Interpretation:** A BLDC motor commanded by $u(t) = 12\cos(50\pi t)$ V delivers an average power of $144/2 = 72$ W into a $1\,\Omega$ load — critical for sizing the battery of a mobile robot.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy of a Decaying Exponential Pulse

**Problem:** Find the energy of $x(t) = A e^{-at} u(t)$ for $a > 0$.

**Step 1 — Set up the energy integral. Since $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$:**
$$E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert A e^{-at} u(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} A^2 e^{-2at} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Pull out the constant.**
$$E_x = A^2 \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2at} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate using $\int e^{-\alpha t} dt = -e^{-\alpha t}/\alpha$.**
$$E_x = A^2 \left[ \frac{-e^{-2at}}{2a} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the limits. As $t \to \infty$, $e^{-2at} \to 0$. At $t = 0$, $e^{0} = 1$:**
$$E_x = A^2 \left( 0 - \frac{-1}{2a} \right) = \frac{A^2}{2a}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the power.**
$$P_x = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{E_x}{2T} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4aT} = 0$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{E_x = \frac{A^2}{2a}, \quad P_x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Energy signal}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotic Insight:** The exponential decay models the response of an RC low-pass filter on a robot's motor feedback line. With $A = 5$ V and $a = 100\,\text{s}^{-1}$, $E_x = 25/200 = 0.125$ J — a tiny pulse representing a single encoder click.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Even-Odd Decomposition of a Rectangular Pulse

**Problem:** Decompose $x(t) = p(t)$, a rectangular pulse of width $2\tau$ centered at the origin, into its even and odd parts.

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} A, & \vert t \vert < \tau \\ 0, & \vert t \vert > \tau \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $x(-t)$.** By symmetry of the rectangle:
$$x(-t) = \begin{cases} A, & \vert -t \vert < \tau \\ 0, & \vert -t \vert > \tau \end{cases} = x(t)$$

**Step 2 — Therefore:**
$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} = \frac{x(t) + x(t)}{2} = x(t)$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = \frac{x(t) - x(t)}{2} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** A centered rectangular pulse is **purely even** — its odd part is identically zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Lesson:** A rectangular pulse *shifted off-center* (e.g., $p(t-t_0)$ with $t_0 \neq 0$) is **neither even nor odd** and possesses *both* non-zero even and odd components.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Periodic and Energy Properties of Common Signals

| Signal $x(t)$ | Periodic? | Period | Energy $E_x$ | Power $P_x$ | Class |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| $A$ (constant DC) | Yes | Any $T$ | $\infty$ | $A^2$ | Power |
| $A\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | Yes | $2\pi/\omega_0$ | $\infty$ | $A^2/2$ | Power |
| $A e^{-at} u(t)$, $a>0$ | No | — | $A^2/(2a)$ | $0$ | Energy |
| $A\,\delta(t)$ | No | — | $A^2$ | $\infty$ | Neither |
| Rect. pulse $A$ for $0 \leq t \leq \tau$ | No | — | $A^2 \tau$ | $0$ | Energy |
| $\sin(\omega_0 t) + \cos(2\omega_0 t)$ | Yes (rational) | $2\pi/\omega_0$ | $\infty$ | $1/2 + 1/2 = 1$ | Power |
| $u(t)$ (unit step) | No | — | $\infty$ | $1/2$ | Power |
| Random noise $n(t)$ | No | — | $\infty$ | finite $P$ | Power |
