- ⚠️ Skipping the verification of $R_{xx}(0) = E_x$ in derivations — examiners reward this final check with 1 mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Domain Representation - Continuous Time Fourier Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Frequency Domain Representation - Continuous Time Fourier Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Continuous-Time Fourier Series (CTFS)** is a fundamental frequency-domain representation technique used to decompose any **periodic continuous-time signal** $x(t)$ into a weighted sum of complex exponentials (or equivalently, sines and cosines) that are harmonically related to the fundamental frequency of the signal.

Mathematically, if $x(t)$ is a periodic signal with fundamental period $T_0$ (and fundamental angular frequency $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_0}$), then the **Complex Exponential Fourier Series** is defined by the synthesis equation:

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, e^{j n \omega_0 t}$$

where the **Fourier coefficients** $C_n$ are computed via the analysis equation:

$$C_n = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \, e^{-j n \omega_0 t} \, dt$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The CTFS exists *only* for **periodic** signals. For aperiodic signals, we use the **Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT)**. This distinction is a frequent KTU exam question and carries **2-3 marks** of easy marks if answered correctly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **robot's audio system** trying to recognize a spoken command. The microphone receives a complex sound wave — a jumbled mix of many pure tones travelling together. How does the robot "see" inside this sound?

> [!NOTE]
> **The Prism Analogy for Robotics:** Just as a **white light prism** splits a beam of white light into its constituent rainbow colors (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet), the **Fourier Series acts as a mathematical prism** for periodic signals. It splits a complicated time-domain signal $x(t)$ into its "spectral colors" — the individual frequency components $C_n$. 
>
> - The **time-domain signal** $x(t)$ is the mixed white light.
> - The **Fourier coefficients** $C_n$ tell you "how much" of each frequency color exists.
> - The **frequency spectrum** $\vert C_n \vert$ vs $n\omega_0$ is the rainbow you see after splitting.

For a **robotic joint control system**, a periodic square-wave control signal can be broken into a fundamental sine wave plus higher-frequency "harmonics" — these harmonics cause **vibration** and **wear** in mechanical joints, which is why engineers must analyze them in the frequency domain.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Constants

- **Fundamental Period ($T_0$):** The smallest positive time interval after which the signal repeats. **Unit: seconds (s)**
- **Fundamental Angular Frequency ($\omega_0$):** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_0} = 2\pi f_0$ . **Unit: radians/second (rad/s)**
- **Fundamental Frequency ($f_0$):** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{T_0}$ . **Unit: Hertz (Hz)**
- **$n^{th}$ Harmonic Frequency:** $n\omega_0$ or $nf_0$
- **Spectral Lines:** Discrete frequency components at $n\omega_0$
- **DC Component:** The coefficient $C_0$, which represents the average value of the signal over one period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of $C_n$ in Robotics:** Each $C_n$ is a **complex number**. Its **magnitude** $\vert C_n \vert$ represents the *strength* (amplitude) of the $n^{th}$ harmonic, and its **angle** $\angle C_n$ represents the *phase shift* of that harmonic relative to the origin.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Equivalent Forms of CTFS

The CTFS can be expressed in three equivalent forms. A high-yield KTU question is: *"Derive the relationship between the trigonometric and exponential Fourier series coefficients."*

### (A) Trigonometric Fourier Series

$$x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t) \right]$$

with coefficients computed as:

$$a_0 = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \, dt$$

$$a_n = \frac{2}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \cos(n\omega_0 t) \, dt$$

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \sin(n\omega_0 t) \, dt$$

### (B) Compact Trigonometric (Harmonic) Form

$$x(t) = C_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} C_n \cos(n\omega_0 t + \theta_n)$$

where:

$$C_0 = a_0 \quad,\quad C_n = \sqrt{a_n^2 + b_n^2} \quad,\quad \theta_n = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-b_n}{a_n}\right)$$

### (C) Complex Exponential Fourier Series (Most General)

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} D_n \, e^{j n \omega_0 t} \quad,\quad D_n = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \, e^{-j n \omega_0 t} \, dt$$

## 2.2 The Critical "Why" — Dirichlet Conditions

A periodic signal $x(t)$ has a Fourier series representation **if and only if** the following **Dirichlet conditions** are satisfied over any single period $T_0$:

1. **Absolute Integrability:** $\displaystyle\int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ (The signal must be integrable, i.e., not have infinite area)
2. **Finite Number of Discontinuities:** $x(t)$ has a finite number of **maxima, minima, and discontinuities** within one period.
3. **Finite Energy per Period:** $\displaystyle\int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt < \infty$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Virtually all practical signals encountered in robotics (joint angles, motor currents, sensor outputs) satisfy these conditions. The CTFS thus applies to nearly every periodic real-world signal a roboticist will encounter.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| Fundamental Angular Frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_0}$ | Core parameter for any periodic signal |
| Synthesis (Exponential) | $x(t) = \sum_{n} D_n e^{j n \omega_0 t}$ | Reconstructs time signal from spectrum |
| Analysis (Exponential) | $D_n = \dfrac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) e^{-j n \omega_0 t} dt$ | Extracts spectral component |
| Trigonometric Coeff. $a_0$ | $a_0 = \dfrac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) dt$ | DC offset / average value |
| Trigonometric Coeff. $a_n$ | $a_n = \dfrac{2}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \cos(n\omega_0 t) dt$ | Cosine amplitude |
| Trigonometric Coeff. $b_n$ | $b_n = \dfrac{2}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \sin(n\omega_0 t) dt$ | Sine amplitude |
| Magnitude Spectrum | $\vert D_n \vert = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{a_n^2 + b_n^2}$ | Strength of $n^{th}$ harmonic |
| Phase Spectrum | $\angle D_n = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{-b_n}{a_n}\right)$ | Phase shift of $n^{th}$ harmonic |
| Trigonometric to Exponential | $D_0 = a_0$, $D_n = \dfrac{a_n - j b_n}{2}$ for $n \geq 1$ | Standard KTU conversion question |
| Exponential to Trigonometric | $a_n = D_n + D_{-n}$, $b_n = j(D_n - D_{-n})$ | Reverse conversion |
| Parseval's Power Theorem | $\dfrac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert D_n \vert^2$ | Power calculation in frequency domain |
| Symmetry: $x(t)$ even | $b_n = 0$ (sines vanish) | Real cosine spectrum |
| Symmetry: $x(t)$ odd | $a_n = 0$ (cosines vanish) | Real sine spectrum |
| Symmetry: $x(t)$ real | $D_{-n} = D_n^{*}$ (conjugate symmetry) | Spectrum Hermitian |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Trigonometric to Complex Exponential Form

**Starting Point:** Trigonometric Fourier Series

$$x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t) \right]$$

**Step 1:** Apply Euler's identities.

$$\cos(n\omega_0 t) = \frac{e^{j n \omega_0 t} + e^{-j n \omega_0 t}}{2}$$

$$\sin(n\omega_0 t) = \frac{e^{j n \omega_0 t} - e^{-j n \omega_0 t}}{2j}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the trigonometric form.

$$x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cdot \frac{e^{j n \omega_0 t} + e^{-j n \omega_0 t}}{2} + b_n \cdot \frac{e^{j n \omega_0 t} - e^{-j n \omega_0 t}}{2j} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Group coefficients of $e^{j n \omega_0 t}$ and $e^{-j n \omega_0 t}$. Use $\dfrac{1}{j} = -j$.

$$x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ \left(\frac{a_n - j b_n}{2}\right) e^{j n \omega_0 t} + \left(\frac{a_n + j b_n}{2}\right) e^{-j n \omega_0 t} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Define $D_n$ for $n \geq 1$ and recognize that the second term can be re-indexed.

$$D_n = \frac{a_n - j b_n}{2} \quad (n \geq 1)$$

$$D_{-n} = \frac{a_n + j b_n}{2} = D_n^{*}$$

**Step 5:** Combine into the unified exponential form.

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} D_n \, e^{j n \omega_0 t} \quad \text{where} \quad D_0 = a_0$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Parseval's Power Theorem (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Start with the average power definition in time domain.

$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \cdot x^{*}(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the Fourier series expansion $x(t) = \sum_{k} D_k e^{j k \omega_0 t}$ and its conjugate.

$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} \left( \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} D_k e^{j k \omega_0 t} \right) \left( \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} D_m^{*} e^{-j m \omega_0 t} \right) dt$$

**Step 3:** Interchange the order of integration and summation.

$$P = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} D_k D_m^{*} \cdot \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} e^{j (k-m) \omega_0 t} \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Apply the orthogonality property of complex exponentials.

$$\frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} e^{j (k-m) \omega_0 t} \, dt = \begin{cases} 1, & k = m \\ 0, & k \neq m \end{cases}$$

**Step 5:** This eliminates one summation ($m = k$), giving the final result.

$$\boxed{P = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert D_n \vert^2}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: CTFS of a Rectangular Pulse Train

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Find the complex exponential Fourier series of the periodic rectangular pulse train with period $T_0 = 4$ seconds, amplitude $A = 2$ in the interval $-1 < t < 1$, and zero elsewhere (in one period).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters. $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_0} = \dfrac{2\pi}{4} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ rad/s.

**Step 2:** Apply the analysis equation over the interval $-1 < t < 1$ (the support of the pulse).

$$D_n = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{-1}^{1} 2 \cdot e^{-j n \omega_0 t} \, dt = \frac{2}{4} \int_{-1}^{1} e^{-j n \pi t / 2} \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Compute the integral.

$$D_n = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{e^{-j n \pi t / 2}}{-j n \pi / 2} \right]_{-1}^{1} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{-j n \pi} \left[ e^{-j n \pi / 2} - e^{j n \pi / 2} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Apply Euler's identity. Recall that $e^{-j\theta} - e^{j\theta} = -2j \sin(\theta)$.

$$D_n = \frac{1}{-j n \pi} \left[ -2j \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right) \right] = \frac{2}{n\pi} \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Special case $n = 0$. By L'Hopital's rule or direct integration:

$$D_0 = \frac{1}{4} \int_{-1}^{1} 2 \, dt = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 4 = 1$$

**Final Compact Result:**

$$D_n = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ \dfrac{2}{n\pi} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi}{2}\right), & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked for "amplitude spectrum," always plot $\vert D_n \vert$ vs $n\omega_0$. The sample value $D_n = 0$ for even $n \neq 0$ because $\sin(n\pi/2) = 0$ for $n = 2, 4, 6, \ldots$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CTFS Analysis-Synthesis Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete signal processing pipeline** for extracting and reconstructing periodic signals in a robotic system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Periodic Continuous Signal x of t] --> B[Acquire Period T0]
    B --> C[Compute Fundamental Frequency omega0 = 2pi slash T0]
    C --> D[Apply Analysis Equation over one period]
    D --> E[Compute Fourier Coefficient D_n = 1 over T0 times integral]
    E --> F[Store Magnitude Spectrum mod D_n]
    E --> G[Store Phase Spectrum angle D_n]
    F --> H[Frequency Domain Representation]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Engineering Decision}
    I --> J[Filter Unwanted Harmonics]
    I --> K[Apply Parsevals Power Theorem]
    I --> L[Reconstruct x of t via Synthesis Equation]
    J --> L
    L --> M[Robotic Control Output]
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: Symmetry-Based Coefficient Shortcuts

This diagram helps students decide which Fourier coefficients to compute for a given signal — a frequent KTU trick question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Inspect x of t for Symmetry] --> Q1{Is x of t even x of minus t equals x of t}
    Q1 -- Yes --> E1[b_n equals 0 only compute a_n]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is x of t odd x of minus t equals minus x of t}
    Q2 -- Yes --> E2[a_n equals 0 only compute b_n]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is x of t half wave symmetric x of t plus T0 slash 2 equals minus x of t}
    Q3 -- Yes --> E3[Only odd harmonics exist a_2k and b_2k equal 0]
    Q3 -- No --> E4[Compute all coefficients a_n and b_n]
    E1 --> End[Solve Problem]
    E2 --> End
    E3 --> End
    E4 --> End
```

## 4.3 CTFS Spectrum Visualization (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[DC Component D0] --> B1[1st Harmonic at omega0]
    B1 --> C1[2nd Harmonic at 2 omega0]
    C1 --> D1[3rd Harmonic at 3 omega0]
    D1 --> E1[4th Harmonic at 4 omega0]
    E1 --> F1[Higher Harmonics decaying in amplitude]
    A -.->|Magnitude mod D0| G1[Spectrum Line]
    B1 -.->|Magnitude mod D1| H1[Spectrum Line]
    C1 -.->|Magnitude mod D2| I1[Spectrum Line]
    D1 -.->|Magnitude mod D3| J1[Spectrum Line]
    E1 -.->|Magnitude mod D4| K1[Spectrum Line]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Robotics:** The discrete "spectral lines" in the magnitude spectrum show the robot's control system *exactly* which vibration frequencies are present. A robotic arm exhibiting resonance at the **3rd harmonic** of its drive frequency can be specifically damped at that single frequency — a major application of CTFS analysis in mechanical engineering.

## 4.4 Tabular Summary: CTFS Forms Side-by-Side

| **Property** | **Trigonometric Form** | **Exponential Form** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Real Coefficients | $N+2$ (for $N$ harmonics) | $2N+1$ (complex) |
| Handles Negative Frequencies | No | Yes |
| Computational Efficiency | Slower | Faster (FFT compatible) |
| Best For | Engineering intuition | Mathematical rigor |
| Symmetry Insight | Direct ($a_n, b_n$ visible) | Conjugate property |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Define Continuous-Time Fourier Series (CTFS). State the Dirichlet conditions for the existence of Fourier series representation of a periodic signal.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Continuous-Time Fourier Series (CTFS)** is a frequency-domain representation that expresses a periodic continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with period $T_0$ as an infinite sum of harmonically related complex exponentials (or sines and cosines) at frequencies $n\omega_0$, where $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T_0$. **(1 mark)**

**Dirichlet Conditions:** A periodic signal $x(t)$ possesses a Fourier series representation if: **(2 marks)**

1. $\displaystyle\int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ (absolute integrability over one period)
2. $x(t)$ has a finite number of discontinuities in any finite interval
3. $x(t)$ has a finite number of maxima and minima in any finite interval

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Derive the relationship between the complex exponential Fourier coefficient $D_n$ and the trigonometric Fourier coefficients $a_n$ and $b_n$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For a real periodic signal $x(t)$ with trigonometric Fourier series $x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}[a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t)]$ and complex exponential form $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} D_n e^{jn\omega_0 t}$, the relationship is: **(1 mark)**

$$D_n = \frac{a_n - jb_n}{2} \quad \text{for } n \geq 1 \qquad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$D_{-n} = \frac{a_n + jb_n}{2} = D_n^{*} \quad \text{for } n \geq 1 \qquad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Also, $D_0 = a_0$ (the DC component).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Find the trigonometric Fourier series for the half-wave rectified sine wave defined as:
$$x(t) = \begin{cases} \sin(\pi t), & 0 < t < 1 \\ 0, & 1 < t < 2 \end{cases}, \quad \text{with period } T_0 = 2$$

Compute the coefficients $a_0$, $a_n$, and $b_n$ up to $n = 3$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the result from part (a), determine the complex exponential Fourier series coefficients $D_n$ for $n = -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3$. Sketch the magnitude spectrum $\vert D_n \vert$ versus $n\omega_0$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Given $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T_0 = 2\pi/2 = \pi$ rad/s. The function is non-zero only on $(0, 1)$.

**Computing $a_0$:**

$$a_0 = \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{1} \sin(\pi t) \, dt = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{-\cos(\pi t)}{\pi} \right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi}[-\cos(\pi) + \cos(0)] = \frac{1}{\pi}$$

**Computing $a_n$:**

$$a_n = \frac{2}{2} \int_{0}^{1} \sin(\pi t) \cos(n\pi t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{1} \sin(\pi t)\cos(n\pi t)\, dt$$

Using product-to-sum: $\sin A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)]$

$$a_n = \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{1} \left[ \sin((1+n)\pi t) + \sin((1-n)\pi t) \right] dt$$

**Case $n = 1$:**

$$a_1 = \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{1} \sin(2\pi t) \, dt = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{-\cos(2\pi t)}{2\pi} \right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0 = 0$$

**Case $n \neq 1$:**

$$a_n = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{-\cos((1+n)\pi t)}{(1+n)\pi} - \frac{\cos((1-n)\pi t)}{(1-n)\pi} \right]_0^1$$

$$a_n = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{1 - \cos((1+n)\pi)}{1+n} + \frac{1 - \cos((1-n)\pi)}{1-n} \right]$$

Since $\cos((1\pm n)\pi) = (-1)^{n+1}$:

$$a_n = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{1 - (-1)^{n+1}}{1+n} + \frac{1 - (-1)^{n+1}}{1-n} \right] = \frac{1 + (-1)^n}{\pi(1-n^2)}$$

Thus: $a_2 = \dfrac{1 + 1}{\pi(1-4)} = -\dfrac{2}{3\pi}$, $a_3 = \dfrac{1-1}{\pi(1-9)} = 0$ **(2 marks for derivation)**

**Computing $b_n$:**

$$b_n = \int_0^1 \sin(\pi t)\sin(n\pi t)\, dt = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^1 [\cos((1-n)\pi t) - \cos((1+n)\pi t)]\, dt$$

For $n = 1$: $b_1 = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_0^1 [1 - \cos(2\pi t)]\, dt = \dfrac{1}{2}$

For $n \neq 1$: $b_n = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[\dfrac{\sin((1-n)\pi)}{(1-n)\pi} - \dfrac{\sin((1+n)\pi)}{(1+n)\pi}\right] = 0$ (since $\sin(k\pi) = 0$ for integer $k$)

So $b_2 = 0$, $b_3 = 0$ **(2 marks)**

**Trigonometric Series:**

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{\pi} - \frac{2}{3\pi}\cos(2\pi t) + \frac{1}{2}\sin(\pi t) + \text{(higher terms)}\; \text{...(1 mark for final form)}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Using $D_0 = a_0 = 1/\pi$, $D_n = (a_n - jb_n)/2$:

| $n$ | $D_n$ |
|---|---|
| $0$ | $1/\pi \approx 0.318$ |
| $\pm 1$ | $(0 - j/2)/2 = -j/4$, magnitude $\vert D_1 \vert = 0.25$ |
| $\pm 2$ | $(-2/(3\pi) - 0)/2 = -1/(3\pi)$, magnitude $\vert D_2 \vert \approx 0.106$ |
| $\pm 3$ | $0$ |

**Magnitude Spectrum Sketch:** Spectral lines at $n\omega_0 = n\pi$ rad/s with heights $\vert D_n \vert$ — DC line at 0.318, lines at $\pm\pi$ of height 0.25, lines at $\pm 2\pi$ of height 0.106, all others zero. **(3 marks for spectrum + 2 marks for conversion derivation)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** State and prove **Parseval's Power Theorem** for continuous-time periodic signals. Hence compute the average power of the signal $x(t) = 1 + 2\cos(100\pi t) + 3\sin(200\pi t)$ in **two ways** — time domain and frequency domain — and verify they match. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic arm is driven by a periodic control signal $x(t)$ that is a **triangular wave** of amplitude $A = 4$ V and period $T_0 = 0.02$ s. Find the **trigonometric Fourier series** of this signal. Comment on the **mechanical significance** of the higher harmonics for the robotic arm. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Statement (1 mark):** For a periodic signal $x(t)$ with Fourier coefficients $D_n$, the average power over one period is:

$$P = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert D_n \vert^2$$

**Proof (3 marks):** [As derived in Section 3.2 of these notes — substitute $x(t) = \sum_k D_k e^{jk\omega_0 t}$, conjugate it, multiply, integrate over $T_0$, apply orthogonality, obtain $\sum \vert D_n \vert^2$.]

**Application (3 marks):** Given $x(t) = 1 + 2\cos(100\pi t) + 3\sin(200\pi t)$.

*Time-domain power:*
$$P_T = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0} [1 + 2\cos(100\pi t) + 3\sin(200\pi t)]^2 dt$$

Expanding and using $\langle \cos^2 \rangle = \langle \sin^2 \rangle = 1/2$ and $\langle \cos \cdot \sin \rangle = 0$:

$$P_T = 1^2 + 2^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + 3^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 1 + 2 + 4.5 = 7.5 \text{ W}$$

*Frequency-domain:* Convert to exponential. $1 = D_0$, $2\cos(100\pi t) = e^{j100\pi t} + e^{-j100\pi t}$ gives $D_{\pm 1} = 1$, $3\sin(200\pi t) = \dfrac{3}{2j}(e^{j200\pi t} - e^{-j200\pi t})$ gives $D_{\pm 2} = \mp j \cdot 1.5$, so $\vert D_{\pm 2} \vert^2 = 2.25$.

$$P_F = 1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 + 2.25 + 2.25 = 7.5 \text{ W} \checkmark$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Setting up (2 marks):** Triangular wave with $A = 4$ V, $T_0 = 0.02$ s, so $\omega_0 = 2\pi/0.02 = 100\pi$ rad/s. The wave is an **even function**, so $b_n = 0$.

**Computing $a_0$ (1 mark):** $a_0 = \frac{1}{T_0}\int x(t)\, dt = 0$ (zero average, symmetric triangular wave about origin)

**Computing $a_n$ (2 marks):** For $n \geq 1$:

$$a_n = \frac{8}{T_0^2}\int_0^{T_0/2} \frac{2A}{T_0}\left(\frac{T_0}{2} - t\right)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt$$

Standard result for a triangular wave:

$$a_n = \frac{8A}{\pi^2 n^2}\sin^2\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right) \cdot (-1)^{(n-1)/2} \text{ for odd } n$$

For $A = 4$:

$$a_n = \begin{cases} \dfrac{32}{\pi^2 n^2}(-1)^{(n-1)/2}, & n \text{ odd} \\ 0, & n \text{ even} \end{cases}$$

**Final Series (1 mark):**

$$x(t) = \frac{32}{\pi^2}\left[\cos(100\pi t) - \frac{1}{9}\cos(300\pi t) + \frac{1}{25}\cos(500\pi t) - \cdots\right]$$

**Mechanical Significance (1 mark):** The higher harmonics decay as $1/n^2$, which is much faster than the $1/n$ decay of a square wave. This means a triangular control signal produces **far less high-frequency vibration** in the robotic arm joints — making it the preferred waveform for precision robotic actuators.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $1/T_0$** in the analysis equation — a $D_n$ value ends up being $T_0$ times too large. Deduct **2 marks** if missed.
> 2. **Sign error in $D_n$ conversion** — confusing $D_n = (a_n - jb_n)/2$ with $D_n = (a_n + jb_n)/2$. Deduct **1 mark**.
> 3. **Wrong integration limits** — students often integrate over $[0, T_0]$ even when the signal is non-zero only on a sub-interval. Always choose the interval that simplifies the integrand.
> 4. **Skipping the DC component** in the final series representation. $D_0 = a_0$ is a **mandatory** term.
> 5. **Forgetting to express the answer in both magnitude AND phase** when asked for the "complex spectrum." Many students provide only magnitude and lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Card for Continuous-Time Fourier Series**

- **CTFS applies ONLY to periodic signals** $x(t)$ with finite period $T_0$.
- The **fundamental angular frequency** is $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T_0$.
- **Three equivalent forms:** Trigonometric, Harmonic (compact trig), and Complex Exponential — all carry the same information.
- **Synthesis equation** reconstructs $x(t)$ from coefficients; **Analysis equation** extracts coefficients from $x(t)$.
- **Dirichlet conditions** are 3: absolute integrability, finite discontinuities, finite extrema per period.
- **Symmetry shortcuts save time:** Even signal → $b_n = 0$; Odd signal → $a_n = 0$; Half-wave symmetric → only odd harmonics exist.
- **For real signals:** $D_{-n} = D_n^{*}$ (conjugate symmetry) — the spectrum is **Hermitian**.
- **Parseval's theorem:** Total power is conserved between time and frequency domains — a key result for energy-aware robot design.
- **Conversion formulas:** $D_n = (a_n - jb_n)/2$ for $n \geq 1$; $D_0 = a_0$; $C_n = \sqrt{a_n^2 + b_n^2}$ for the compact form.
- **Spectral decay rate signals physical behavior:** $1/n$ decay = discontinuity in signal; $1/n^2$ decay = slope discontinuity; $1/n^3$ decay = curvature discontinuity.
- **In robotics:** CTFS is the **mathematical foundation** for analyzing motor control signals, vibration analysis of robotic joints, and identifying resonant frequencies that cause mechanical failure.
- **Memory aid for KTU:** "**A**nalysis gives **D**irect **N**umbers; **S**ynthesis **S**ums them up." 
- **Common exam trap:** The CTFS is *discrete* in frequency (spectral lines at $n\omega_0$) but the CTFT is *continuous* in frequency. Don't confuse them.
- **Final formula to memorize:** $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} D_n e^{j n \omega_0 t}$ and $D_n = \dfrac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0} x(t) e^{-j n \omega_0 t} dt$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Time Fourier Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuous Time Fourier Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Continuous Time Fourier Transform (CTFT)** is a mathematical transform that decomposes a continuous, time-domain signal $x(t)$ into its constituent frequency components. It is the limiting case of the Continuous Time Fourier Series (CTFS) when the fundamental period $T \to \infty$, causing the discrete harmonic spectrum to merge into a continuous frequency spectrum.

The **Analysis Equation (Forward Transform)** expresses a signal as a continuous superposition of complex exponentials:

$$X(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

The **Synthesis Equation (Inverse Transform)** reconstructs the time-domain signal from its frequency-domain representation:

$$x(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{X(j\omega)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\omega) e^{j\omega t} d\omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The notation $X(j\omega)$ is preferred over $X(\omega)$ because it explicitly emphasizes that the frequency variable is purely imaginary on the $j\omega$-axis of the complex s-plane. This convention aligns with the Laplace Transform evaluated on the imaginary axis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Prism Analogy:** Imagine shining a beam of white light (your time-domain signal) through a glass prism. The prism splits this single, complex beam into a beautiful rainbow spectrum — that is exactly what the Fourier Transform does to a signal. Each color in the rainbow represents a specific frequency, and the intensity of each color tells you "how much" of that frequency is present in the original signal. The **Inverse Fourier Transform** is the reverse process — a second prism that recombines the rainbow back into white light.

### 1.3 The Concept of "Frequency Content" for Aperiodic Signals

For **periodic signals**, the Fourier Series gives a discrete line spectrum with harmonics spaced at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency $\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T}$. When $T \to \infty$:
- The spacing between harmonics approaches **zero** ($\omega_0 \to 0$).
- The discrete lines merge into a **continuous spectrum**.
- The amplitude of each harmonic becomes infinitesimally small, requiring a density function (the spectrum $X(j\omega)$) to represent energy distribution per unit bandwidth.

This transition is **fundamental** in robotics signal processing, where real-world sensor signals (LIDAR, motor encoders, EMG) are almost always **aperiodic and non-repeating**.

### 1.4 Standardized Conventions Used in KTU

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $x(t)$ | Continuous-time signal | Volts (V) or Amperes (A) |
| $X(j\omega)$ | Fourier Transform of $x(t)$ | V/Hz or V·s |
| $\omega$ | Angular frequency | **radians per second (rad/s)** |
| $f$ | Cyclic frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Conversion relation | — |
| $j$ | Imaginary unit $\sqrt{-1}$ | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Robotics Application:** In robotic vision, a 2D Fourier Transform of an image reveals its spatial frequency content. Low frequencies encode smooth regions (sky, walls), while high frequencies encode edges and fine details (screw threads, text). This is the basis of frequency-domain filtering in image processing pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Existence Conditions — Dirichlet Conditions

The CTFT exists (converges) for a signal $x(t)$ if and only if it satisfies the following three **Dirichlet Conditions**:

1. **Absolute Integrability:** The signal must be absolutely integrable over all time.

   $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$

2. **Finite Number of Discontinuities:** The signal can have only a **finite number of maxima, minima, and discontinuities** within any finite interval.

3. **Bounded Variation:** The signal must be of bounded variation in any finite interval.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Misconception:** Signals like $\sin(\omega t)$, $u(t)$ (unit step), and $\cos(\omega t)$ are **NOT** absolutely integrable. Yet their CTFTs exist as **generalized functions** (distributions) involving Dirac delta functions. KTU examiners frequently test this nuance.

### 2.2 The Transform Pair in Both Frequency Domains

The CTFT can be expressed in two mathematically equivalent forms:

**Angular Frequency Form (preferred in KTU):**

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\omega) e^{j\omega t} d\omega$$

**Cyclic (Hz) Frequency Form:**

$$X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j2\pi f t} dt$$

$$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) e^{j2\pi f t} df$$

The conversion identity between the two forms is:

$$X(j\omega) = X(f) \Big\vert_{f = \omega/2\pi}$$

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic: Why Does the CTFT Work?

The CTFT emerges logically from the CTFS through the following chain of reasoning:

- **Step 1 — Start with CTFS:** A periodic signal with period $T$ has harmonics at $\omega_k = k\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi k}{T}$ with complex amplitudes $a_k = \frac{1}{T}\int_{-T/2}^{T/2} x(t) e^{-jk\omega_0 t} dt$.
- **Step 2 — Introduce Spectral Density:** Define a new quantity $X(j\omega_k) = T \cdot a_k$. This represents **energy per unit bandwidth** rather than raw amplitude.
- **Step 3 — Take the Limit $T \to \infty$:** As $T$ grows, the spacing $\Delta\omega = \omega_0 \to 0$, harmonics merge, and the discrete sum becomes a continuous integral.
- **Step 4 — Reconstruction:** Substituting back and taking the limit yields the synthesis integral with the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor that preserves Parseval's energy theorem.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Property / Concept | Time Domain $x(t)$ | Frequency Domain $X(j\omega)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linearity** | $a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)$ | $a X_1(j\omega) + b X_2(j\omega)$ |
| 2 | **Time Shifting** | $x(t - t_0)$ | $X(j\omega) e^{-j\omega t_0}$ |
| 3 | **Frequency Shifting** | $x(t) e^{j\omega_0 t}$ | $X(j(j\omega - j\omega_0))$ |
| 4 | **Time Scaling** | $x(at)$ | $\frac{1}{\vert a \vert} X\left(\frac{j\omega}{a}\right)$ |
| 5 | **Time Reversal** | $x(-t)$ | $X(-j\omega)$ |
| 6 | **Differentiation (time)** | $\frac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | $j\omega \, X(j\omega)$ |
| 7 | **Integration (time)** | $\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$ | $\frac{X(j\omega)}{j\omega} + \pi X(0) \delta(\omega)$ |
| 8 | **Multiplication (conv.)** | $x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} X_1(j\omega) * X_2(j\omega)$ |
| 9 | **Convolution** | $x_1(t) * x_2(t)$ | $X_1(j\omega) \cdot X_2(j\omega)$ |
| 10 | **Duality** | $X(jt)$ | $2\pi \, x(-\omega)$ |
| 11 | **Parseval's Theorem** | $\int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$ |
| 12 | **Conjugate Symmetry** (real $x$) | $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}$ | $X(-j\omega) = X^*(j\omega)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Property 6 (Differentiation) is **THE** most-tested property in Part B questions, especially when paired with solving linear constant-coefficient differential equations in the frequency domain. Memorize: differentiation in time $\Leftrightarrow$ multiplication by $j\omega$ in frequency.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotic Control Systems:** CTFT converts differential equations of motor dynamics into algebraic equations, dramatically simplifying stability and controller design.
- **Audio/Speech Processing:** Voice recognition front-ends use CTFT (via STFT) to extract formants and MFCCs.
- **Vibration Analysis:** Robot arm structural vibrations are diagnosed by inspecting their frequency signatures.
- **Filter Design:** Butterworth, Chebyshev, and elliptic analog filters are specified by their frequency responses $H(j\omega)$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: CTFT of the Rectangular Pulse

**Problem:** Derive $X(j\omega)$ for the rectangular pulse $x(t) = \text{rect}\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$, defined as:

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert t \vert < \frac{\tau}{2} \\ 0, & \vert t \vert > \frac{\tau}{2} \end{cases}$$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the analysis equation.** Substitute $x(t)$ into the forward transform definition.

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the piecewise definition.** The pulse is non-zero only on the interval $\left[-\frac{\tau}{2}, \frac{\tau}{2}\right]$, so the integration limits collapse to this interval.

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} (1) \cdot e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration.** The antiderivative of $e^{-j\omega t}$ with respect to $t$ is $\frac{e^{-j\omega t}}{-j\omega}$.

$$X(j\omega) = \left[ \frac{e^{-j\omega t}}{-j\omega} \right]_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at the upper bound $t = \tau/2$.**

$$X(j\omega) = \frac{e^{-j\omega \tau/2}}{-j\omega}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at the lower bound $t = -\tau/2$.**

$$X(j\omega) = \frac{e^{+j\omega \tau/2}}{-j\omega}$$

**Step 6 — Subtract the lower from upper bound.**

$$X(j\omega) = \frac{e^{-j\omega \tau/2} - e^{+j\omega \tau/2}}{-j\omega}$$

**Step 7 — Factor the numerator.** Recognize the difference of exponentials as the sine form.

$$e^{-j\omega \tau/2} - e^{+j\omega \tau/2} = -2j \sin\!\left(\frac{\omega \tau}{2}\right)$$

**Step 8 — Substitute and simplify.** The two $j$'s cancel, leaving a real, even function.

$$X(j\omega) = \frac{-2j \sin(\omega \tau/2)}{-j\omega} = \frac{2 \sin(\omega \tau/2)}{\omega}$$

**Step 9 — Express in normalized sinc form.** Recall the definition $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$. The final result is:

$$\boxed{X(j\omega) = \tau \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega \tau}{2\pi}\right) = \tau \, \frac{\sin(\omega \tau/2)}{\omega \tau/2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A rectangular pulse in time produces a sinc function in frequency. As $\tau$ increases (wider pulse), the main lobe in frequency becomes narrower. This is the fundamental **time-frequency duality** that underlies radar pulse compression, a key technology in robotic sensing.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: CTFT of the Exponential Decay

**Problem:** Derive $X(j\omega)$ for $x(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$, where $a > 0$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the analysis equation.** The unit step $u(t)$ limits the integration to $t \geq 0$.

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-at} u(t) \cdot e^{-j\omega t} dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(a + j\omega)t} dt$$

**Step 2 — Combine the exponents.**

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(a + j\omega)t} dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate.** For $\text{Re}(a) > 0$, the integrand decays exponentially, ensuring convergence.

$$X(j\omega) = \left[ \frac{e^{-(a+j\omega)t}}{-(a+j\omega)} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 4 — Apply limits.** At $t \to \infty$, the exponential vanishes; at $t = 0$, it equals 1.

$$X(j\omega) = \left( 0 - \frac{1}{-(a+j\omega)} \right) = \frac{1}{a + j\omega}$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{X(j\omega) = \frac{1}{a + j\omega}, \quad a > 0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always specify $a > 0$. Without this constraint, the integral diverges and the CTFT **does not exist** in the classical sense.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Proving the Differentiation Property

**Theorem:** If $X(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\}$, then $\mathcal{F}\!\left\{\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = j\omega \, X(j\omega)$.

**Step-by-Step Proof:**

**Step 1 — Start with the synthesis equation.**

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\omega) e^{j\omega t} d\omega$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $t$.** Since $X(j\omega)$ is independent of $t$, it acts as a constant inside the integral.

$$\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\omega) \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{j\omega t}\right] d\omega$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the exponential.** $\frac{d}{dt} e^{j\omega t} = j\omega \, e^{j\omega t}$.

$$\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ j\omega \, X(j\omega) \right] e^{j\omega t} d\omega$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the inverse transform structure.** The right-hand side has the exact form of $\mathcal{F}^{-1}\{j\omega \, X(j\omega)\}$.

$$\boxed{\frac{dx(t)}{dt} \;\longleftrightarrow\; j\omega \, X(j\omega) \quad \blacksquare}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Standard CTFT Pairs (KTU Must-Know)

| # | Time-Domain Signal $x(t)$ | Frequency-Domain $X(j\omega)$ | Magnitude Characteristic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta(t)$ | $1$ | Flat (all-pass) |
| 2 | $1$ (constant) | $2\pi \delta(\omega)$ | Impulse at DC |
| 3 | $e^{j\omega_0 t}$ | $2\pi \delta(\omega - \omega_0)$ | Impulse at $\omega_0$ |
| 4 | $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | $\pi[\delta(\omega-\omega_0) + \delta(\omega+\omega_0)]$ | Two symmetric impulses |
| 5 | $\sin(\omega_0 t)$ | $\frac{\pi}{j}[\delta(\omega-\omega_0) - \delta(\omega+\omega_0)]$ | Two symmetric impulses |
| 6 | $u(t)$ (unit step) | $\frac{1}{j\omega} + \pi \delta(\omega)$ | $1/\omega$ roll-off plus DC impulse |
| 7 | $e^{-at} u(t)$, $a>0$ | $\frac{1}{a+j\omega}$ | First-order low-pass |
| 8 | $e^{-a \vert t \vert}$, $a>0$ | $\frac{2a}{a^2 + \omega^2}$ | Symmetric low-pass |
| 9 | $\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$ | $\tau \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega \tau}{2\pi}\right)$ | Sinc shape |
| 10 | $e^{-t^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$ | $\sigma \sqrt{2\pi} \, e^{-\sigma^2 \omega^2 / 2}$ | Gaussian $\to$ Gaussian |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: CTFT Analysis-Synthesis Pair

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the fundamental CTFT engine — the forward and inverse paths with their domain conversions, signal flow direction, and key inputs/outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigA["x of t"]:::sigNode --> mulA["Multiplication by e power minus j omega t"]:::procNode
    mulA --> intA["Integration from minus infinity to plus infinity over t"]:::procNode
    intA --> spec["X of j omega"]:::freqNode

    spec --> mulB["Multiplication by e power j omega t"]:::procNode
    mulB --> intB["Integration from minus infinity to plus infinity over omega"]:::procNode
    intB --> scale["Multiply by 1 over 2 pi"]:::procNode
    scale --> recA["Reconstructed x of t"]:::sigNode

    axis1[/"Time Domain: t in seconds"/]:::axisStyle -.-> sigA
    axis2[/"Frequency Domain: omega in rad per s"/]:::axisStyle -.-> spec

    classDef sigNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef freqNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procNode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef axisStyle fill:none,stroke:none,color:#555,font-style:italic
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: CTFT Derivation Roadmap (CTFS → CTFT)

This diagram shows the conceptual evolution from periodic Fourier Series to the aperiodic Fourier Transform, which is a frequent **KTU 14-mark question** theme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Periodic Signal x of t with period T"]:::startNode --> fs["Compute CTFS Coefficients a sub k"]:::procNode
    fs --> dens["Define Spectral Density X of j omega k = T times a sub k"]:::procNode
    dens --> lim["Take Limit T approaches infinity"]:::procNode
    lim --> omegaC["Fundamental Frequency omega naught approaches 0"]:::procNode
    omegaC --> cont["Discrete Spectrum becomes Continuous"]:::procNode
    cont --> ctft["Continuous Time Fourier Transform"]:::finalNode
    ctft --> pair["Analysis Synthesis Pair Established"]:::finalNode

    classDef startNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef finalNode fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: CTFT in a Robotic Signal Processing Pipeline

The block diagram below contextualizes the CTFT inside a **typical robotic sensor processing chain**, demonstrating real-world engineering relevance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    preamp --> adc["Analog to Digital Converter ADC"]:::procNode
    adc --> win["Apply Window Function w of t"]:::procNode
    win --> ctft["Compute CTFT X of j omega"]:::keyNode
    ctft --> mag["Magnitude Spectrum vertical bar X bar"]:::procNode
    ctft --> phs["Phase Spectrum angle X"]:::procNode
    mag --> fex["Feature Extraction for SLAM or Control"]:::keyNode
    phs --> fex
    fex --> dec["Robot Decision Making"]:::hwNode

    classDef hwNode fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procNode fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The blue/orange/purple color coding follows a consistent convention: **blue** for time-domain signals, **orange** for frequency-domain spectra, and **purple** for processing operations. This convention is used throughout KTU 2024 Scheme reference materials.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1] [Module 2]**
>
> **Q1.** Define the Continuous Time Fourier Transform (CTFT) of a signal $x(t)$. State the Dirichlet conditions for its existence. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The CTFT of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

It represents the signal as a continuous superposition of complex exponentials $e^{j\omega t}$.

The **Dirichlet conditions** for existence are:
1. $x(t)$ must be absolutely integrable: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$.
2. $x(t)$ must have a finite number of discontinuities, maxima, and minima in any finite interval.
3. $x(t)$ must be of bounded variation in any finite interval.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2] [Module 2]**
>
> **Q2.** State and explain any **three properties** of the Continuous Time Fourier Transform. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Property | Mathematical Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linearity** | $\mathcal{F}\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a X_1(j\omega) + b X_2(j\omega)$ |
| 2 | **Time Shifting** | $x(t - t_0) \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(j\omega) e^{-j\omega t_0}$ |
| 3 | **Frequency Shifting (Modulation)** | $x(t) e^{j\omega_0 t} \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(j(\omega - \omega_0))$ |
| 4 | **Convolution in Time** | $x_1(t) * x_2(t) \;\longleftrightarrow\; X_1(j\omega) \cdot X_2(j\omega)$ |
| 5 | **Differentiation in Time** | $\frac{dx(t)}{dt} \;\longleftrightarrow\; j\omega \, X(j\omega)$ |

(Any three properties with correct mathematical statements earn full marks.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper] [Module 2]**
>
> **Q3 (A). (a)** Find the Fourier Transform of the rectangular pulse $x(t)$ defined as $A$ for $\vert t \vert < \tau$ and $0$ otherwise. Sketch both $x(t)$ and $X(j\omega)$. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using the properties of Fourier Transform, find the CTFT of $x(t) = t \cdot e^{-at} u(t)$ where $a > 0$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q3 (A) — Part (a):

**Step 1 — Define the signal.** The rectangular pulse is $x(t) = A$ for $\vert t \vert < \tau$, zero elsewhere.

**Step 2 — Apply the analysis equation.** The signal is non-zero only in $[-\tau, \tau]$.

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{-\tau}^{\tau} A \cdot e^{-j\omega t} dt = A \int_{-\tau}^{\tau} e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration.**

$$X(j\omega) = A \left[ \frac{e^{-j\omega t}}{-j\omega} \right]_{-\tau}^{\tau} = \frac{A}{-j\omega} \left[ e^{-j\omega \tau} - e^{j\omega \tau} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using Euler's identity.** $e^{-j\theta} - e^{j\theta} = -2j \sin(\theta)$.

$$X(j\omega) = \frac{A \cdot (-2j \sin(\omega \tau))}{-j\omega} = \frac{2A \sin(\omega \tau)}{\omega}$$

**Step 5 — Express in sinc form.**

$$\boxed{X(j\omega) = 2A\tau \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega \tau}{\pi}\right) = 2A\tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega \tau)}{\omega \tau}}$$

**Step 6 — Sketch characteristics:**
- $X(0) = 2A\tau$ (peak value at DC).
- Zeros at $\omega = \frac{n\pi}{\tau}$ for $n = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$
- Even symmetry: $X(-j\omega) = X(j\omega)$.

#### Model Solution for Q3 (A) — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the known transform pair.** The standard result is:

$$e^{-at} u(t) \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{a + j\omega}, \quad a > 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply the frequency-differentiation property.** The general property is:

$$t \cdot x(t) \;\longleftrightarrow\; j \frac{dX(j\omega)}{d\omega}$$

This follows from differentiating $X(j\omega) = \int x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ with respect to $\omega$.

**Step 3 — Differentiate the known transform.**

$$\frac{d}{d\omega} \left[ \frac{1}{a + j\omega} \right] = \frac{-j}{(a + j\omega)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $j$ to apply the property.**

$$X(j\omega) = j \cdot \frac{-j}{(a + j\omega)^2} = \frac{1}{(a + j\omega)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\mathcal{F}\{t \, e^{-at} u(t)\} = \frac{1}{(a + j\omega)^2}}$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper] [Module 2]**
>
> **Q3 (B). (a)** Derive the Fourier Transform of the **triangular pulse** $x(t) = 1 - \frac{\vert t \vert}{T}$ for $\vert t \vert < T$, and zero elsewhere. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** State and prove **Parseval's Theorem** for Continuous Time Fourier Transform. Explain its physical significance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q3 (B) — Part (a):

**Step 1 — Express as a convolution.** A triangular pulse is the convolution of two rectangular pulses of width $T$ and height $1$:

$$x(t) = \left( \frac{1}{T} \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{T}\right) \right) * \left( \frac{1}{T} \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{T}\right) \right)$$

Wait — re-examining, the simpler direct approach is direct integration.

**Step 1 (Alternative — Direct Integration):** The pulse has even symmetry, so use $2 \int_0^T$:

$$X(j\omega) = 2 \int_{0}^{T} \left(1 - \frac{t}{T}\right) \cos(\omega t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Split into two integrals.**

$$X(j\omega) = 2 \int_0^T \cos(\omega t) dt - \frac{2}{T} \int_0^T t \cos(\omega t) dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the first integral.**

$$2 \int_0^T \cos(\omega t) dt = 2 \left[ \frac{\sin(\omega t)}{\omega} \right]_0^T = \frac{2 \sin(\omega T)}{\omega}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the second integral using integration by parts.** Let $u = t$, $dv = \cos(\omega t) dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = \frac{\sin(\omega t)}{\omega}$.

$$\int_0^T t \cos(\omega t) dt = \left[ \frac{t \sin(\omega t)}{\omega} \right]_0^T - \int_0^T \frac{\sin(\omega t)}{\omega} dt$$

$$= \frac{T \sin(\omega T)}{\omega} - \left[ -\frac{\cos(\omega t)}{\omega^2} \right]_0^T = \frac{T \sin(\omega T)}{\omega} + \frac{\cos(\omega T) - 1}{\omega^2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back.**

$$X(j\omega) = \frac{2 \sin(\omega T)}{\omega} - \frac{2}{T} \left[ \frac{T \sin(\omega T)}{\omega} + \frac{\cos(\omega T) - 1}{\omega^2} \right]$$

**Step 6 — Simplify.**

$$X(j\omega) = \frac{2 \sin(\omega T)}{\omega} - \frac{2 \sin(\omega T)}{\omega} - \frac{2(\cos(\omega T) - 1)}{T \omega^2}$$

$$X(j\omega) = \frac{2(1 - \cos(\omega T))}{T \omega^2} = \frac{4 \sin^2(\omega T / 2)}{T \omega^2}$$

**Step 7 — Final sinc-squared form.**

$$\boxed{X(j\omega) = T \left[ \frac{\sin(\omega T / 2)}{\omega T / 2} \right]^2 = T \, \text{sinc}^2\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2\pi}\right)}$$

#### Model Solution for Q3 (B) — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Statement.** Parseval's Theorem for the CTFT states:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$$

**Step 2 — Proof.** Start with the right-hand side:

$$\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\omega) X^*(j\omega) d\omega$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $X^*(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^*(t) e^{+j\omega t} dt$.**

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\omega) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^*(t) e^{+j\omega t} dt \right] d\omega$$

**Step 4 — Swap order of integration (Fubini's Theorem).**

$$= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^*(t) \left[ \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\omega) e^{j\omega t} d\omega \right] dt$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the inner integral as the inverse Fourier Transform**, which equals $x(t)$.

$$= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^*(t) \cdot x(t) dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 6 — Physical Significance.** The theorem states that the total energy of a signal computed in the **time domain equals** the total energy computed in the **frequency domain**. It establishes that the Fourier Transform is an **energy-preserving** operation. In robotics, this is used to compute signal power in either domain interchangeably, especially for designing filters that minimize energy loss.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in CTFT questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor** in the inverse transform — costs 1–2 marks almost every time.
> 2. **Mixing up cyclic and angular frequency** forms — when the question gives $f$ in Hz and you write $\omega$, marks are deducted for "incomplete conversion."
> 3. **Not stating existence conditions** for the transform — always mention $a > 0$ for exponentials, finite energy for arbitrary signals.
> 4. **Sign errors in modulation property**: $x(t) e^{+j\omega_0 t} \to X(j(\omega - \omega_0))$ — the sign of the shift is reversed from the time-shifting property.
> 5. **Skipping the final sinc form**: Writing $\frac{\sin(\omega\tau/2)}{\omega/2}$ is correct but **not the standardized KTU form**. Always reduce to $\tau \, \text{sinc}(\cdot)$.
> 6. **Not specifying real/imaginary parts separately** when asked to sketch — examiners expect $X(j\omega) = A(\omega) + jB(\omega)$ decomposition.
> 7. **Convolution vs. Multiplication duality confusion**: Time-domain convolution $\Leftrightarrow$ Frequency-domain multiplication. Getting this flipped is a 7-mark disaster in Part B.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **CTFT exists only for aperiodic signals** (or for one period of a periodic signal truncated to a finite interval).
- ✅ **The factor $\frac{1}{2\pi}$** appears in the **inverse** transform (angular frequency form), not the forward.
- ✅ **Dirichlet Conditions:** absolute integrability, finite discontinuities, bounded variation.
- ✅ **Rectangular pulse in time $\to$ sinc in frequency** (and vice versa — by duality).
- ✅ **Gaussian in time $\to$ Gaussian in frequency** (self-dual — unique property).
- ✅ **Differentiate in time $\Leftrightarrow$ multiply by $j\omega$ in frequency.**
- ✅ **Convolve in time $\Leftrightarrow$ multiply in frequency.** Multiply in time $\Leftrightarrow$ convolve in frequency (with $\frac{1}{2\pi}$).
- ✅ **Time shift by $t_0$** introduces linear phase $e^{-j\omega t_0}$ — magnitude spectrum is unchanged.
- ✅ **Real signals have conjugate-symmetric spectra:** $X(-j\omega) = X^*(j\omega)$.
- ✅ **Even functions in time** $\to$ **real, even spectra**; **odd functions in time** $\to$ **imaginary, odd spectra**.
- ✅ **Unit impulse $\delta(t) \leftrightarrow 1$** (flat spectrum); **constant $1 \leftrightarrow 2\pi\delta(\omega)$** (impulse spectrum).
- ✅ **Energy is conserved** under the CTFT (Parseval's Theorem) — total energy in time = total energy in frequency.
- ✅ **For the KTU exam, ALWAYS** show the explicit integration steps, even if the transform pair is "standard" — partial-step credit depends on it.
- ✅ **The notation $X(j\omega)$ is preferred** over $X(\omega)$ in KTU 2024 Scheme papers — reflects the imaginary-axis interpretation.
- ✅ **Generalized functions ($\delta$, $u$) require special handling** — they are NOT absolutely integrable but their CTFTs exist as distributions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laplace Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Laplace Transform # Module 2: Laplace Transform — Section 1: Foundations & Intuition ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Laplace Transform** is a powerful integral transform that converts a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ defined in the **time domain** into a complex-valued function $X(s)$ in the **s-domain** (complex frequency domain). Mathematically, it is defined as: $$ X(s) = \mathcal{L}\{x(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-st} \, dt $$ Where: - $x(t)$ is the time-domain signal (typically causal, i.e., $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$) - $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is a complex frequency variable - $\sigma$ is the real part (damping factor), $\omega$ is the imaginary part (angular frequency) - The integral is evaluated from $0$ to $\infty$ (one-sided Laplace Transform) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The course uses the **Unilateral (One-Sided) Laplace Transform** with lower limit $t = 0^-$. The variable $s$ is complex, distinguishing the Laplace Transform from the Fourier Transform (which uses $s = j\omega$). ## 1.2 Region of Convergence (ROC) The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is the set of all values of $s$ in the complex plane for which the Laplace Transform integral converges to a finite value. It is essential because two different signals can have identical $X(s)$ expressions but different ROCs. > [!NOTE] > **Key Properties of ROC:** > - ROC is always bounded by poles (singularities) of $X(s)$ > - ROC cannot include any pole of $X(s)$ > - For a **right-sided signal** (causal), ROC extends to the right of the rightmost pole > - For a **left-sided signal**, ROC extends to the left of the leftmost pole > - For a **two-sided signal**, ROC is a vertical strip between two poles ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are a **robotics engineer** analyzing the response of a robotic arm to a motor command. Measuring the arm's position $x(t)$ over time gives you a messy, oscillating curve — hard to analyze directly. > **Analogy: The Audio Equalizer** > Think of a song waveform on a screen (time domain). It looks like jagged, irregular peaks. Now imagine a graphic equalizer (frequency domain) showing separate bars for bass, mid, treble. The Laplace Transform does exactly this for **any engineering signal** — it breaks down a complex time-domain function into a "recipe" of exponentially-growing or decaying sinusoids characterized by complex frequency $s$. In robotics, this lets you analyze motor controllers, sensor filters, and feedback loops using simple algebraic equations instead of complex differential equations. ## 1.4 Physical Significance in Robotics - **Solving Differential Equations:** Robot dynamics (e.g., $J\ddot{\theta} + b\dot{\theta} + k\theta = \tau$) become simple algebraic equations in $s$-domain - **Transfer Function Analysis:** Characterize DC motors, PID controllers, and filters - **Stability Analysis:** Position of poles in ROC directly indicates system stability - **Sensor Signal Processing:** De-noise IMU and LiDAR signals - **Control System Design:** Block diagram manipulation becomes algebraic > [!TIP] > **Why Laplace over Fourier in Robotics?** Many robotic signals are **non-periodic** or have **exponential growth/decay** (e.g., step responses, transient responses). The Laplace Transform handles these naturally via the $\sigma$ damping factor, while Fourier Transform may not converge for such signals. ## 1.5 Standard Notation and Conventions | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range | |--------|---------|---------------| | $x(t)$ | Continuous-time input signal | $t \in [0, \infty)$ | | $X(s)$ | Laplace Transform of $x(t)$ | $s \in \mathbb{C}$ | | $s$ | Complex frequency | $s = \sigma + j\omega$ | | $\sigma$ | Real part (Neper frequency) | $\mathbb{R}$ | | $\omega$ | Imaginary part (radian frequency) | rad/sec | | ROC | Region of Convergence | Subset of $s$-plane | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** Always state the ROC alongside the transform expression for full marks, unless the problem specifies a causal signal. # Module 2: Laplace Transform — Section 2: Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Laplace Transform of Elementary Functions The following derivations form the **foundation** for all robotics signal processing problems. Memorize the transform pairs in the table below. ### Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown) **Step 1 — Time-domain signal $x(t)$** is identified. **Step 2 — Substitute** $x(t)$ into the defining integral $X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$. **Step 3 — Evaluate** the resulting integral using standard calculus techniques (integration by parts, known standard forms). **Step 4 — Simplify** the expression to obtain a closed-form rational function of $s$. **Step 5 — Identify the ROC** by finding values of $s$ for which the integral converges. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Standard Laplace Transform Pairs** — Essential for KTU 2024 Examinations. Notation: $\mathcal{L}\{x(t)\} = X(s)$, ROC shown in subscript. | # | Time Domain $x(t)$ | Laplace Domain $X(s)$ | ROC | |---|---------------------|------------------------|-----| | 1 | $\delta(t)$ (Unit Impulse) | $1$ | All $s$ | | 2 | $u(t)$ (Unit Step) | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $\operatorname{Re}(s) > 0$ | | 3 | $e^{-at}u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s+a}$ | $\operatorname{Re}(s) > -a$ | | 4 | $t \cdot u(t)$ (Ramp) | $\dfrac{1}{s^{2}}$ | $\operatorname{Re}(s) > 0$ | | 5 | $t^{n} u(t)$ | $\dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$ | $\operatorname{Re}(s) > 0$ | | 6 | $\cos(\omega_{0} t) u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^{2} + \omega_{0}^{2}}$ | $\operatorname{Re}(s) > 0$ | | 7 | $\sin(\omega_{0} t) u(t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega_{0}}{s^{2} + \omega_{0}^{2}}$ | $\operatorname{Re}(s) > 0$ | | 8 | $e^{-at}\cos(\omega_{0} t) u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s+a}{(s+a)^{2} + \omega_{0}^{2}}$ | $\operatorname{Re}(s) > -a$ | | 9 | $e^{-at}\sin(\omega_{0} t) u(t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega_{0}}{(s+a)^{2} + \omega_{0}^{2}}$ | $\operatorname{Re}(s) > -a$ | | 10 | $\sinh(at) u(t)$ | $\dfrac{a}{s^{2} - a^{2}}$ | $\operatorname{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ | | 11 | $\cosh(at) u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^{2} - a^{2}}$ | $\operatorname{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ | | 12 | $t e^{-at} u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{(s+a)^{2}}$ | $\operatorname{Re}(s) > -a$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction:** For the cosine pair, $X(s) = s / (s^{2} + \omega_{0}^{2})$, the **numerator has $s$**, not a constant. A common mistake is writing $\omega_{0}/(s^{2}+\omega_{0}^{2})$ for cosine — that is **sine**! ## 2.3 KTU Properties of Laplace Transform (Theorem Sheet) > [!NOTE] > These properties are tested every KTU cycle. Each can yield a 7-mark sub-question. | # | Property | Time Domain $x(t)$ | $s$-Domain $X(s)$ | ROC | |---|----------|---------------------|---------------------|-----| | 1 | **Linearity** | $a x_{1}(t) + b x_{2}(t)$ | $a X_{1}(s) + b X_{2}(s)$ | At least $\text{ROC}_{1} \cap \text{ROC}_{2}$ | | 2 | **Time Shifting** | $x(t - t_{0}) u(t - t_{0})$ | $e^{-st_{0}} X(s)$ | Same as $X(s)$ | | 3 | **Frequency Shifting** | $e^{at} x(t)$ | $X(s - a)$ | Shifted by $a$ | | 4 | **Time Scaling** | $x(at)$, $a > 0$ | $\dfrac{1}{a} X\!\left(\dfrac{s}{a}\right)$ | Scaled ROC | | 5 | **Differentiation** | $\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | $s X(s) - x(0^-)$ | At least ROC of $X(s)$ | | 6 | **$n$-th Differentiation** | $\dfrac{d^{n} x(t)}{dt^{n}}$ | $s^{n} X(s) - \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} s^{n-1-k} x^{(k)}(0^-)$ | At least ROC of $X(s)$ | | 7 | **Integration** | $\int_{0^-}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$ | $\dfrac{X(s)}{s}$ | $\text{ROC} \cap \operatorname{Re}(s) > 0$ | | 8 | **Convolution** | $x_{1}(t) * x_{2}(t)$ | $X_{1}(s) \cdot X_{2}(s)$ | At least $\text{ROC}_{1} \cap \text{ROC}_{2}$ | | 9 | **Multiplication by $t$** | $t \, x(t)$ | $-\dfrac{d}{ds} X(s)$ | Same as $X(s)$ | | 10 | **Multiplication by $t^{n}$** | $t^{n} x(t)$ | $(-1)^{n} \dfrac{d^{n}}{ds^{n}} X(s)$ | Same as $X(s)$ | | 11 | **Initial Value Theorem** | $x(0^+)$ | $\lim_{s \to \infty} s X(s)$ | Requires $x(0^+)$ to exist | | 12 | **Final Value Theorem** | $\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t)$ | $\lim_{s \to 0} s X(s)$ | All poles of $sX(s)$ in LHP | ## 2.4 Initial and Final Value Theorems — Critical Conditions > [!WARNING] > **Final Value Theorem Pitfall:** The theorem $\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} sX(s)$ is **valid only if all poles of $sX(s)$ lie strictly in the left half-plane** (i.e., $\operatorname{Re}(\text{poles}) < 0$). Applying it otherwise yields **wrong answers** — a common KTU error. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Robotic Arm Control:** The DC motor transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{\theta(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{K}{s(Js + b)(Ls + R)}$ is derived using Laplace. 2. **PID Controller Design:** Proportional, Integral, Derivative terms are implemented as $K_p$, $K_i/s$, $K_d s$ in $s$-domain. 3. **Sensor Filter Design:** Low-pass, high-pass, band-pass filters for LiDAR and cameras. 4. **Stability Analysis:** Pole location determines whether a robot controller oscillates or settles. 5. **Communication Systems:** Modulation and demodulation in robotic communication links. # Module 2: Laplace Transform — Section 3: Exhaustive Derivations ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Laplace Transform of Unit Step Function $u(t)$ **Given:** $x(t) = u(t) = 1$ for $t \geq 0$, and $0$ for $t < 0$. **Goal:** Find $X(s) = \mathcal{L}\{u(t)\}$. **Step 1:** Start from the defining integral. $$ X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-st} \, dt $$ **Step 2:** Substitute $x(t) = 1$ for $t \in [0, \infty)$. $$ X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} (1) \, e^{-st} \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st} \, dt $$ **Step 3:** Write $s = \sigma + j\omega$ and pull out the real part for convergence analysis. $$ X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(\sigma + j\omega)t} \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\sigma t} e^{-j\omega t} \, dt $$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the integral. The exponential integrates to: $$ X(s) = \left[ \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} \right]_{0}^{\infty} $$ **Step 5:** Apply the limits carefully. At $t \to \infty$, $e^{-st} \to 0$ only if $\operatorname{Re}(s) = \sigma > 0$. $$ X(s) = \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} - \frac{e^{0}}{-s} = 0 - \left( \frac{1}{-s} \right) = \frac{1}{s} $$ **Step 6:** State ROC explicitly. $$ \boxed{\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s}, \quad \text{ROC: } \operatorname{Re}(s) > 0} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Laplace Transform of Exponential $e^{-at}u(t)$ **Given:** $x(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$ where $a$ is a real constant. **Step 1:** Apply the definition. $$ X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} \cdot e^{-st} \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t} \, dt $$ **Step 2:** Combine the exponentials. $$ X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t} \, dt $$ **Step 3:** Perform the integration. Let $\alpha = s + a$. $$ X(s) = \left[ \frac{e^{-\alpha t}}{-\alpha} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \left[ \frac{e^{-(s+a)t}}{-(s+a)} \right]_{0}^{\infty} $$ **Step 4:** For convergence at $t \to \infty$, require $\operatorname{Re}(s+a) > 0$, i.e., $\operatorname{Re}(s) > -a$. $$ X(s) = 0 - \frac{1}{-(s+a)} = \frac{1}{s+a} $$ **Step 5:** Final result. $$ \boxed{\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s+a}, \quad \text{ROC: } \operatorname{Re}(s) > -a} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Laplace Transform of $\cos(\omega_{0} t) u(t)$ **Given:** $x(t) = \cos(\omega_{0} t) u(t)$. **Step 1:** Use Euler's identity: $\cos(\omega_{0} t) = \dfrac{e^{j\omega_{0} t} + e^{-j\omega_{0} t}}{2}$. $$ X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \cos(\omega_{0} t) \, e^{-st} \, dt = \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\infty} \left(e^{j\omega_{0} t} + e^{-j\omega_{0} t}\right) e^{-st} \, dt $$ **Step 2:** Split into two integrals. $$ X(s) = \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s - j\omega_{0})t} \, dt + \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s + j\omega_{0})t} \, dt $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate each integral separately using the exponential formula $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\alpha t} dt = 1/\alpha$. $$ X(s) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{s - j\omega_{0}} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{s + j\omega_{0}} $$ **Step 4:** Combine the fractions over a common denominator. $$ X(s) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(s + j\omega_{0}) + (s - j\omega_{0})}{(s - j\omega_{0})(s + j\omega_{0})} $$ **Step 5:** Simplify numerator and denominator. $$ X(s) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2s}{s^{2} + \omega_{0}^{2}} = \frac{s}{s^{2} + \omega_{0}^{2}} $$ **Step 6:** Final result. $$ \boxed{\mathcal{L}\{\cos(\omega_{0} t) u(t)\} = \frac{s}{s^{2} + \omega_{0}^{2}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \operatorname{Re}(s) > 0} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Robot Application:** This transform describes the response of an undamped second-order robotic joint to an initial displacement — fundamental for analyzing joint oscillations. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Laplace Transform of $\sin(\omega_{0} t) u(t)$ **Given:** $x(t) = \sin(\omega_{0} t) u(t)$. **Step 1:** Use Euler's identity: $\sin(\omega_{0} t) = \dfrac{e^{j\omega_{0} t} - e^{-j\omega_{0} t}}{2j}$. $$ X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \sin(\omega_{0} t) \, e^{-st} \, dt = \frac{1}{2j} \int_{0}^{\infty} \left(e^{j\omega_{0} t} - e^{-j\omega_{0} t}\right) e^{-st} \, dt $$ **Step 2:** Split and integrate each term. $$ X(s) = \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{1}{s - j\omega_{0}} - \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{1}{s + j\omega_{0}} $$ **Step 3:** Combine over a common denominator. $$ X(s) = \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{(s + j\omega_{0}) - (s - j\omega_{0})}{(s - j\omega_{0})(s + j\omega_{0})} $$ **Step 4:** Simplify numerator and denominator. $$ X(s) = \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{2j\omega_{0}}{s^{2} + \omega_{0}^{2}} = \frac{\omega_{0}}{s^{2} + \omega_{0}^{2}} $$ **Step 5:** Final result. $$ \boxed{\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega_{0} t) u(t)\} = \frac{\omega_{0}}{s^{2} + \omega_{0}^{2}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \operatorname{Re}(s) > 0} $$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Differentiation Property **Statement:** If $X(s) = \mathcal{L}\{x(t)\}$, then $\mathcal{L}\!\left\{\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = sX(s) - x(0^-)$. **Step 1:** Start from the definition. $$ \mathcal{L}\!\left\{\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{dx(t)}{dt} e^{-st} \, dt $$ **Step 2:** Apply integration by parts. Let $u = e^{-st}$, $dv = \dfrac{dx}{dt} dt$. Then $du = -s e^{-st} dt$, $v = x(t)$. $$ \int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du $$ $$ \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{dt} e^{-st} \, dt = \left[ x(t) e^{-st} \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot (-s) e^{-st} \, dt $$ **Step 3:** At $t \to \infty$, for a well-behaved signal, $x(t) e^{-st} \to 0$ inside ROC. $$ = 0 - x(0^-) + s \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} \, dt $$ **Step 4:** Recognize the integral as $X(s)$. $$ = sX(s) - x(0^-) $$ **Step 5:** Final result. $$ \boxed{\mathcal{L}\!\left\{\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = sX(s) - x(0^-)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Matters in Robotics:** The equation $J\ddot{\theta}(t) + b\dot{\theta}(t) + k\theta(t) = \tau(t)$ (robot joint dynamics) becomes $J[s^{2}\Theta(s) - s\theta(0^-) - \dot{\theta}(0^-)] + b[s\Theta(s) - \theta(0^-)] + k\Theta(s) = T(s)$ — a pure algebraic equation in $s$. --- ## 3.6 Worked Example: Inverse Laplace Transform Using Partial Fractions **Problem:** Find the inverse Laplace Transform of $X(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$. **Step 1:** Express $X(s)$ using partial fraction expansion. $$ \frac{1}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s+1} + \frac{C}{s+2} $$ **Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $s(s+1)(s+2)$. $$ 1 = A(s+1)(s+2) + B \cdot s(s+2) + C \cdot s(s+1) $$ **Step 3:** Find $A$ by setting $s = 0$. $$ 1 = A(1)(2) \implies A = \frac{1}{2} $$ **Step 4:** Find $B$ by setting $s = -1$. $$ 1 = B(-1)(1) \implies B = -1 $$ **Step 5:** Find $C$ by setting $s = -2$. $$ 1 = C(-2)(-1) \implies C = \frac{1}{2} $$ **Step 6:** Write the partial fraction expansion. $$ X(s) = \frac{1/2}{s} + \frac{-1}{s+1} + \frac{1/2}{s+2} $$ **Step 7:** Take the inverse Laplace Transform term by term. $$ x(t) = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) u(t) - e^{-t} u(t) + \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) e^{-2t} u(t) $$ **Step 8:** Final result. $$ \boxed{x(t) = \left[ \frac{1}{2} - e^{-t} + \frac{1}{2} e^{-2t} \right] u(t)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Robotics Interpretation:** This is the step response of a third-order system — for example, a robotic manipulator with flexible joints and motor dynamics. The output shows an initial transient ($e^{-t}$ and $e^{-2t}$ terms) settling to a steady-state value of $1/2$. # Module 2: Laplace Transform — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Signal Transformation Pipeline The following block diagram illustrates the complete Laplace Transform pipeline as applied in robotic control systems — from a physical input signal to a useful $s$-domain representation. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["PHYSICAL INPUT
