The **Theorems** and **Inverse Methods** are the operational tools for solving 14-mark problems. # Module 2: Laplace Transform — Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Definition and ROC `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** Define the Laplace Transform of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$. What is the Region of Convergence (ROC)? **Model Answer:** The **Laplace Transform** of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as: $$ X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-st} \, dt $$ where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is a complex variable. The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is the set of all complex values of $s$ for which this integral converges to a finite value. For a right-sided (causal) signal, the ROC is a region in the complex $s$-plane to the **right of the rightmost pole** of $X(s)$. > [!TIP] > **3-mark strategy:** Define the integral, mention the complex variable $s$, and state ROC briefly. Avoid derivations. --- ### Question 2: Initial and Final Value Theorem `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** State the Initial Value Theorem and Final Value Theorem of Laplace Transform. Mention the conditions for the Final Value Theorem to be applicable. **Model Answer:** **Initial Value Theorem (IVT):** $$ x(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s X(s) $$ provided $x(0^+)$ exists (i.e., the signal has no impulses at $t=0$). **Final Value Theorem (FVT):** $$ \lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s X(s) $$ **Conditions for FVT validity:** All poles of $sX(s)$ must lie strictly in the **left half of the s-plane** (i.e., $\operatorname{Re}(\text{pole}) < 0$). If any pole lies on the $j\omega$-axis or in the right half-plane, the FVT is **not applicable**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Part B — Question A (Internal Choice Option 1) #### Question A: Solving Differential Equation using Laplace Transform `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** Solve the following second-order differential equation using Laplace Transform: $$ \frac{d^{2}y(t)}{dt^{2}} + 5 \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + 6 \, y(t) = u(t), \quad y(0^-) = 1, \quad \dot{y}(0^-) = 0 $$ **Model Solution:** **Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]: Apply Laplace Transform and substitute initial conditions.** **Step 1:** Apply the Laplace Transform to both sides of the differential equation. Using the differentiation property $\mathcal{L}\{y'(t)\} = sY(s) - y(0^-)$ and $\mathcal{L}\{y''(t)\} = s^{2}Y(s) - sy(0^-) - y'(0^-)$: $$ \left[ s^{2} Y(s) - s \cdot y(0^-) - \dot{y}(0^-) \right] + 5 \left[ s Y(s) - y(0^-) \right] + 6 Y(s) = \frac{1}{s} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute initial conditions $y(0^-) = 1$ and $\dot{y}(0^-) = 0$: $$ s^{2} Y(s) - s - 0 + 5 s Y(s) - 5 + 6 Y(s) = \frac{1}{s} $$ **Step 3:** Collect the $Y(s)$ terms on the left side and the constants on the right side: $$ (s^{2} + 5s + 6) Y(s) = \frac{1}{s} + s + 5 $$ **Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]: Solve for $Y(s)$ and find inverse Laplace transform.** **Step 4:** Factor the quadratic $s^{2} + 5s + 6 = (s+2)(s+3)$: $$ (s+2)(s+3) Y(s) = \frac{1}{s} + s + 5 = \frac{1 + s^{2} + 5s}{s} $$ **Step 5:** Solve for $Y(s)$: $$ Y(s) = \frac{s^{2} + 5s + 1}{s(s+2)(s+3)} $$ **Step 6:** Apply partial fraction expansion: $$ Y(s) = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s+2} + \frac{C}{s+3} $$ **Step 7:** Find residues. For $A$ (set $s = 0$): $$ A = \frac{0 + 0 + 1}{(0+2)(0+3)} = \frac{1}{6} $$ For $B$ (set $s = -2$): $$ B = \frac{4 - 10 + 1}{(-2)(-2+3)} = \frac{-5}{(-2)(1)} = \frac{5}{2} $$ For $C$ (set $s = -3$): $$ C = \frac{9 - 15 + 1}{(-3)(-3+2)} = \frac{-5}{(-3)(-1)} = \frac{-5}{3} $$ **Step 8:** Write the partial fraction expansion: $$ Y(s) = \frac{1/6}{s} + \frac{5/2}{s+2} + \frac{-5/3}{s+3} $$ **Step 9:** Apply inverse Laplace Transform to each term: $$ y(t) = \frac{1}{6} u(t) + \frac{5}{2} e^{-2t} u(t) - \frac{5}{3} e^{-3t} u(t) $$ **Step 10:** Final answer. $$ \boxed{y(t) = \left[ \frac{1}{6} + \frac{5}{2} e^{-2t} - \frac{5}{3} e^{-3t} \right] u(t)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** At $t = 0^+$, $y(0^+) = 1/6 + 5/2 - 5/3 = 1/6 + 15/6 - 10/6 = 6/6 = 1$. ✓ Matches initial condition. > As $t \to \infty$, $y(t) \to 1/6$ — the steady-state response of the system. This is a **stable** system because all poles ($-2$, $-3$) are in the left half-plane. --- ### Part B — Question B (Internal Choice Option 2) #### Question B: Transfer Function and Stability Analysis `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** A robotic joint control system has the transfer function: $$ H(s) = \frac{2s + 6}{s^{3} + 6s^{2} + 11s + 6} $$ **(a)** Find the poles and zeros of the system. **(b)** Determine whether the system is stable, marginally stable, or unstable. Justify your answer. **Model Solution:** **Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]: Poles and Zeros.** **Step 1:** Find the zeros. Set the numerator to zero: $$ 2s + 6 = 0 \implies s = -3 $$ **Step 2:** Find the poles. Set the denominator to zero and factor: $$ s^{3} + 6s^{2} + 11s + 6 = 0 $$ **Step 3:** Try $s = -1$ as a root (Rational Root Theorem): $$ (-1)^{3} + 6(-1)^{2} + 11(-1) + 6 = -1 + 6 - 11 + 6 = 0 $$ So $s = -1$ is a root. **Step 4:** Perform polynomial division to factor out $(s+1)$: $$ s^{3} + 6s^{2} + 11s + 6 = (s+1)(s^{2} + 5s + 6) $$ **Step 5:** Factor the quadratic: $$ s^{2} + 5s + 6 = (s+2)(s+3) $$ **Step 6:** Final factorization: $$ s^{3} + 6s^{2} + 11s + 6 = (s+1)(s+2)(s+3) $$ **Step 7:** List poles and zeros. $$ \boxed{\text{Zero: } s = -3; \quad \text{Poles: } s = -1, \, s = -2, \, s = -3} $$ **Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]: Stability Analysis.** **Step 8:** Identify the location of each pole in the complex s-plane. All three poles are real and lie on the negative real axis: - $p_{1} = -1$ (left half-plane) - $p_{2} = -2$ (left half-plane) - $p_{3} = -3$ (left half-plane) **Step 9:** Apply the stability criterion. For an LTI system to be **asymptotically stable**, **all poles must have strictly negative real parts** (lie in the left half-plane, LHP). The system is also BIBO stable if and only if the ROC includes the $j\omega$-axis. **Step 10:** Verify the ROC. For a causal system, the ROC extends to the right of the rightmost pole. The rightmost pole is at $s = -1$. Thus, the ROC is $\operatorname{Re}(s) > -1$, which **includes the $j\omega$-axis** ($\operatorname{Re}(s) = 0$). **Step 11:** Final conclusion. $$ \boxed{\text{The system is ASYMPTOTICALLY STABLE (and BIBO stable).}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** If this $H(s)$ represents a robotic arm joint controller, the negative real poles ($s = -1, -2, -3$) correspond to **exponentially decaying modes** in the time response. The joint will smoothly return to its commanded position without oscillation — the ideal behavior for precision robotics. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign errors in factoring:** Students often write $(s-1)$ instead of $(s+1)$ when testing roots — double-check by substitution. > 2. **Forgetting to state the ROC:** A transfer function is incomplete without its ROC. Always state the ROC for full marks. > 3. **Confusing zero and pole cancellation:** Here, both zero and pole exist at $s = -3$ — this is a **pole-zero cancellation**. The system is still stable, but the cancellation reduces the effective system order from 3 to 2. > 4. **Misclassifying marginal stability:** Poles on the imaginary axis ($j\omega$) indicate marginal stability (sustained oscillation), **not** asymptotic stability. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction. > 5. **Missing initial conditions:** When solving ODEs, always substitute $y(0^-)$ and $\dot{y}(0^-)$ correctly into the $sX(s) - x(0^-)$ formula. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Laplace Transform** - **Definition:** $X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$ with $s = \sigma + j\omega$ — **unilateral** form. - **ROC** must always be specified; it excludes poles and is bounded by them. - **Standard pairs to memorize:** $\delta(t) \leftrightarrow 1$, $u(t) \leftrightarrow 1/s$, $e^{-at} \leftrightarrow 1/(s+a)$, $\cos/\sin \leftrightarrow s/(s^{2}+\omega_{0}^{2})$ and $\omega_{0}/(s^{2}+\omega_{0}^{2})$. - **Numerator for cosine is $s$**, not $\omega_{0}$ — common KTU trap. - **Differentiation property** converts ODEs to algebraic equations: $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}\} = sX(s) - x(0^-)$. - **Convolution property** in $s$-domain is **multiplication**: $\mathcal{L}\{x_{1} * x_{2}\} = X_{1}(s) X_{2}(s)$. - **FVT condition:** All poles of $sX(s)$ strictly in LHP, otherwise FVT is invalid. - **IVT requires** that $x(0^+)$ exists (no impulses at origin). - **Stability rule:** All poles in LHP → stable; on $j\omega$-axis → marginal; in RHP → unstable. - **Partial fraction method** is the standard technique for inverse Laplace Transform in KTU exams. - **Initial conditions** must always be included when applying differentiation property — students often forget $x(0^-)$. - **Pole-zero cancellation** may reduce effective system order — check carefully. - **In robotics:** $H(s)$ for a DC motor is $K/[s(Js+b)(Ls+R)]$ — poles at $0$, $-b/J$, $-R/L$ — verify all in LHP for stable operation. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** > 1. Write the defining integral and ROC explicitly. > 2. For ODEs, show **all** 5 steps (Laplace, substitute, solve, partial fractions, inverse). > 3. For stability, **plot poles in the s-plane** with values — examiners reward visual clarity. > 4. Always end with a boxed final answer in time domain.

topics.title: Inverse Laplace Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inverse Laplace Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Inverse Laplace Transform** is the mathematical operator that recovers a time-domain function $f(t)$ (or $x(t)$ in signal processing contexts) from its complex-frequency-domain representation $F(s)$. It is formally defined by the **Bromwich Integral** (also called the Mellin-Fourier inversion formula):

$$
f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \int_{\gamma - j\infty}^{\gamma + j\infty} F(s)\, e^{st}\, ds
$$

where:

- $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is the complex frequency variable.
- $\gamma$ is a real constant chosen such that the vertical contour of integration lies entirely within the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of $F(s)$.
- $t \geq 0$ for unilateral (one-sided) Laplace transforms, which is the standard convention in KTU engineering problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In OERAT831 (Robotics context), $F(s)$ typically represents the **transfer function** of an actuator, sensor, or controller, and $f(t)$ represents the corresponding **time-domain response** (e.g., step response of a robotic arm joint, impulse response of a low-pass filter).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Decoder"

Imagine you are given a perfectly baked cake (the **output response** in the $s$-domain, $F(s)$) and you are asked to reconstruct the **original recipe** (the **time-domain signal**, $f(t)$).

- The **forward Laplace Transform** is like baking: you take simple raw ingredients ($f(t)$ — exponentials, sines, step functions) and combine them into a finished algebraic cake ($F(s)$).
- The **Inverse Laplace Transform** is like being a **culinary detective**: you taste the cake, identify the ingredients algebraically, and write the recipe back down.

For a robotics student, this is what happens when you measure the frequency response of a DC motor with a spectrum analyzer and then mathematically "decode" it back into how the motor shaft actually moves over time.

### 1.3 Existence and Uniqueness

The inverse Laplace transform **exists** if:

1. $F(s)$ is **analytic** (has no poles) on the line $\text{Re}(s) = \gamma$.
2. $F(s)$ has **at most a finite number of isolated singularities** (poles) in any finite region.
3. $F(s)$ does not grow faster than an exponential as $\vert s \vert \rightarrow \infty$.

The inverse transform is **unique** at all points of continuity of $f(t)$. At jump discontinuities, it returns the **average** of the left-hand and right-hand limits (this is the *Dirichlet condition*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Reminder:** No physical constants are intrinsic to the inverse transform itself, but in robotic applications the recovered $f(t)$ is typically measured in **meters (m)** for position, **rad/s** for velocity, or **volts (V)** for voltage, depending on whether $F(s)$ came from a mechanical, rotational, or electrical subsystem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conditions for Existence

For the inverse Laplace transform to exist and be unique, the following conditions must hold (these are board-favorite 3-mark questions):

1. **Piecewise Continuity:** $f(t)$ must be piecewise continuous on every finite interval $[0, T]$.
2. **Exponential Order:** There must exist real constants $M > 0$ and $\alpha$ such that $\vert f(t) \vert \leq M e^{\alpha t}$ for all $t \geq 0$.
3. **Finite Discontinuities:** $f(t)$ must have at most a finite number of discontinuities in any finite interval.

### 2.2 Standard Inverse Transform Pairs (The "Vocabulary" You Must Memorize)

These are the atomic building blocks from which **every** inverse Laplace problem is constructed. KTU examiners expect these to be recalled instantly.

| # | $F(s)$ | $f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\}$ | ROC / Conditions |
|---|--------|-----------------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $u(t)$ (unit step) | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{s^n}$ | $\dfrac{t^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$, $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{s+a}$ | $e^{-at}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{(s+a)^n}$ | $\dfrac{t^{n-1} e^{-at}}{(n-1)!}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\sin(\omega t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 6 | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\cos(\omega t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ | $e^{-at} \sin(\omega t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 8 | $\dfrac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ | $e^{-at} \cos(\omega t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 9 | $\dfrac{1}{s^2 - a^2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{a}\sinh(at)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ |
| 10 | $\dfrac{a}{s^2 - a^2}$ | $\cosh(at)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Warning for Tables:** Notice the ROC column uses `\vert a \vert` (LaTeX absolute value) **without** the literal `|` pipe character, because literal pipes break markdown tables. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside table cells.

### 2.3 The Master Theorems (Properties of Inverse Laplace Transform)

These five properties are the "grammar rules" of the language. Every KTU problem is solved by combining standard pairs (vocabulary) with these theorems (grammar).

#### (A) Linearity Property
$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{a F(s) + b G(s)\} = a\, f(t) + b\, g(t)
$$

for any constants $a, b$. This allows you to break a complex rational function into partial fractions.

#### (B) First Shifting Theorem (s-Domain Shift — *Most Important for KTU*)
$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s - a)\} = e^{at}\, f(t)
$$

**Interpretation:** Shifting the $s$-variable to the **right** by $a$ corresponds to multiplying the time signal by $e^{at}$.

#### (C) Second Shifting Theorem (t-Domain Shift)
$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{e^{-as} F(s)\} = f(t - a)\, u(t - a)
$$

**Interpretation:** Multiplying $F(s)$ by $e^{-as}$ introduces a **time delay** of $a$ seconds. Critical for modeling delays in robotic communication links.

#### (D) Multiplication by $s$ (Differentiation in Time)
$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{s F(s) - f(0)\} = \dfrac{df(t)}{dt}
$$

#### (E) Division by $s$ (Integration in Time)
$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{F(s)}{s}\right\} = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

#### (F) Convolution Theorem (Critical for Robotics LTI Systems)
$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\, G(s)\} = (f * g)(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\, g(t - \tau)\, d\tau
$$

This is the mathematical heart of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system theory**: the output of cascading two systems equals the convolution of their impulse responses.

### 2.4 KTU Real-World Engineering Utility

| Robotics Subsystem | Use of Inverse Laplace |
|---|---|
| **DC Motor Position Control** | Given transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{\theta(s)}{V(s)}$, inverse Laplace gives the angular position trajectory $\theta(t)$. |
| **PID Controller Design** | Inverse Laplace converts controller output $U(s)$ back to actuator voltage $u(t)$. |
| **Sensor Filtering** | Impulse response $h(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{H(s)\}$ characterizes how a low-pass filter smooths noisy encoder signals. |
| **System Stability** | Poles of $F(s)$ (left half-plane) directly determine whether $f(t)$ decays or diverges. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Core Inversion Strategy — "Algorithm for the Student"

Every KTU inverse Laplace problem follows the same five-step algorithm:

1. **Inspect** $F(s)$: Is it already a standard form? If yes, use the table directly.
2. **Complete the square** if $s^2 + \omega^2$ terms appear with linear $s$ offsets (damped sinusoids).
3. **Apply partial fraction decomposition** if $F(s)$ is a rational function $\dfrac{P(s)}{Q(s)}$ with $\deg Q > \deg P$.
4. **Factor the denominator** into linear factors $(s - p_i)$ where $p_i$ are the poles.
5. **Match each partial fraction to the standard table** and use linearity.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Pure Standard Form (Foundation Level)

**Problem:** Find $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{4}{s+3}\right\}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Recognize the standard pair. From row 3 of the table, with $a = 3$:

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+a}\right\} = e^{-at}
$$

Step 2 — Apply the constant multiplier (linearity, property A):

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{4}{s+3}\right\} = 4 \cdot \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+3}\right\}
$$

Step 3 — Substitute $a = 3$ into the standard form:

$$
f(t) = 4\, e^{-3t}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Final Answer:** $f(t) = 4 e^{-3t}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always write "$t \geq 0$" explicitly. It signals to the examiner that you understand the unilateral transform convention and can earn you 0.5–1 extra mark for rigor.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Partial Fraction Decomposition (Most Common KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Find $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{2s + 5}{s^2 + 5s + 6}\right\}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Factor the denominator. Find two numbers that multiply to $6$ and add to $5$: these are $2$ and $3$.

$$
s^2 + 5s + 6 = (s + 2)(s + 3)
$$

So:

$$
F(s) = \dfrac{2s + 5}{(s + 2)(s + 3)}
$$

Step 2 — Set up partial fractions.

$$
\dfrac{2s + 5}{(s + 2)(s + 3)} = \dfrac{A}{s + 2} + \dfrac{B}{s + 3}
$$

Step 3 — Multiply both sides by $(s + 2)(s + 3)$ to clear denominators.

$$
2s + 5 = A(s + 3) + B(s + 2)
$$

Step 4 — Solve for $A$ using the **cover-up method** (substitute $s = -2$):

$$
A = \left.\dfrac{2s + 5}{s + 3}\right|_{s = -2} = \dfrac{2(-2) + 5}{-2 + 3} = \dfrac{-4 + 5}{1} = 1
$$

Step 5 — Solve for $B$ by substituting $s = -3$:

$$
B = \left.\dfrac{2s + 5}{s + 2}\right|_{s = -3} = \dfrac{2(-3) + 5}{-3 + 2} = \dfrac{-6 + 5}{-1} = 1
$$

Step 6 — Verify: $A + B = 2$ and the linear coefficient gives $A(1) + B(1) = 2$. Check matches the numerator's $s$-coefficient, so $A = B = 1$ is correct.

Step 7 — Substitute back and decompose.

$$
F(s) = \dfrac{1}{s + 2} + \dfrac{1}{s + 3}
$$

Step 8 — Apply the inverse transform to each term using linearity.

$$
f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s + 2}\right\} + \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s + 3}\right\}
$$

Step 9 — Use the standard pair $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+a}\right\} = e^{-at}$.

$$
f(t) = e^{-2t} + e^{-3t}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Final Answer:** $f(t) = (e^{-2t} + e^{-3t})\, u(t)$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Completing the Square (Damped Sinusoid)

**Problem:** Find $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{s + 4}{s^2 + 4s + 13}\right\}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Complete the square in the denominator. Take half of $4$, square it, and add/subtract.

$$
s^2 + 4s + 13 = (s^2 + 4s + 4) + 9 = (s + 2)^2 + 3^2
$$

Step 2 — Manipulate the numerator to match row 7 or 8 of the table. We need either $(s+2)$ or a constant in the numerator.

Rewrite the numerator:

$$
s + 4 = (s + 2) + 2
$$

Step 3 — Split into two fractions.

$$
F(s) = \dfrac{s + 2}{(s + 2)^2 + 3^2} + \dfrac{2}{(s + 2)^2 + 3^2}
$$

Step 4 — Normalize the second fraction to match the standard form. Recall that row 7 has $\omega$ in the numerator:

$$
\dfrac{2}{(s + 2)^2 + 3^2} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{3}{(s + 2)^2 + 3^2}
$$

Step 5 — Apply rows 8 and 7 of the table with $a = 2$ and $\omega = 3$.

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{s + 2}{(s + 2)^2 + 3^2}\right\} = e^{-2t} \cos(3t)
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{3}{(s + 2)^2 + 3^2}\right\} = e^{-2t} \sin(3t)
$$

Step 6 — Combine using linearity.

$$
f(t) = e^{-2t} \cos(3t) + \dfrac{2}{3}\, e^{-2t} \sin(3t)
$$

Step 7 — Factor out $e^{-2t}$ for the final compact form.

$$
f(t) = e^{-2t}\left[\cos(3t) + \dfrac{2}{3} \sin(3t)\right], \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Final Answer:** $f(t) = e^{-2t}\left(\cos 3t + \dfrac{2}{3}\sin 3t\right) u(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning for Robotics:** The factor $e^{-2t}$ is the **envelope decay** — it tells you how fast the oscillation dies out. The $\cos(3t)$ term oscillates at $\omega = 3$ rad/s. If $e^{-2t}$ decays slowly, your robot joint is underdamped and will vibrate before settling — a classic KTU 14-mark problem context.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Repeated Roots (Higher-Order Pole)

**Problem:** Find $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2 (s+2)}\right\}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Set up partial fractions for a repeated root.

$$
\dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2 (s+2)} = \dfrac{A}{s+1} + \dfrac{B}{(s+1)^2} + \dfrac{C}{s+2}
$$

Step 2 — Multiply through.

$$
1 = A(s+1)(s+2) + B(s+2) + C(s+1)^2
$$

Step 3 — Solve for $B$ (set $s = -1$).

$$
B = \left.\dfrac{1}{s+2}\right|_{s = -1} = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1
$$

Step 4 — Solve for $C$ (set $s = -2$).

$$
C = \left.\dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2}\right|_{s = -2} = \dfrac{1}{(-1)^2} = 1
$$

Step 5 — Solve for $A$ by substituting any convenient $s$, e.g., $s = 0$.

$$
1 = A(1)(2) + B(2) + C(1)^2 = 2A + 2(1) + 1(1) = 2A + 3
$$

$$
2A = -2 \implies A = -1
$$

Step 6 — Substitute back.

$$
F(s) = \dfrac{-1}{s+1} + \dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2} + \dfrac{1}{s+2}
$$

Step 7 — Apply rows 3 and 4 of the standard table.

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+1}\right\} = e^{-t}
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2}\right\} = t\, e^{-t}
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+2}\right\} = e^{-2t}
$$

Step 8 — Combine.

$$
f(t) = -e^{-t} + t\, e^{-t} + e^{-2t}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Final Answer:** $f(t) = \left[e^{-2t} + (t - 1)e^{-t}\right] u(t)$.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Applying the Second Shifting Theorem

**Problem:** Find $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{e^{-3s}}{s^2 + 4}\right\}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the structure: $e^{-as} \cdot F(s)$ with $a = 3$ and $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2 + 4}$.

Step 2 — Find the inverse of $F(s)$ alone. From row 5 with $\omega = 2$:

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s^2 + 4}\right\} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sin(2t) = f(t)
$$

Step 3 — Apply the second shifting theorem (property C):

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{e^{-3s}}{s^2 + 4}\right\} = f(t - 3)\, u(t - 3)
$$

Step 4 — Write the explicit time-domain expression:

$$
f(t) = \dfrac{1}{2} \sin[2(t - 3)]\, u(t - 3)
$$

**Final Answer:** $f(t) = \dfrac{1}{2} \sin[2(t - 3)]\, u(t - 3)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotics Application:** This result models a robotic command that is **delayed by 3 seconds** — for instance, a signal that arrives at an actuator 3 seconds after it was generated by the controller. The unit step $u(t-3)$ ensures the signal is exactly zero before $t = 3$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Decision Flowchart for Solving Inverse Laplace Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Receive F of s as input"] --> B{"Is F s a standard transform pair?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Apply standard table directly with linearity"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Does F s contain e to minus a s factor?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Apply second shifting theorem introduce u of t minus a"]
    D -- "No" --> F{"Does F s contain s minus a shift in s domain?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Apply first shifting theorem multiply by e to the a t"]
    F -- "No" --> H{"Does denominator have quadratic with linear s?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["Complete the square match damped sine cosine"]
    H -- "No" --> J{"Is F s a proper rational function?"}
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Partial fraction decomposition factor denominator"]
    J -- "No, improper" --> L["Long division split into polynomial plus proper fraction"]
    K --> M["Identify poles cover up method for residues"]
    M --> N["Match each term to standard table"]
    I --> N
    G --> N
    E --> N
    L --> N
    C --> O["Combine all terms using linearity final answer f of t"]
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Inverse Laplace in a Robotic Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_DOMAIN["TIME DOMAIN"]
        A1["Reference command r of t"]
        A2["Sensor noise n of t"]
    end

    subgraph LAPLACE_FORWARD["FORWARD LAPLACE TRANSFORM BLOCK"]
        B1["Laplace of r of t equals R of s"]
        B2["Laplace of n of t equals N of s"]
    end

    subgraph PLANT_DOMAIN["S DOMAIN PLANT MODEL"]
        C1["Controller C of s"]
        C2["Plant Transfer Function G of s"]
        C3["Sensor H of s"]
    end

    subgraph INVERSE_BLOCK["INVERSE LAPLACE BLOCK KTU FOCUS"]
        D1["Compute Y of s equals C of s times G of s times R of s"]
        D2["Inverse Laplace y of t equals L inverse of Y of s"]
        D3["Output trajectory y of t sent to robotic actuator"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_DOMAIN["PHYSICAL ROBOT"]
        E1["Joint angle theta of t in radians"]
        E2["End effector position p of t in meters"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    B1 --> C1 --> C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    D3 --> E2
    B2 --> C3 --> D1
```

### 4.3 Concept Map — Mapping Inverse Laplace to Robotic System Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1["F of s in s domain"] --> G1{"Pole location analysis"}
    G1 --> G2["Pole in left half plane stable decay"]
    G1 --> G3["Pole on imaginary axis marginal stability"]
    G1 --> G4["Pole in right half plane unstable divergence"]

    G2 --> H1["Inverse Laplace gives decaying f of t"]
    G3 --> H2["Inverse Laplace gives sustained oscillation"]
    G4 --> H3["Inverse Laplace gives unbounded growth"]

    H1 --> I1["Robot settles to target steady state"]
    H2 --> I2["Robot oscillates around target constant amplitude"]
    H3 --> I3["Robot overshoots and crashes safety hazard"]

    F1 --> J1["Residue calculation"]
    J1 --> J2["Coefficients of exponentials in f of t"]
    J2 --> K1["Determines initial overshoot percentage"]
    K1 --> I1
    K1 --> I2
    K1 --> I3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Diagram 4.1 should be followed top-to-bottom for every KTU problem. Diagrams 4.2 and 4.3 show that the inverse Laplace is **not just an abstract math operation** — its output literally determines whether a robot behaves safely or crashes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OERAT831]`

**State the condition for the existence of the Inverse Laplace Transform of a function $F(s)$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Inverse Laplace Transform of $F(s)$ exists if the following three conditions are simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Piecewise Continuity:** $f(t)$ must be piecewise continuous on every finite interval $[0, T]$ for $T > 0$. (1 Mark)

2. **Exponential Order:** There must exist real constants $M > 0$ and $\alpha \geq 0$ such that $\vert f(t) \vert \leq M e^{\alpha t}$ for all $t \geq 0$. (1 Mark)

3. **Bounded Growth of $F(s)$:** $F(s)$ must not grow faster than $\dfrac{1}{s}$ as $\vert s \vert \rightarrow \infty$, i.e., $\lim_{\vert s \vert \to \infty} F(s) = 0$ for proper rational functions. (1 Mark)

Under these conditions, the inversion is **unique** at every point of continuity of $f(t)$, and the Bromwich integral converges.

---

#### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OERAT831]`

**Define the First Shifting Theorem of the Inverse Laplace Transform and state its mathematical form.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **First Shifting Theorem** (also called the $s$-domain shifting property) states that a shift in the complex frequency variable $s$ corresponds to an exponential multiplier in the time domain. (1 Mark)

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s - a)\} = e^{at}\, f(t), \quad \text{where } f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\}
$$

(1 Mark)

**Condition:** The shift constant $a$ can be any real or complex number, and the result is valid for $t \geq 0$. (1 Mark)

**Example Application:**

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s^2 + 2s + 5}\right\} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2 + 4}\right\} = \dfrac{1}{2} e^{-t} \sin(2t)
$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question B (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OERAT831]`

**(a)** Find the Inverse Laplace Transform of:

$$
F(s) = \dfrac{3s + 7}{s^2 - 2s - 3}
$$

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Factor the denominator.

$$
s^2 - 2s - 3 = (s - 3)(s + 1)
$$

Step 2 — Set up partial fractions.

$$
\dfrac{3s + 7}{(s - 3)(s + 1)} = \dfrac{A}{s - 3} + \dfrac{B}{s + 1}
$$

Step 3 — Clear denominators.

$$
3s + 7 = A(s + 1) + B(s - 3)
$$

Step 4 — Solve for $A$ by setting $s = 3$.

$$
A = \left.\dfrac{3s + 7}{s + 1}\right|_{s = 3} = \dfrac{9 + 7}{4} = \dfrac{16}{4} = 4
$$

Step 5 — Solve for $B$ by setting $s = -1$.

$$
B = \left.\dfrac{3s + 7}{s - 3}\right|_{s = -1} = \dfrac{-3 + 7}{-4} = \dfrac{4}{-4} = -1
$$

Step 6 — Substitute and decompose.

$$
F(s) = \dfrac{4}{s - 3} - \dfrac{1}{s + 1}
$$

Step 7 — Apply inverse Laplace using row 3 of the standard table with $a = -3$ and $a = 1$.

$$
f(t) = 4 e^{3t} - e^{-t}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Final Answer:** $f(t) = (4 e^{3t} - e^{-t})\, u(t)$. **(7/7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** Using the Inverse Laplace Transform, determine the time response $y(t)$ of a robotic joint whose transfer function relationship in the $s$-domain is:

$$
Y(s) = \dfrac{5}{(s+2)(s^2 + 2s + 5)}
$$

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Complete the square in the second denominator.

$$
s^2 + 2s + 5 = (s + 1)^2 + 2^2
$$

Step 2 — Set up partial fractions with a repeated linear denominator structure.

$$
\dfrac{5}{(s+2)\left[(s+1)^2 + 4\right]} = \dfrac{A}{s+2} + \dfrac{B(s+1) + C}{(s+1)^2 + 4}
$$

Step 3 — Multiply both sides by the full denominator.

$$
5 = A\left[(s+1)^2 + 4\right] + [B(s+1) + C](s+2)
$$

Step 4 — Solve for $A$ by setting $s = -2$.

$$
A = \left.\dfrac{5}{(s+1)^2 + 4}\right|_{s = -2} = \dfrac{5}{(-1)^2 + 4} = \dfrac{5}{5} = 1
$$

Step 5 — Substitute $s = 0$ to obtain one equation.

$$
5 = 1 \cdot \left[1 + 4\right] + [B(1) + C](2) = 5 + 2B + 2C
$$

$$
2B + 2C = 0 \implies B + C = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

Step 6 — Compare coefficients of $s^2$ on both sides. Expanding the left side gives $0 s^2 + 0 s + 5$. The right side $s^2$ coefficient comes from $A(1) + B(1) = 1 + B$. So:

$$
1 + B = 0 \implies B = -1
$$

Step 7 — From equation (i), $C = 1$.

Step 8 — Substitute back.

$$
Y(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+2} + \dfrac{-(s+1) + 1}{(s+1)^2 + 4} = \dfrac{1}{s+2} - \dfrac{s+1}{(s+1)^2 + 4} + \dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2 + 4}
$$

Step 9 — Normalize the last term to match row 7.

$$
\dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2 + 4} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{2}{(s+1)^2 + 4}
$$

Step 10 — Apply inverse Laplace to each piece.

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+2}\right\} = e^{-2t}
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{s+1}{(s+1)^2 + 4}\right\} = e^{-t} \cos(2t)
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{2}{(s+1)^2 + 4}\right\} = e^{-t} \sin(2t)
$$

Step 11 — Combine with linearity.

$$
y(t) = e^{-2t} - e^{-t} \cos(2t) + \dfrac{1}{2} e^{-t} \sin(2t), \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Final Answer:** $y(t) = \left[e^{-2t} - e^{-t}\cos(2t) + \dfrac{1}{2}e^{-t}\sin(2t)\right] u(t)$. **(7/7 Marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize:** In Step 9, students often write $\dfrac{1}{(s+1)^2 + 4}$ directly without extracting the factor of $2$. This gives an incorrect coefficient $\dfrac{1}{4}$ instead of $\dfrac{1}{2}$. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 2. **Sign errors in partial fractions:** In Step 6, when comparing $s^2$ coefficients, students often forget that $A$ contributes to the $s^2$ term too. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 3. **Missing unit step $u(t)$:** In Step 11, omitting the unit step function is technically acceptable but loses a minor style mark. **Penalty: −0.5 mark.**
> 4. **Not writing $t \geq 0$:** Always state the domain. **Penalty: −0.5 mark.**

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OERAT831]`

**(a)** Find the Inverse Laplace Transform of:

$$
F(s) = \dfrac{2s^2 + 6s + 5}{(s+1)(s+2)^2}
$$

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Verify that $\deg(\text{numerator}) = 2$ and $\deg(\text{denominator}) = 3$, so $F(s)$ is a proper rational function (no long division needed).

Step 2 — Set up partial fractions accounting for the repeated root $(s+2)^2$.

$$
\dfrac{2s^2 + 6s + 5}{(s+1)(s+2)^2} = \dfrac{A}{s+1} + \dfrac{B}{s+2} + \dfrac{C}{(s+2)^2}
$$

Step 3 — Multiply through by the full denominator.

$$
2s^2 + 6s + 5 = A(s+2)^2 + B(s+1)(s+2) + C(s+1)
$$

Step 4 — Solve for $A$ by setting $s = -1$.

$$
A = \left.\dfrac{2s^2 + 6s + 5}{(s+2)^2}\right|_{s = -1} = \dfrac{2 - 6 + 5}{1} = 1
$$

Step 5 — Solve for $C$ by setting $s = -2$.

$$
C = \left.\dfrac{2s^2 + 6s + 5}{s+1}\right|_{s = -2} = \dfrac{8 - 12 + 5}{-1} = \dfrac{1}{-1} = -1
$$

Step 6 — Solve for $B$ by comparing $s^2$ coefficients. Expand the right side:

$$
A(s+2)^2 = A(s^2 + 4s + 4)
$$

$$
B(s+1)(s+2) = B(s^2 + 3s + 2)
$$

$$
C(s+1) = Cs + C
$$

The $s^2$ coefficient on the right is $A + B = 1 + B$. This must equal $2$ from the left side. So $B = 1$.

Step 7 — Substitute back.

$$
F(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1} + \dfrac{1}{s+2} - \dfrac{1}{(s+2)^2}
$$

Step 8 — Apply inverse Laplace to each term using rows 3 and 4 of the standard table.

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+1}\right\} = e^{-t}
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+2}\right\} = e^{-2t}
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{(s+2)^2}\right\} = t\, e^{-2t}
$$

Step 9 — Combine.

$$
f(t) = e^{-t} + e^{-2t} - t\, e^{-2t}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Final Answer:** $f(t) = \left[e^{-t} + (1 - t)e^{-2t}\right] u(t)$. **(7/7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** The output of a robotic position-control system in the $s$-domain is given by:

$$
Y(s) = \dfrac{e^{-2s}}{s(s + 4)}
$$

Find the time-domain response $y(t)$ using the Inverse Laplace Transform and the Second Shifting Theorem.

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Decompose the rational part (excluding $e^{-2s}$).

$$
\dfrac{1}{s(s + 4)} = \dfrac{A}{s} + \dfrac{B}{s + 4}
$$

Step 2 — Apply cover-up method.

$$
A = \left.\dfrac{1}{s + 4}\right|_{s = 0} = \dfrac{1}{4}
$$

$$
B = \left.\dfrac{1}{s}\right|_{s = -4} = -\dfrac{1}{4}
$$

Step 3 — Substitute.

$$
\dfrac{1}{s(s+4)} = \dfrac{1/4}{s} - \dfrac{1/4}{s+4} = \dfrac{1}{4}\left[\dfrac{1}{s} - \dfrac{1}{s+4}\right]
$$

Step 4 — Find the inverse of the rational part.

$$
g(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s(s+4)}\right\} = \dfrac{1}{4}\left[1 - e^{-4t}\right]
$$

Step 5 — Apply the Second Shifting Theorem. Since $Y(s) = e^{-2s} \cdot \dfrac{1}{s(s+4)}$, the time-domain response is $g(t)$ delayed by $2$ seconds:

$$
y(t) = g(t - 2)\, u(t - 2)
$$

Step 6 — Write the final explicit form.

$$
y(t) = \dfrac{1}{4}\left[1 - e^{-4(t-2)}\right]\, u(t - 2)
$$

**Final Answer:** $y(t) = \dfrac{1}{4}\left[1 - e^{-4(t-2)}\right] u(t - 2)$. **(7/7 Marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Applying shift theorem to wrong variable:** The shift theorem multiplies $F(s)$ by $e^{-as}$. If a student mistakenly multiplies the time-domain output by $e^{-2t}$ instead of shifting by $2$ seconds, the answer is **fundamentally wrong**. **Penalty: −4 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting the unit step $u(t-2)$:** This is the most common omission. The function is **zero for $t < 2$**, and failing to write $u(t-2)$ loses a mark. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 3. **Argument of exponential error:** Writing $e^{-4t}$ instead of $e^{-4(t-2)}$ inside the bracket. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 4. **Not simplifying the partial fractions first:** Students sometimes apply the second shifting theorem before decomposing $\dfrac{1}{s(s+4)}$. The theorem only works on already-decomposed standard pairs. **Penalty: −2 marks.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Inverse Laplace Transform**

- **Definition:** $f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi j}\int_{\gamma - j\infty}^{\gamma + j\infty} F(s) e^{st}\, ds$ via the Bromwich integral.
- **Existence requires:** piecewise continuity, exponential order, and bounded $F(s)$ as $\vert s \vert \to \infty$.
- **Uniqueness:** guaranteed at all points of continuity of $f(t)$; at jumps, returns the average of left and right limits.
- **Standard Pair #1:** $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+a}\right\} = e^{-at}$ (the single most useful pair).
- **Standard Pair #2:** $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}\right\} = \sin(\omega t)$ and $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}\right\} = \cos(\omega t)$.
- **Standard Pair #3:** $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s^n}\right\} = \dfrac{t^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}$.
- **Linearity:** Always split into a sum of standard pairs — never try to invert a sum as a single unit.
- **First Shifting Theorem:** $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s-a)\} = e^{at} f(t)$ — shifts the pole to the **left**.
- **Second Shifting Theorem:** $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{e^{-as} F(s)\} = f(t-a)\, u(t-a)$ — adds a **time delay** $a$.
- **Convolution Theorem:** $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s) G(s)\} = (f*g)(t)$ — used for cascading LTI robotic subsystems.
- **Repeated Roots:** Always include a $\dfrac{1}{(s+a)^2}$ term with coefficient $B$ **and** a $\dfrac{1}{s+a}$ term with coefficient $A$ when the denominator has $(s+a)^2$.
- **Completing the Square:** Always rewrite $s^2 + bs + c$ as $\left(s + \dfrac{b}{2}\right)^2 + \left(c - \dfrac{b^2}{4}\right)$.
- **Final Answer Format:** Always include $u(t)$ or specify $t \geq 0$ to indicate the unilateral convention.
- **Pole Location Meaning:** Pole in left half-plane → decaying $f(t)$ → **stable**; pole in right half-plane → growing $f(t)$ → **unstable** (safety hazard in robotics).
- **Cover-Up Method:** For simple poles $p_i$, $A_i = \left.(s - p_i) F(s)\right|_{s = p_i}$. Faster than equating coefficients.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Equation Representation of Continuous Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differential Equation Representation of Continuous Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Continuous-Time (CT) System** is a mathematical operator that maps an input continuous-time signal $x(t)$ to an output continuous-time signal $y(t)$ through a transformation governed by a set of physical laws. When the input–output relationship can be expressed in terms of derivatives of the signals with respect to the continuous time variable $t$, the system is said to have a **Differential Equation Representation**.

For a **Linear, Time-Invariant (LTI) Continuous-Time System**, the most general form of the governing equation is a *Linear Constant-Coefficient Differential Equation (LCCDE)* of order $N$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \frac{d^k y(t)}{dt^k} = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \frac{d^k x(t)}{dt^k}$$

where:
- $y(t)$ is the system output (response)
- $x(t)$ is the system input (excitation)
- $a_k$ and $b_k$ are **real, constant coefficients** (parameters of the system)
- $N$ is the **order of the system** (highest derivative of the output)
- $M$ is the highest derivative of the input
- The system is **causal** if $M \le N$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In robotics, the differential equation representation is the bridge between *physical dynamics* (Newton's laws, Kirchhoff's laws, torque equations of motors) and the *signal-processing model* used for simulation, control design, and stability analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a **robotic arm** (the system). You give it a command (the **input** $x(t)$ — for example, a desired joint angle). The arm does not instantly jump to that angle — it accelerates, decelerates, and may even overshoot before settling. The **output** $y(t)$ is the actual joint angle over time.

Why does it behave this way? Because the motor windings have **inductance** (resist change in current), the mechanical load has **inertia** (resist change in velocity), and friction damps motion. Each of these physical "resistances to change" appears mathematically as a **derivative term** in the equation.

Think of the differential equation as a **recipe card**:
- The **left-hand side** describes *what the system naturally wants to do* (its own internal dynamics — inertia, damping, energy storage).
- The **right-hand side** describes *what you are forcing it to do* (the external command).
- Solving the equation = predicting exactly how the robotic arm will respond to your command.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (Robotics) |
|---|---|---|
| Time | $t$ | seconds (**s**) |
| Output (e.g., position) | $y(t)$ | meters (**m**) or radians (**rad**) |
| Input (e.g., voltage) | $x(t)$ | Volts (**V**) |
| Damping coefficient | $a_1$ | N·s/m or N·m·s/rad |
| Stiffness / Spring constant | $a_0$ | N/m or N·m/rad |
| Mass / Inertia | $a_2$ | kg or kg·m² |
| System order | $N$ | dimensionless (integer) |

> [!NOTE]
> A robotic joint with *inertia $J$*, *damping $B$*, and *stiffness $K$* is described by a 2nd-order LCCDE: $J\ddot{\theta}(t) + B\dot{\theta}(t) + K\theta(t) = T(t)$, where $T(t)$ is the applied torque (input). This is the same canonical form used throughout this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Differential Operator $D$

To compactly write a differential equation, we define the **differential operator**:

$$D \triangleq \frac{d}{dt}, \quad D^2 \triangleq \frac{d^2}{dt^2}, \quad \ldots, \quad D^k \triangleq \frac{d^k}{dt^k}$$

The general LCCDE then becomes:

$$Q(D)\,y(t) = P(D)\,x(t)$$

where the operator polynomials are:

$$Q(D) = a_N D^N + a_{N-1} D^{N-1} + \cdots + a_1 D + a_0$$

$$P(D) = b_M D^M + b_{M-1} D^{M-1} + \cdots + b_1 D + b_0$$

> **Why operator notation matters:** It allows us to manipulate the differential equation using the *algebra of polynomials*, which is much simpler than writing derivative symbols everywhere. This is the gateway to the *transfer function* and *Laplace transform* in later modules.

## 2.2 The Characteristic Equation (Auxiliary Equation)

The **Characteristic Equation** of the system is obtained by setting the input $x(t) = 0$ and replacing the operator $D$ with a complex variable $\lambda$:

$$Q(\lambda) = a_N \lambda^N + a_{N-1} \lambda^{N-1} + \cdots + a_1 \lambda + a_0 = 0$$

The $N$ roots $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_N$ of this equation are called the **characteristic roots** or **natural modes** of the system. They determine the **natural (homogeneous) response** of the system.

## 2.3 Complete Response = Natural Response + Forced Response

The total output of the system is the sum of two parts:

$$y(t) = y_h(t) + y_p(t)$$

| Component | Name | Origin | Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| $y_h(t)$ | **Homogeneous / Natural response** | Solution of $Q(D)\,y_h(t) = 0$ | Sum of exponentials: $\sum C_i e^{\lambda_i t}$ |
| $y_p(t)$ | **Particular / Forced response** | A specific solution of the full equation | Has the same *form* as the input $x(t)$ |

### Natural Response (Homogeneous Solution)

For distinct real roots $\lambda_i$:

$$y_h(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i e^{\lambda_i t}$$

For repeated roots (multiplicity $m$):

$$y_h(t) = (C_1 + C_2 t + C_3 t^2 + \cdots + C_m t^{m-1}) e^{\lambda t}$$

For complex conjugate roots $\lambda = \alpha \pm j\beta$:

$$e^{\alpha t}\left[ A \cos(\beta t) + B \sin(\beta t) \right]$$

### Forced Response (Particular Solution)

The form of $y_p(t)$ is *guessed* from the input $x(t)$:

| Input $x(t)$ | Trial form of $y_p(t)$ |
|---|---|
| Constant $K$ | Constant $A$ |
| $e^{at}$ | $A e^{at}$ |
| $\cos(\omega t)$ or $\sin(\omega t)$ | $A\cos(\omega t) + B\sin(\omega t)$ |
| Polynomial $t^n$ | Polynomial of degree $n$ |

## 2.4 Stability Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion (Asymptotic / BIBO):** A causal LTI CT system is **stable** if and only if **every characteristic root has a strictly negative real part**, i.e., $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for all $i$.

| Root location | Natural response behaviour | System status |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ | Decays to zero as $t \to \infty$ | **Stable** |
| $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) = 0$ (simple) | Sustained oscillation / constant | **Marginally stable** |
| $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) > 0$ | Grows unbounded | **Unstable** |

In a **robot arm**, instability means the joint angle grows without bound — the arm swings violently and may self-destruct. Hence, every robotic controller must ensure all characteristic roots stay in the left half-plane.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Robotics

1. **DC Motor Modelling:** The armature circuit + rotating shaft yields a 2nd-order LCCDE linking applied voltage to shaft angle.
2. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) Dynamics:** Sensor bias and drift are captured by 1st/2nd-order differential equations.
3. **Legged Locomotion:** The contact dynamics between foot and ground are described by impulsive differential-equation models.
4. **PID Controller Realisation:** Each PID block corresponds to differential and integral operators acting on the error signal.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | General $N$th-order LCCDE | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k D^k y(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k D^k x(t)$ |
| 2 | Operator form | $Q(D) y(t) = P(D) x(t)$ |
| 3 | Characteristic equation | $Q(\lambda) = a_N \lambda^N + \cdots + a_1 \lambda + a_0 = 0$ |
| 4 | Natural response (distinct real roots) | $y_h(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i e^{\lambda_i t}$ |
| 5 | Natural response (complex pair $\alpha \pm j\beta$) | $e^{\alpha t}[A\cos\beta t + B\sin\beta t]$ |
| 6 | Complete response | $y(t) = y_h(t) + y_p(t)$ |
| 7 | 1st-order canonical form | $\tau \dot{y}(t) + y(t) = K x(t)$ |
| 8 | 2nd-order canonical form | $\ddot{y} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{y} + \omega_n^2 y = \omega_n^2 K x(t)$ |
| 9 | Damping ratio (2nd order) | $\zeta = \dfrac{a_1}{2\sqrt{a_0 a_2}}$ |
| 10 | Natural frequency (2nd order) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{a_0 / a_2}$ |
| 11 | Stability condition | $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0 \quad \forall i$ |
| 12 | Order of system | $N$ = highest derivative of output |
| 13 | Time constant (1st order) | $\tau = a_1 / a_0$ |
| 14 | DC gain | $K = b_0 / a_0$ (at steady state, for constant input) |
| 15 | DC motor DE | $J\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} = K_t i,\; L \frac{di}{dt} + Ri = V - K_e\dot{\theta}$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — 1st-Order Robotic Sensor System

A temperature sensor in a robot is modelled as a 1st-order LCCDE. We solve it **completely** (homogeneous + particular) below.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A robotic thermal sensor obeys:

$$2 \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + 4 y(t) = x(t), \quad y(0) = 0$$

with a step input $x(t) = 10\, u(t)$ (a sudden 10 °C temperature jump). Find $y(t)$ for $t \ge 0$.

### 3.1.2 Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Put the equation in standard form** (coefficient of the highest derivative = 1):

$$\frac{dy}{dt} + 2\, y(t) = 0.5\, x(t)$$

Here $N = 1$, $a_1 = 2$, $a_0 = 4$, $b_0 = 1$.

**Step 2: Write in operator form** $Q(D) y = P(D) x$:

$$(2D + 4)\, y(t) = x(t)$$

So $Q(D) = 2D + 4$, $P(D) = 1$.

**Step 3: Find the characteristic equation** by setting $Q(\lambda) = 0$:

$$2\lambda + 4 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda = -2$$

> The natural mode is $e^{-2t}$, which **decays** — the sensor is **stable**.

**Step 4: Write the general homogeneous solution**:

$$y_h(t) = C_1 e^{\lambda_1 t} = C_1 e^{-2t}$$

**Step 5: Guess the particular solution** for the constant input $x(t) = 10\, u(t)$:

Try $y_p(t) = A$ (a constant). Substitute into the original equation:

$$2 \cdot \frac{dA}{dt} + 4 A = 10$$

The derivative of a constant is zero, so:

$$4A = 10 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A = 2.5$$

Thus $y_p(t) = 2.5$.

**Step 6: Form the complete (general) solution**:

$$y(t) = y_h(t) + y_p(t) = C_1 e^{-2t} + 2.5, \quad t \ge 0$$

**Step 7: Apply the initial condition** $y(0) = 0$:

$$0 = C_1 e^{0} + 2.5 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_1 = -2.5$$

**Step 8: Final closed-form answer**:

$$\boxed{\,y(t) = 2.5 \left(1 - e^{-2t}\right), \quad t \ge 0\,}$$

**Step 9: Verification (sanity check)**:
- At $t = 0$: $y(0) = 2.5(1 - 1) = 0$ ✓ (matches initial condition)
- At $t \to \infty$: $y(\infty) = 2.5$ ✓ (steady-state value, equal to DC gain $\times$ input = $\tfrac{1}{4}\cdot 10 = 2.5$)
- Time constant: $\tau = \tfrac{1}{2} = 0.5\,\text{s}$, so $y$ reaches ~63 % of 2.5 (= 1.575) at $t = 0.5\,\text{s}$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — 2nd-Order Robotic Joint (Canonical Form)

A robotic joint with inertia $J = 1\,\text{kg·m}^2$, damping $B = 4\,\text{N·m·s/rad}$, and stiffness $K = 3\,\text{N·m/rad}$ has torque input $T(t)$. Find the response to a unit step torque $T(t) = u(t)$, given $\theta(0) = 0,\ \dot{\theta}(0) = 0$.

### 3.2.1 The Governing Equation

Newton's 2nd law for rotation:

$$J \ddot{\theta}(t) + B \dot{\theta}(t) + K \theta(t) = T(t)$$

Substituting the values:

$$\ddot{\theta}(t) + 4 \dot{\theta}(t) + 3 \theta(t) = u(t)$$

### 3.2.2 Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Characteristic equation** — set $T = 0$ and replace $D$ with $\lambda$:

$$\lambda^2 + 4\lambda + 3 = 0$$

**Step 2: Factor the polynomial**:

$$(\lambda + 1)(\lambda + 3) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda_1 = -1,\ \lambda_2 = -3$$

Both real, distinct, and negative ⇒ **overdamped, stable** system.

**Step 3: Homogeneous solution**:

$$\theta_h(t) = C_1 e^{-t} + C_2 e^{-3t}$$

**Step 4: Guess the particular solution** for $T = 1$ (constant): try $\theta_p(t) = A$:

$$0 + 0 + 3A = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A = \tfrac{1}{3}$$

So $\theta_p(t) = \tfrac{1}{3}$.

**Step 5: Complete general solution**:

$$\theta(t) = C_1 e^{-t} + C_2 e^{-3t} + \tfrac{1}{3}$$

**Step 6: Apply the two initial conditions**:

Condition (i): $\theta(0) = 0$:

$$C_1 + C_2 + \tfrac{1}{3} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_1 + C_2 = -\tfrac{1}{3}$$

Condition (ii): $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$. Differentiate $\theta(t)$:

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = -C_1 e^{-t} - 3C_2 e^{-3t}$$

At $t = 0$:

$$-C_1 - 3C_2 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_1 = -3 C_2$$

**Step 7: Solve the simultaneous equations**:

Substitute $C_1 = -3 C_2$ into $C_1 + C_2 = -\tfrac{1}{3}$:

$$-3 C_2 + C_2 = -\tfrac{1}{3} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad -2 C_2 = -\tfrac{1}{3} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_2 = \tfrac{1}{6}$$

Then $C_1 = -3 \cdot \tfrac{1}{6} = -\tfrac{1}{2}$.

**Step 8: Final answer**:

$$\boxed{\,\theta(t) = -\tfrac{1}{2} e^{-t} + \tfrac{1}{6} e^{-3t} + \tfrac{1}{3},\quad t \ge 0\,}$$

**Step 9: Verification**:
- $\theta(0) = -\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{3} = 0$ ✓
- $\dot{\theta}(0) = \tfrac{1}{2} - \tfrac{1}{2} = 0$ ✓
- $\theta(\infty) = \tfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$ rad ✓ (DC gain $\times$ step = $\tfrac{1}{3}\cdot 1$)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Root Location on 2nd-Order System

Given $a_2 \ddot{y} + a_1 \dot{y} + a_0 y = b_0 x$ with characteristic roots $\lambda_{1,2} = -\zeta \omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$:

| Case | Damping $\zeta$ | Roots | Natural response $y_h(t)$ | Behaviour | Robotics implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Overdamped | $\zeta > 1$ | Two distinct real negatives | $C_1 e^{\lambda_1 t} + C_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}$ | Slow, no oscillation | Heavily damped joint — sluggish |
| Critically damped | $\zeta = 1$ | Repeated real negative | $(C_1 + C_2 t) e^{-\omega_n t}$ | Fastest non-oscillatory | Ideal step response for a joint |
| Underdamped | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Complex conjugates, $\text{Re}<0$ | $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}(A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t)$ | Decaying oscillation | Compliant joint — useful for force control |
| Undamped | $\zeta = 0$ | Pure imaginary $\pm j\omega_n$ | $A\cos\omega_n t + B\sin\omega_n t$ | Sustained oscillation | **Marginal** — never settles |
| Unstable | $\zeta < 0$ | Roots with $\text{Re} > 0$ | Exponentially growing | Runaway | Robot crashes — must be avoided |

where the **damped natural frequency** is $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CT System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the signal flow from the physical input through the differential-equation-governed system to the output, including the conceptual separation of *natural* and *forced* response components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Signal x(t)"] --> B["Apply Differential Equation Q(D) y = P(D) x"]
    B --> C{"Solve LCCDE"}
    C --> D["Homogeneous Solution y_h(t) from Q(D) y_h = 0"]
    C --> E["Particular Solution y_p(t) from P(D) x substitution"]
    D --> F["Combine y(t) = y_h(t) + y_p(t)"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Apply Initial Conditions to find C_1 ... C_N"]
    G --> H["Final Output y(t)"]
    H --> I{"Check Stability: Re of roots less than 0?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Stable CT System - bounded response"]
    I -- No --> K["Unstable - robotic hazard"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a 2nd-Order Robotic Joint (Direct Form I Realisation)

This is the canonical **block-diagram realisation** of the LCCDE
$\ddot{\theta} + 4\dot{\theta} + 3\theta = T$ using integrators.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Torque T(t)"] --> B["Sum Junction"]
    B --> C["Integrator 1: gives theta dot"]
    C --> D["Integrator 2: gives theta"]
    D --> E["Gain x -4: damping feedback"]
    E --> B
    D --> F["Gain x -3: stiffness feedback"]
    F --> B
    D --> G["Output theta(t)"]
```

## 4.3 Robot Joint Dynamics — Physical-to-Mathematical Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Physical Component"] --> B["Differential Equation Term"]
    A1["Inertia J"] --> B1["J times theta double dot"]
    A2["Viscous Damping B"] --> B2["B times theta dot"]
    A3["Joint Stiffness K"] --> B3["K times theta"]
    A4["Applied Torque T(t)"] --> B4["T(t) on RHS"]
    B1 --> C["Combined LCCDE: J theta double dot + B theta dot + K theta = T(t)"]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D["Characteristic Equation: J lambda squared + B lambda + K = 0"]
    D --> E["Roots lambda_1, lambda_2 decide joint behaviour"]
```

## 4.4 Pole-Zero Style View (Conceptual Locus)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Complex lambda-plane"] --> B["Re of lambda less than 0: Stable region"]
    A --> C["Re of lambda greater than 0: Unstable region"]
    A --> D["Imaginary axis: Marginally stable"]
    B --> B1["Root at -1 + j0: stable mode"]
    B --> B2["Root at -3 + j0: stable mode"]
    D --> D1["Root at j omega: sustained oscillation"]
    C --> C1["Root at +2 + j0: runaway growth"]
```
