```mermaid
    G --> H[Reconstructed Signal x hat t]

    classDef analog fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    classDef digital fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#000

    class A,B,G,H analog
    class C,D,E,F process
    class E digital
```

### 4.2 Frequency-Domain Spectrum Replication Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DOMAIN[Frequency Domain Behavior]
        A1[Original Spectrum X of f Bandlimited to fm] --> A2[Sampled Spectrum Xs of f]
        A2 --> A3[Replica at minus fs]
        A2 --> A4[Replica at zero baseband]
        A2 --> A5[Replica at plus fs]
    end

    A2 --> B{Sampling Condition}
    B -- fs greater than 2fm --> C1[No Overlap Guard Band Exists]
    B -- fs less than 2fm --> C2[Overlap Aliasing Occurs]

    C1 --> D1[Reconstruction via Ideal LPF Sinc Interpolator]
    C2 --> D2[Information Loss Irreversible]

    classDef ok fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef err fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef base fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000

    class A1,A4 base
    class A3,A5 base
    class C1,D1 ok
    class C2,D2 err
```

### 4.3 Sampling Theorem Decision Flowchart (Engineering Decision Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Identify Maximum Signal Frequency fm] --> Q1{Is Source Bandlimited}
    Q1 -- No --> A1[Apply Anti Aliasing LPF to Enforce Bandlimit]
    Q1 -- Yes --> A2[Proceed to Rate Selection]
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> Q2{Select Sampling Rate fs}
    Q2 -- Conservative fs equals 4fm to 10fm --> R1[Oversampling High SNR Robust]
    Q2 -- Exact fs equals 2fm --> R2[Theoretical Nyquist Minimum]
    Q2 -- Risky fs less than 2fm --> R3[ALIASING Signal Distorted]

    R1 --> OUT[Pass to ADC and DSP]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> WARN[Increase fs or Add LPF]

    classDef safe fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
    classDef danger fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000

    class R1,R2,OUT safe
    class R3,WARN danger
    class A1,A2,Q2 warn
```

### 4.4 Sample-and-Hold Timing Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T[Time Domain Behavior]
        I[Continuous x of t] --> S1[Sample at t equals nTs]
        S1 --> S2[Hold Value Constant for One Period]
        S2 --> S3[Output x held t = Staircase Waveform]
    end

    S3 --> N[Quantize and Encode in ADC]
    N --> O[Digital Sample x of n]

    classDef analog fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef digital fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
    classDef hold fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666666,color:#000

    class I,S1,S2,S3 hold
    class N,O digital
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagrams:** Diagram 4.1 shows the **full pipeline** from analog sensor input to reconstructed analog output (critical for closed-loop robotic control). Diagram 4.2 illustrates **why the Nyquist criterion matters** — overlapping spectral copies cause permanent data loss. Diagram 4.3 is a **design-decision flowchart** for choosing $f_s$ in real robotics projects. Diagram 4.4 shows the **practical realization** of a sample-and-hold circuit (used inside every ADC).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern, 2 Marks Conceptual + 1 Mark Diagram/Justification]**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**State and explain the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem. What is the Nyquist rate for a signal with maximum frequency $f_m = 5\,\text{kHz}$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement:** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with no frequency components above $f_m$ Hz can be **completely reconstructed** from its discrete samples $x(nT_s)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

> **Condition:** The signal must be strictly bandlimited (no energy above $f_m$), and the replicas in the spectrum must not overlap.

> **Numerical computation:**

$$f_N = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5\,\text{kHz} = 10\,\text{kHz}$$

> Hence, the **Nyquist rate is $10\,\text{kHz}$**, and the corresponding sampling period is $T_s = 1/f_N = 100\,\mu\text{s}$.

**[Mark distribution: 1 — Theorem statement, 1 — Condition/importance, 1 — Numerical calculation]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]**
**What is aliasing? How can it be prevented in a robotic audio acquisition system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a signal, when sampled at an insufficient rate ($f_s < 2f_m$), "fold back" into the lower frequency band and become indistinguishable from genuine low-frequency content.

> **Prevention methods (any two for full marks):**
> 1. **Anti-aliasing low-pass filter (LPF)** placed before the ADC, with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$.
> 2. **Increase the sampling rate** above $2f_m$ (oversampling, e.g., $f_s = 4f_m$ to $10f_m$).
> 3. **Bandlimit the input signal** at the source (mechanical, optical, or acoustic design).

> In a **robotic audio system**, aliasing manifests as the robot misinterpreting ultrasonic interference as voice commands — a critical safety hazard that the anti-aliasing filter prevents.

**[Mark distribution: 1 — Definition, 1 — Two prevention methods, 1 — Robotics context]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Sampling Theorem & Mathematical Derivation*

**[KTU University Exam — ESE Pattern, Module 3, 14-Mark Full Question]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** State and prove the sampling theorem. Derive the expression for the spectrum of a sampled signal. Show with a neat sketch how aliasing occurs when $f_s < 2f_m$.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A continuous-time signal $x(t) = 5\cos(200\pi t) + 3\cos(400\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 300\,\text{Hz}$.
> (i) Determine if aliasing occurs.
> (ii) Find the discrete-time signal $x[n]$.
> (iii) Determine the frequencies that will be observed after sampling.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Statement (2 marks):** As in Q1 above: A bandlimited signal with $f_m$ can be reconstructed from its samples if $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $x(t)$ be the bandlimited input and $\delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n} \delta(t - nT_s)$ be the impulse train.

The sampled signal is:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$

Taking the Fourier transform and using the convolution property:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)$$

If $f_s \geq 2f_m$, the spectral copies do not overlap, and an ideal LPF of bandwidth $f_s/2$ can extract $X(f)$ exactly, enabling reconstruction.

**Sketch (1 mark):** Show baseband spectrum $\vert f \vert \leq f_m$, and its replicas at $\pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$ For $f_s \geq 2f_m$, gaps exist. For $f_s < 2f_m$, adjacent replicas overlap, causing **irrecoverable distortion (aliasing)**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) [2 marks] Maximum signal frequency:**
The two tones have frequencies $f_1 = 100\,\text{Hz}$ and $f_2 = 200\,\text{Hz}$. So $f_m = 200\,\text{Hz}$.

Nyquist rate $= 2 f_m = 400\,\text{Hz}$.

Since $f_s = 300\,\text{Hz} < 400\,\text{Hz}$, **aliasing occurs**.

**(ii) [2 marks] Discrete-time signal:**

With $T_s = 1/300\,\text{s}$, we get:

$$x[n] = x(nT_s) = 5\cos\!\left(200\pi \cdot \frac{n}{300}\right) + 3\cos\!\left(400\pi \cdot \frac{n}{300}\right)$$

$$x[n] = 5\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{3}\right) + 3\cos\!\left(\frac{4\pi n}{3}\right)$$

**(iii) [3 marks] Observed frequencies (aliasing computation):**

For $f_1 = 100\,\text{Hz}$:
$k_1 = \text{round}(f_1 / f_s) = \text{round}(100/300) = 0$
$f_{a1} = \vert 100 - 0 \times 300 \vert = 100\,\text{Hz}$ (no aliasing, since $f_1 < f_s/2 = 150$).

For $f_2 = 200\,\text{Hz}$:
$k_2 = \text{round}(f_2 / f_s) = \text{round}(200/300) = 1$
$f_{a2} = \vert 200 - 1 \times 300 \vert = \vert -100 \vert = 100\,\text{Hz}$

**Result:** Both $100\,\text{Hz}$ and $200\,\text{Hz}$ tones alias to the **same observed frequency of $100\,\text{Hz}$**, making them indistinguishable — a textbook aliasing failure.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Reconstruction, Sample-and-Hold, and Robotics Application*

**[KTU University Exam — ESE Pattern, Module 3, Alternative Choice]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the natural sampling and flat-top sampling processes. Derive the mathematical expression for a flat-top sampled signal in the frequency domain, and explain the **aperture effect**.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the complete process of **signal reconstruction** from its samples. Why is a low-pass filter mandatory? A robot's joint encoder outputs a position signal with $f_m = 250\,\text{Hz}$. Design a sampling system: specify $f_s$, $T_s$, and justify your choice of anti-aliasing filter cutoff.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Natural Sampling (2 marks):** The signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by a finite-width rectangular pulse train $p(t)$ of pulse width $\tau < T_s$. Output: $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$. Each pulse "chops" a portion of the signal, retaining its natural curvature. Used in PAM communication systems.

**Flat-Top Sampling (Sample-and-Hold) (2 marks):** Each sample value is **held constant** for the entire sampling period $T_s$ using a capacitor. Output: a **staircase waveform**. This is the method used inside every modern ADC.

**Mathematical Derivation (2 marks):** Flat-top sampling is modeled as:
$$x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot h(t - nT_s)$$
where $h(t)$ is a rectangular pulse of width $T_s$ and unit height. In the frequency domain:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} H(f) \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)$$

**Aperture Effect (1 mark):** The factor $H(f) = T_s\,\text{sinc}(fT_s) e^{-j\pi f T_s}$ multiplies the reconstructed spectrum, causing **high-frequency attenuation** (a $\text{sinc}$-shaped droop). The wider the pulse, the greater the distortion. This is the **aperture effect**, compensated by an equalization filter in high-fidelity ADCs.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Reconstruction Pipeline (3 marks):** Digital samples → DAC (producing staircase waveform) → **Low-pass reconstruction filter** (smooths staircase back to continuous analog) → Output $x(t)$.

**Why LPF is mandatory (2 marks):** The output of the DAC contains the baseband spectrum $X(f)$ **plus** all the higher-frequency replicas centered at $kf_s$. Without the LPF, these replicas would pass through and contaminate downstream analog stages. The LPF removes all energy above $f_s/2$, leaving only the desired baseband signal.

**Design Problem (2 marks):** Given $f_m = 250\,\text{Hz}$:

$$f_s = 4 f_m = 4 \times 250 = 1\,\text{kHz} \quad (\text{oversampled for safety, OSR} = 2)$$
$$T_s = 1/f_s = 1\,\text{ms}$$

**Anti-aliasing filter cutoff** $f_c$ should be slightly below $f_s/2 = 500\,\text{Hz}$ — a 4th-order Butterworth LPF with $f_c = 480\,\text{Hz}$ is a typical industrial choice (steep roll-off prevents aliasing of noise near the Nyquist edge).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Nyquist Rate with Nyquist Frequency:** $f_N = 2f_m$ is the **rate**; $f_s/2 = f_m$ is the **frequency**. Marks deducted if swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter:** Examiners will deduct **1 to 2 marks** in 14-mark questions if you don't mention that real signals are not perfectly bandlimited.
> 3. **Aliasing calculation error:** Always use $k = \text{round}(f/f_s)$, not $k = \lfloor f/f_s \rfloor$. Using the wrong rounding function shifts the aliased frequency.
> 4. **Units:** Forgetting to state Hz or seconds — silent mark loss in numerical problems.
> 5. **Confusing natural and flat-top sampling:** Examiners specifically test this — natural retains the curve, flat-top holds constant. Draw the waveforms if asked.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Sampling" Module 3**

- ✅ **Sampling** converts continuous $x(t)$ to discrete $x[n] = x(nT_s)$.
- ✅ **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ is the necessary and sufficient condition for perfect reconstruction.
- ✅ **Nyquist Rate** $= 2 f_m$ (minimum sampling rate). **Nyquist Frequency** $= f_s/2$ (maximum recoverable frequency).
- ✅ **Time-domain model:** $x_s(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$.
- ✅ **Frequency-domain model:** $X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_k X(f - kf_s)$ — **spectrum is replicated** at multiples of $f_s$.
- ✅ **Aliasing** occurs when $f_s < 2 f_m$; it is **irreversible** and is prevented by an **anti-aliasing LPF** with cutoff at $f_s/2$.
- ✅ **Natural sampling** retains signal curvature; **flat-top sampling** holds each sample constant (used in ADCs).
- ✅ **Aperture effect** in flat-top sampling causes a $\text{sinc}(fT_s)$ droop, corrected by an equalizer.
- ✅ **Reconstruction** is done with an **ideal LPF (sinc interpolator)** or practically via a **zero-order hold (ZOH)** + smoothing filter.
- ✅ **Whittaker-Shannon formula:** $x(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}(\pi(t - nT_s)/T_s)$.
- ✅ **Robotics context:** IMU sampling $\geq 100\,\text{Hz}$, audio at $44.1\,\text{kHz}$, encoder/control loops at $1$–$10\,\text{kHz}$. Always oversample for safety and use anti-aliasing filters at every ADC input.
- ✅ **Design rule of thumb:** $f_s = 4 f_m$ to $10 f_m$ for practical robotic systems (over-sampling factor of 2 to 5 above the theoretical minimum).
- ✅ **Killer formula to memorize:** $T_s = 1/f_s$, $f_N = 2 f_m$, $N = T \cdot f_s$, $f_a = \vert f - kf_s \vert$ with $k = \text{round}(f/f_s)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aliasing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aliasing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in signal processing where a continuous-time (analog) signal is misrepresented upon sampling, such that two or more distinct analog signals become **indistinguishable** (aliases of one another) when reconstructed from their discrete samples. Mathematically, aliasing occurs when a signal contains frequency components at or above **half the sampling frequency** ($f_s / 2$), known as the **Nyquist frequency** ($f_N$).

In the context of robotics, aliasing is encountered whenever analog sensor data (e.g., LIDAR, sonar, encoder pulses, vision frames, IMU outputs) is converted into digital form via an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**. If the sampling rate is insufficient, high-frequency information folds back into lower frequency bands, corrupting perception, navigation, and control.

**KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem states that a band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples **if and only if**:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

The minimum permissible sampling rate, $f_s = 2 f_{max}$, is called the **Nyquist Rate** ($f_{Nyquist}$), and $f_{max} = f_s / 2$ is the **Nyquist Frequency** ($f_N$).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Spinning Wheel Illusion**
> Imagine watching a spinning wagon wheel in a Western movie. As the wheel rotates faster, the spokes appear to slow down, stop, and even rotate **backward**. Why? Because your eyes (and the camera's shutter) sample the motion at discrete intervals (e.g., 24 frames per second). When the spoke's rotation speed exceeds half your sampling rate, your brain receives ambiguous frames and "guesses" incorrectly — creating an alias.
>
> **A Robotic Analogy:** Consider a robot's ultrasonic sensor sampling distance at 10 Hz. If the robot's arm vibrates at 8 Hz due to motor resonance, the sensor will misinterpret the vibration as a slow, fictitious motion of nearby objects — a "ghost" measurement. This is aliasing causing a **false perception** that can destabilize control loops.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (KTU Standard Notation)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Typical Robotics Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $f_s$ | Sampling Frequency | **Hz** (samples/sec) | $100$ – $10000$ Hz |
| $f_{Nyquist}$ | Nyquist Rate | **Hz** | $2 \cdot f_{max}$ |
| $f_N$ | Nyquist Frequency | **Hz** | $f_s / 2$ |
| $T_s$ | Sampling Period | **seconds (s)** | $1 / f_s$ |
| $f_{max}$ | Signal Bandwidth | **Hz** | Sensor-dependent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for Robotics:** A sampling frequency **lower** than $2 f_{max}$ **always** produces aliasing. There is no digital trick that can remove aliasing once it has occurred — it must be prevented **before** sampling using a low-pass **anti-aliasing filter**.

---

## 1.4 The Three Faces of Aliasing in Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **Spatial Aliasing:** Occurs in vision and LIDAR point clouds (e.g., Moiré patterns on striped surfaces).
> **Temporal Aliasing:** Occurs in time-series sensor data (e.g., encoder readings, IMU drift).
> **Spectral Aliasing:** Occurs in the frequency domain when Fourier components overlap after sampling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Mathematical Origin of Aliasing

When a continuous signal $x_c(t)$ is sampled by an ideal **impulse train sampler** at intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$, the resulting discrete signal is:

$$x_s(t) = x_c(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)$$

In the frequency domain, this multiplication becomes a **periodic replication** of the signal's spectrum at every integer multiple of $f_s$:

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_c(f - k f_s)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Aliasing Mechanism:** If $X_c(f)$ is non-zero for $\vert f \vert > f_s / 2$, the spectral replicas **overlap**. This overlap is aliasing. The overlapping region corrupts the baseband, and the original information is irrecoverable.

---

## 2.2 The Frequency Folding Theorem (KTU High-Yield Result)

> [!NOTE]
> **Folding Rule:** Any input frequency $f_{in}$ outside the Nyquist band $[-f_N, f_N]$ appears in the sampled output as an **aliased frequency** $f_{alias}$ computed as:

$$f_{alias} = \left\vert f_{in} - m \cdot f_s \right\vert \quad \text{where } m = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{f_{in}}{f_s}\right)$$

Equivalently, using the **modulo-fold** form:

$$f_{alias} = f_N - \left(f_{in} \bmod f_s - f_N\right) \quad \text{if } f_{in} \bmod f_s > f_N$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision before KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Determining minimum ADC rate |
| 2 | Nyquist Frequency | $f_N = f_s / 2$ | Highest alias-free frequency |
| 3 | Nyquist Rate | $f_{Nyq} = 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate |
| 4 | Sampling Period | $T_s = 1 / f_s$ | Time between samples |
| 5 | Folding Formula | $f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - m f_s \vert$ | Computing perceived frequency |
| 6 | Reconstruction Condition | $X_c(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_N$ | Band-limited assumption |
| 7 | Anti-Alias Filter Cutoff | $f_c \leq f_N = f_s / 2$ | Hardware pre-filter design |
| 8 | Oversampling Ratio (OSR) | $OSR = f_s / (2 f_{max})$ | Quantifies "safety margin" |
| 9 | Anti-Alias Filter Order | Use Butterworth / Chebyshev | Smooth roll-off selection |
| 10 | Reconstructed Signal (Ideal) | $x_r(t) = \sum x[n] \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t-nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ | Shannon–Whittaker interpolation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** An **Oversampling Ratio (OSR)** $\geq 2$ is the bare minimum. In practice, KTU board exam problems and real robotic systems use $OSR = 4$ to $10$ to accommodate practical anti-aliasing filter roll-off.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in OERAT831:**
> 1. **LIDAR / Depth Sensors:** Avoid spatial aliasing on striped floors or fencing.
> 2. **IMU Sampling:** Prevent aliasing of high-frequency vibrations into the control loop.
> 3. **Motor Encoders:** Choose $f_s$ at least 2× the maximum expected shaft frequency.
> 4. **Computer Vision:** Frame rate must exceed $2 \times$ maximum motion frequency to prevent "wagon wheel" artifacts.
> 5. **Speech/Audio Interaction:** Robot microphones must satisfy the Nyquist criterion for the human voice band ($4$ kHz $\Rightarrow f_s \geq 8$ kHz, typically $44.1$ kHz in practice).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Spectral Folding from Impulse Sampling

**Goal:** Prove that the spectrum of a sampled signal is a periodic replication of the original spectrum, and derive the aliasing condition.

**Step 1: Define the sampling function.**
The ideal impulse-train sampler is:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)$$

**Step 2: Express the sampled signal.**
The sampled signal $x_s(t)$ is the product of the continuous signal $x_c(t)$ with the impulse train:

$$x_s(t) = x_c(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_c(n T_s) \, \delta(t - n T_s)$$

**Step 3: Apply the Fourier Transform.**
The Fourier Transform of an impulse train is itself an impulse train in frequency:

$$P(f) = \mathcal{F}\{p(t)\} = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(f - \frac{k}{T_s}\right) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 4: Apply the Multiplication Property.**
Multiplication in time corresponds to convolution in frequency (scaled by $1/T_s$):

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} X_c(f) \ast P(f)$$

**Step 5: Substitute and simplify.**
Because $P(f)$ is an impulse train, convolution replicates $X_c(f)$ at every multiple of $f_s$:

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_c(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 6: State the aliasing condition.**
If $X_c(f)$ has nonzero content for $\vert f \vert > f_s/2$, the shifted copies overlap, producing **aliasing**:

$$\text{Aliasing occurs if} \quad f_{max} > \frac{f_s}{2} = f_N$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The sampled spectrum is a perfectly periodic, scaled replication. Overlap is the geometric origin of aliasing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Computing an Aliased Frequency (Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A robot's vibration sensor contains a $3.5$ kHz resonance component. The ADC samples at $f_s = 2$ kHz. What frequency does the controller "see"?

**Step 1: Identify the Nyquist Frequency.**

$$f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{2000}{2} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: Apply the folding rule.**
Choose $m$ such that $f_{in} - m f_s$ falls in the range $[-f_N, f_N]$:

$$m = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{f_{in}}{f_s}\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{3500}{2000}\right) = \text{round}(1.75) = 2$$

**Step 3: Compute the aliased frequency.**

$$f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - m f_s \vert = \vert 3500 - 2 \cdot 2000 \vert = \vert 3500 - 4000 \vert = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.**
The controller perceives a **nonexistent $500$ Hz vibration**, which it may try to damp — destabilizing the robot. **This is aliasing-induced control failure.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Sampling Rate for a Robotics Sensor

**Problem:** A robotic arm has a structural resonance at $f_{res} = 1.2$ kHz. The controller requires an OSR of $5$ for safety margin. What is the required ADC sampling rate?

**Step 1: Apply the Nyquist criterion.**

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \cdot 1200 = 2400 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: Apply the oversampling factor.**

$$f_s = OSR \cdot 2 f_{max} = 5 \cdot 2400 = 12000 \text{ Hz} = 12 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3: Compute the sampling period.**

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{12000} \approx 83.33 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The OSR $= 5$ leaves room for the anti-aliasing filter to roll off without leaking spectral energy above $f_N$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for Anti-Aliasing Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this table in design viva questions.**

| Scenario | Sampling Rate $f_s$ | Required Anti-Alias Filter Cutoff | Recommended Filter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Audio (Speech, 4 kHz) | $\geq 8$ kHz | $\leq 4$ kHz | 4th-order Butterworth |
| IMU (200 Hz vibration) | $\geq 1$ kHz | $\leq 500$ Hz | 2nd-order RC low-pass |
| LIDAR Point Cloud | Frame rate $\geq 2 f_{motion}$ | N/A (digital) | Spatial smoothing |
| Motor Encoder (1 kHz RPM sig.) | $\geq 5$ kHz | $\leq 2.5$ kHz | 2nd-order active LPF |
| Vision Frame (30 Hz motion) | $\geq 60$ FPS | N/A | Optical low-pass |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Aliasing Mechanism — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the signal flow from the analog world through the sampler, demonstrating where aliasing is introduced and how the anti-aliasing filter prevents it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Analog Signal xc(t)"] --> nodeB{"Band-limited Check"}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeD["Anti-Alias LPF"]
    nodeB -- No --> nodeC["Pre-Filter Required"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE["ADC Sampler fs Hz"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Discrete Signal xn"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Digital Processor"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"Fs >= 2Fmax ?"}
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI["Clean Reconstruction"]
    nodeH -- No --> nodeJ["Aliasing Folding Error"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Faulty Robot Decision"]
```

---

## 4.2 Frequency-Domain Aliasing — Spectrum Replication Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Original Spectrum Xc(f) centered at 0"] --> nodeB["Copy 1: Shifted to fs"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Copy 2: Shifted to 2fs"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Copy 3: Shifted to -fs"]
    nodeB --> nodeE{"Overlap with Original?"}
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeF["Aliasing Occurs"]
    nodeE -- No --> nodeG["Safe Reconstruction via LPF"]
```

---

## 4.3 Anti-Aliasing Filter Placement in a Robotic Sensor Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Physical Sensor"] --> nodeB["Analog Signal"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Anti-Alias Filter LPF"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Sample and Hold Circuit"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["ADC"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Microcontroller"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Robot Actuator Command"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from Diagram 4.3:** The anti-aliasing filter must be placed **before** the sampler. Once aliasing enters the digital domain, it cannot be removed — only mitigated.

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Does My Signal Need an Anti-Alias Filter?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Signal Source"] --> nodeB{"Is Signal Band-Limited?"}
    nodeB -- No --> nodeC["Apply LPF at fs/2"]
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeD{"Fs >= 2Fmax?"}
    nodeD -- No --> nodeE["Increase Fs or Filter"]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeF["No Aliasing"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Safe to Sample"]
    nodeE --> nodeG
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define aliasing. State the Nyquist sampling theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Aliasing (1 Mark):** Aliasing is the phenomenon in which two or more distinct continuous-time signals, when sampled at a given rate $f_s$, produce identical sample sequences, making them indistinguishable upon reconstruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Nyquist Sampling Theorem (2 Marks):** A band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**, and $f_s / 2$ is the **Nyquist frequency**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is an anti-aliasing filter? Why must it be placed before the ADC?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 Marks):** An anti-aliasing filter is a low-pass analog filter placed at the input of an ADC, designed to remove all frequency components above $f_N = f_s / 2$ from the input signal, ensuring the Nyquist criterion is satisfied before sampling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Placement Justification (1 Mark):** It must precede the ADC because once a high-frequency component is sampled, its spectral energy folds into the baseband and becomes indistinguishable from genuine low-frequency content. No digital post-processing can recover the original signal after aliasing has occurred.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** A robotic manipulator arm has a torsional vibration at $f_{vib} = 850$ Hz. The position encoder samples at $f_s = 1$ kHz. Determine whether aliasing occurs. If yes, compute the aliased frequency observed by the controller. **[$7$ Marks]**

**(b)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the role of an anti-aliasing filter in a robotic sensor acquisition system. **[$7$ Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1: Compute the Nyquist Frequency.**

$$f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{1000}{2} = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: Compare with vibration frequency.**

$$f_{vib} = 850 \text{ Hz} > f_N = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3: Conclude aliasing.** Since $f_{vib} > f_N$, **aliasing will occur**.

**Step 4: Compute the aliased frequency using the folding formula.**
Find $m = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{f_{vib}}{f_s}\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{850}{1000}\right) = 1$.

$$f_{alias} = \vert f_{vib} - m \cdot f_s \vert = \vert 850 - 1 \cdot 1000 \vert = 150 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5: Engineering Interpretation.**
The controller perceives a **$150$ Hz phantom oscillation** in the arm position. This artifact may trigger incorrect damping commands, leading to instability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (7 Marks):** Aliasing occurs; aliased frequency $= 150$ Hz.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Block Diagram (5 Marks):**

The student must produce a labelled chain:

1. **Physical Sensor** (encoder / IMU / LIDAR)
2. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter** — analog, with cutoff $f_c \leq f_N$
3. **Sample-and-Hold Circuit**
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — sampling at $f_s$
5. **Digital Signal Processor / Microcontroller**
6. **Robot Actuator Command Output**

**Explanation (2 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The anti-aliasing filter restricts the analog input bandwidth to $\vert f \vert \leq f_s / 2$, guaranteeing that no spectral component can fold into the baseband after sampling. In robotics, this is critical for accurate state estimation in feedback loops. Typical implementations use **2nd-order to 4th-order Butterworth filters** for maximally flat passband response.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** State and prove the Nyquist sampling theorem. Derive the condition for distortion-free reconstruction. **[$7$ Marks]**

**(b)** A robot's microphone is designed to capture human speech (bandwidth $4$ kHz). The onboard ADC samples at $f_s = 8$ kHz. Compute the OSR and determine the highest safe input frequency. A sudden high-pitched scream at $12$ kHz is also picked up. What aliased frequency contaminates the speech signal? **[$7$ Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Statement (1 Mark):**
A band-limited signal $x_c(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be reconstructed exactly from its samples $x[n] = x_c(n T_s)$ if and only if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

**Proof (6 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Express the sampled signal using an impulse train.

$$x_s(t) = x_c(t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_c(n T_s) \delta(t - n T_s)$$

**Step 2:** Take the Fourier Transform.

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_c\!\left(f - \frac{k}{T_s}\right) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_c(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 3:** Analyze the replicas.
If $X_c(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_N = f_s/2$, the spectral replicas do not overlap. Each replica is a clean, scaled copy.

**Step 4:** Reconstruct using an ideal low-pass filter with gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_c = f_N$.

$$X_c(f) = T_s \cdot X_s(f) \cdot H_{LPF}(f)$$

**Step 5:** Apply the inverse Fourier transform to recover the time-domain signal.

$$x_c(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_c(n T_s) \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - n T_s}{T_s}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion (Condition):** Distortion-free reconstruction requires $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (no spectral overlap). If violated, reconstruction is impossible due to irreversible aliasing.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1: Compute the OSR.**

$$OSR = \frac{f_s}{2 f_{max}} = \frac{8000}{2 \cdot 4000} = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** $OSR = 1$ is the bare minimum (theoretical Nyquist boundary). In practice, this is unsafe because the anti-aliasing filter cannot have an infinitely sharp roll-off.

**Step 2: Highest Safe Frequency.**

$$f_{safe} = f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{8000}{2} = 4000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Aliased Frequency for the $12$ kHz Scream.**

$$m = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{12000}{8000}\right) = \text{round}(1.5) = 2$$

$$f_{alias} = \vert 12000 - 2 \cdot 8000 \vert = \vert 12000 - 16000 \vert = 4000 \text{ Hz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (7 Marks):** OSR $= 1$; Safe max frequency $= 4$ kHz; Aliased scream frequency $= 4$ kHz (sits exactly on the Nyquist boundary, contaminating the highest speech band).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $f_{Nyquist}$ with $f_N$:** Some students write $f_{Nyquist} = f_s/2$ and $f_N = 2 f_{max}$. The correct convention: $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$ (the rate), $f_N = f_s / 2$ (the frequency).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the absolute value:** When applying $f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - m f_s \vert$, omitting $\vert \cdot \vert$ loses **1 mark** per occurrence.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the anti-alias filter placement:** A correct block diagram is worth 4–5 marks. Placing the filter **after** the ADC is an automatic 0 for that sub-part.
>
> 4. **Not interpreting results in robotics context:** KTU 2024 Scheme awards marks for "real-world engineering insight." Always add a concluding sentence on **how aliasing affects robot behaviour**.
>
> 5. **Misuse of OSR formula:** A common error is $OSR = f_s / f_{max}$ instead of $f_s / (2 f_{max})$. Remember the factor of **$2$**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Aliasing (OERAT831, Module 3)**

- **Aliasing** = high-frequency components masquerading as low-frequency components after sampling.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — this is a *necessary and sufficient* condition for band-limited signals.
- **Nyquist Rate** $f_{Nyq} = 2 f_{max}$ is the *minimum* sampling rate; **Nyquist Frequency** $f_N = f_s/2$ is the *maximum* alias-free input frequency.
- **Folding formula:** $f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - m f_s \vert$, where $m = \text{round}(f_{in}/f_s)$.
- **Anti-aliasing filter** must be an **analog low-pass filter** placed **before** the ADC; typical cutoffs $\leq f_s/2$.
- **Irreversibility:** Aliasing **cannot** be undone digitally — prevention is the only cure.
- **Oversampling Ratio (OSR):** $OSR = f_s / (2 f_{max})$; recommended $\geq 4$ in practice.
- **Sampled spectrum:** $X_s(f) = f_s \sum_k X_c(f - k f_s)$ — periodic replication, overlap causes aliasing.
- **Ideal reconstruction:** $x_c(t) = \sum x[n] \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - n T_s}{T_s}\right)$ (Shannon–Whittaker formula).
- **Robotics hotspots:** LIDAR spatial aliasing, IMU vibration folding, encoder under-sampling, vision wagon-wheel effect, audio $f_s \geq 2 f_{speech}$.
- **Practical tip:** When asked for an $f_s$ in design problems, always quote a value **above** the theoretical Nyquist rate to accommodate filter roll-off.
- **Common exam trap:** A $12$ kHz signal sampled at $8$ kHz folds to $4$ kHz, not $-4$ kHz, not $12$ kHz. Master the modulo-fold rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Aliasing.** Continue to the next sub-topic within Module 3 for full syllabus coverage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Z Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Z Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Z-Transform** is a mathematical operator that maps a discrete-time sequence $x(n)$ into a complex function $X(z)$ of the complex variable $z$. It is the discrete-time counterpart of the Laplace transform and serves as the primary frequency-domain analytical tool for digital signals and discrete-time Linear Time-Invariant (DTI/LTI) systems in robotics, digital control, and DSP applications.

For a discrete-time sequence $x(n)$, the **Bilateral (Two-Sided) Z-Transform** is formally defined as:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \, z^{-n}$$

For causal sequences (which are the standard working case in robotics, since sensor data and actuator commands are inherently forward-in-time), the **Unilateral (One-Sided) Z-Transform** is used:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x(n) \, z^{-n}$$

where $z$ is a complex variable expressed in polar form as $z = r \cdot e^{j\omega}$, with $r$ being the radius and $\omega$ being the digital frequency in **radians/sample**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Z-transform problem is **incomplete** unless the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is explicitly stated. The algebraic expression $X(z)$ alone does not uniquely specify the sequence; the ROC disambiguates it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Standard Metrics in KTU Notation:**
> - $n$: discrete-time sample index (dimensionless integer, $n \in \mathbb{Z}$)
> - $\omega$: digital angular frequency in **radians/sample** (range $-\pi \le \omega \le \pi$ for principal value)
> - $z$: dimensionless complex variable
> - ROC: expressed as a modulus inequality such as $\vert z \vert > a$

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Discrete-Time Crystal Ball"

Imagine a **discrete sequence $x(n)$** as a series of flashes of light at instants $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ Each flash has an intensity $x(n)$. The Z-transform is like a **weighted camera lens** with magnification $z^{-n}$:

- The factor $z^{-n}$ **attenuates** and **phase-shifts** each flash by an amount that depends on *when* it occurred (its sample index $n$) and the chosen lens position $z$.
- The camera sums all these weighted flashes into a single snapshot $X(z)$.
- Change the lens position (vary $z$), and you get a *different* snapshot — collectively, all snapshots form the complete Z-transform.

> **Real-World Engineering Connection:** In a robotic arm's digital PID controller, the Z-transform converts the discrete error sequence $e(n)$ into a transfer function $E(z)$, allowing the engineer to design, analyze stability, and tune the controller entirely in the $z$-domain — without simulating every sample step-by-step.

### 1.3 Why Z-Transform is Indispensable in Robotics

| Application | Role of Z-Transform |
|---|---|
| Digital PID/State-Space Control of Robots | Converts difference equations to algebraic equations in $z$ |
| Sensor Signal Filtering (IMU, LiDAR) | Designs digital filters using $H(z)$ |
| Stability Analysis | Tests if all poles lie **inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$ |
| System Identification | Matches measured $x(n), y(n)$ to rational $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ |
| Kinematic Trajectory Generation | Polynomial paths expressed compactly in $z$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Critical Components of a Z-Transform Result

A complete Z-transform problem (university/board answer) must always provide:

1. **The Algebraic Expression $X(z)$** — a rational function in $z^{-1}$ (or $z$).
2. **The Region of Convergence (ROC)** — the set of $z$ values for which the summation converges.
3. **Poles and Zeros Plot** (where asked) — visual representation on the $z$-plane.

### 2.2 Region of Convergence (ROC) — Rigorous Definition

The ROC is the set of all values of $z = re^{j\omega}$ in the complex $z$-plane for which the infinite sum $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) z^{-n}$ converges to a finite, bounded value.

Mathematically, the ROC is defined by the **absolute summability condition**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left| x(n) \, z^{-n} \right| = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left| x(n) \right| \, r^{-n} < \infty$$

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Properties of the ROC

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Guaranteed Question Element:** At least one ROC property appears in nearly every ESE paper. Memorize the bullets below.

- **P1:** The ROC is a **ring** (annular region) in the $z$-plane bounded by poles: $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$.
- **P2:** The ROC **does not contain any pole** of $X(z)$.
- **P3:** If $x(n)$ is a **finite-duration** sequence, the ROC is the **entire $z$-plane** except possibly $z = 0$ or $z = \infty$.
- **P4:** If $x(n)$ is **right-sided** (causal, $x(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$), the ROC is the region **outside the outermost pole**: $\vert z \vert > R_1$.
- **P5:** If $x(n)$ is **left-sided** (anti-causal, $x(n) = 0$ for $n > 0$), the ROC is the region **inside the innermost pole**: $\vert z \vert < R_2$.
- **P6:** If $x(n)$ is **two-sided**, the ROC is an **annular ring**: $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$.
- **P7:** The ROC must be a **connected region** (no disjoint patches).
- **P8:** For a **stable** causal LTI system, the ROC **must include the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.

### 2.4 Poles and Zeros — Geometric Interpretation

For a rational $X(z)$:

$$X(z) = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)} = \frac{b_0 \, (z - z_1)(z - z_2) \cdots (z - z_M)}{a_0 \, (z - p_1)(z - p_2) \cdots (z - p_N)}$$

- **Zeros** ($z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_M$): values of $z$ that make $X(z) = 0$.
- **Poles** ($p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_N$): values of $z$ that make $X(z) \to \infty$. These **always bound the ROC**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Pair | Time Domain $x(n)$ | Z-Domain $X(z)$ | ROC |
|:-:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Unit Impulse | $\delta(n)$ | $1$ | All $z$ |
| 2 | Unit Step | $u(n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| 3 | Delayed Impulse | $\delta(n - k)$ | $z^{-k}$ | All $z$ except $0$ (for $k>0$) |
| 4 | Causal Exponential | $a^n u(n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| 5 | Anti-causal Exponential | $-a^n u(-n-1)$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ |
| 6 | Ramp | $n \, u(n)$ | $\dfrac{z^{-1}}{(1 - z^{-1})^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| 7 | Causal Expo-Ramp | $n \, a^n u(n)$ | $\dfrac{a \, z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| 8 | Sinusoid | $\sin(\omega_0 n) \, u(n)$ | $\dfrac{z^{-1} \sin(\omega_0)}{1 - 2z^{-1}\cos(\omega_0) + z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| 9 | Cosine | $\cos(\omega_0 n) \, u(n)$ | $\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}\cos(\omega_0)}{1 - 2z^{-1}\cos(\omega_0) + z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| 10 | Linearity | $a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)$ | $a X_1(z) + b X_2(z)$ | $\sup(\text{ROC}_1, \text{ROC}_2)$ |
| 11 | Time Shift | $x(n - n_0)$ | $z^{-n_0} X(z)$ | ROC unchanged (except $0/\infty$) |
| 12 | Time Reversal | $x(-n)$ | $X(1/z)$ | Inverted ROC |
| 13 | Multiplication by $a^n$ | $a^n x(n)$ | $X(z/a)$ | Scaled ROC: $\vert a \vert \cdot \text{ROC}$ |
| 14 | Differentiation in $z$ | $n \, x(n)$ | $-z \dfrac{d X(z)}{dz}$ | ROC unchanged |
| 15 | Convolution | $x_1(n) * x_2(n)$ | $X_1(z) \cdot X_2(z)$ | $\sup(\text{ROC}_1, \text{ROC}_2)$ |
| 16 | Accumulation | $\sum_{k=0}^{n} x(k)$ | $\dfrac{X(z)}{1 - z^{-1}}$ | ROC $\cap$ $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| 17 | Initial Value Thm. | $x(0)$ | $\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | Requires causal, ROC $\vert z \vert > R$ |
| 18 | Final Value Thm. | $\lim_{n \to \infty} x(n)$ | $\lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$ | Requires ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$ |

### 2.6 Stability Criterion (Critical for Robotics)

A causal LTI system with rational transfer function $H(z) = \dfrac{B(z)}{A(z)}$ is **BIBO Stable** if and only if **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** When tuning a digital controller for a robotic manipulator (e.g., joint position control), the designer computes the closed-loop transfer function's poles. If any pole exits the unit circle, the robot's joint angle **diverges** — the arm oscillates or crashes. This check is performed entirely using Z-transform pole locations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Z-Transform of the Unit Step Sequence $u(n)$

**Starting Point:** The unit step sequence is defined as:

$$u(n) = \begin{cases} 1, & n \ge 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Apply the Z-Transform Definition:**

$$U(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} u(n) \, z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (1) \, z^{-n}$$

**Recognize the Infinite Geometric Series Form:**

$$U(z) = 1 + z^{-1} + z^{-2} + z^{-3} + \cdots$$

**Apply the Geometric Series Summation Formula** $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n = \dfrac{1}{1 - r}$ where $r = z^{-1}$:

$$U(z) = \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - 1}$$

**Determine ROC:** The geometric series converges only if $\vert z^{-1} \vert < 1 \implies \vert z \vert > 1$.

> **Conclusion:** $\quad \mathcal{Z}\{u(n)\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Z-Transform of the Causal Exponential $a^n u(n)$

**Apply Z-Transform Definition:**

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n \, z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a z^{-1})^n$$

**This is a Geometric Series in $(a z^{-1})$:**

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - a}$$

**Convergence requires $\vert a z^{-1} \vert < 1 \implies \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$:**

> **Conclusion:** $\quad \mathcal{Z}\{a^n u(n)\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linearity Property of the Z-Transform

**Statement:** $\mathcal{Z}\{a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)\} = a X_1(z) + b X_2(z)$

**Proof:**

$$\mathcal{Z}\{a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} [a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)] \, z^{-n}$$

**Distribute the summation:**

$$= a \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1(n) \, z^{-n} + b \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_2(n) \, z^{-n}$$

**Recognize the Z-transforms:**

$$= a \, X_1(z) + b \, X_2(z) \quad \blacksquare$$

The ROC is the intersection of $\text{ROC}_1$ and $\text{ROC}_2$, extended outward as far as possible.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Time Shifting Property $x(n - n_0) \longleftrightarrow z^{-n_0} X(z)$

**Let $y(n) = x(n - n_0)$. Apply Z-transform definition:**

$$Y(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n - n_0) \, z^{-n}$$

**Substitute $m = n - n_0 \implies n = m + n_0, \, dn = dm$:**

$$Y(z) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x(m) \, z^{-(m + n_0)}$$

**Separate the exponent:**

$$Y(z) = z^{-n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x(m) \, z^{-m} = z^{-n_0} X(z) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Inverse Z-Transform via Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)

**Problem Type (Highly Probable in KTU ESE):** Given $X(z) = \dfrac{N(z)}{D(z)}$, find $x(n)$ for $n \ge 0$.

**Worked Example:** Find the inverse Z-transform of:

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.8$$

**Step 1: Express $X(z)/z$ in partial fractions:**

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{1}{z (1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})}$$

**Step 2: Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^2$ to convert to polynomial form:**

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z}{(z - 0.5)(z - 0.8)}$$

**Step 3: Apply PFE (cover-up method):**

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{A}{z - 0.5} + \frac{B}{z - 0.8}$$

**Step 4: Compute residues:**

$$A = \left. \frac{z}{z - 0.8} \right|_{z = 0.5} = \frac{0.5}{0.5 - 0.8} = \frac{0.5}{-0.3} = -\frac{5}{3}$$

$$B = \left. \frac{z}{z - 0.5} \right|_{z = 0.8} = \frac{0.8}{0.8 - 0.5} = \frac{0.8}{0.3} = \frac{8}{3}$$

**Step 5: Reconstruct $X(z)$ by multiplying by $z$:**

$$X(z) = \frac{-5/3}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{8/3}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 6: Apply the standard pair $\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} \longleftrightarrow a^n u(n)$:**

$$x(n) = \left( -\frac{5}{3} (0.5)^n + \frac{8}{3} (0.8)^n \right) u(n)$$

**Step 7: Verify ROC:** Since the sequence is causal and the dominant pole is at $0.8$, the ROC is $\vert z \vert > 0.8$. ✓

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Convolution Property (Foundation of LTI System Analysis)

**Statement:** $y(n) = x(n) * h(n) \longleftrightarrow Y(z) = X(z) H(z)$

**Starting from the discrete convolution sum:**

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \, h(n - k)$$

**Take the Z-transform:**

$$Y(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) h(n - k) \right] z^{-n}$$

**Interchange summation order:**

$$Y(z) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h(n - k) z^{-n} \right]$$

**Apply the time-shift property to the inner sum:**

$$Y(z) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \, z^{-k} H(z) = X(z) H(z) \quad \blacksquare$$

> **Robotics Application:** A robot's response $y(n)$ to a sensor input $x(n)$ is found by simply **multiplying** the Z-transforms $X(z) \cdot H(z)$ — no summation required. This is the foundation of digital filter design in robotics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Z-Transform Analysis in a Robotic Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Discrete Time Input x of n"] --> nodeB["Apply Z Transform Definition"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Compute X of z as Sum"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Identify Poles and Zeros"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Determine ROC"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"Sequence Type?"}
    nodeF -- Causal --> nodeG["ROC Outside Outer Pole"]
    nodeF -- Anticausal --> nodeH["ROC Inside Inner Pole"]
    nodeF -- Twosided --> nodeI["ROC Annular Ring R1 less z less R2"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Stability Check All Poles Inside Unit Circle"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK{"Stable System?"}
    nodeK -- Yes --> nodeL["Use PFE to Get h of n"]
    nodeK -- No --> nodeM["Redesign Controller or Filter"]
    nodeL --> nodeN["Reconstruct x of n via Inverse Z Transform"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Apply to Robot Actuator Output y of n"]
    nodeM --> nodeB
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Visualization of the Z-Plane ROC Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph pl["Z-Plane ROC Configurations"]
        direction TB
        nodeP1["Causal Sequence: ROC is region outside the outermost pole. The unit circle lies inside the ROC if the system is stable."]
        nodeP2["Anti-causal Sequence: ROC is region inside the innermost pole. Unit circle must be excluded for stability."]
        nodeP3["Two-sided Sequence: ROC is annular ring R1 less than mod z less than R2. Both poles bound the ROC on different sides."]
        nodeP4["Finite Duration: ROC is the entire z-plane except possibly z=0 and z=infinity. No poles restrict it."]
    end
    pl --> nodeQ["KTU 2024 Exam Tip: Always sketch the pole-zero plot alongside the ROC description to secure full marks."]
```

### 4.3 LTI System Flow in the Z-Domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["X of z Input"] --> nodeS["H of z Transfer Function"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["Multiplier X times H"]
    nodeT --> nodeU["Y of z Output"]
    nodeU --> nodeV["Inverse Z Transform"]
    nodeV --> nodeW["y of n Discrete Time Response"]
```

### 4.4 Pole-Zero Plot Schematic (Text Representation)

```
           Imaginary Axis (jω)
                  │
              × ──┼── ×          × = pole
             ╱    │   ╲         ○ = zero
            ╱     │    ╲        ○ = unit circle (stability boundary)
           ○      │     ○
        ───────────●─────────── Real Axis (σ)
           ○      │     ○
            ╲     │    ╱
             ╲    │   ╱
              × ──┼── ×
                  │
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Rule (for KTU board answers):** If all **×** (poles) lie strictly **inside** the unit circle **○**, the system is stable. If any pole sits on the unit circle, the system is **marginally stable**. If any pole lies outside, the system is **unstable** — and the robotic system will diverge.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define the Z-transform of a discrete-time sequence $x(n)$. What is the Region of Convergence (ROC)?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Z-transform of a discrete-time sequence $x(n)$ is defined as the bilateral series:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \, z^{-n}$$

The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is the set of all complex values $z = re^{j\omega}$ for which this infinite summation converges to a finite value, i.e., $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n) \vert r^{-n} < \infty$. (3 marks: definition 2 marks + ROC definition 1 mark)

---

**Q2. State any three properties of the Region of Convergence (ROC) of the Z-transform.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. The ROC does not contain any pole of $X(z)$. (1 mark)
2. For a right-sided (causal) sequence, the ROC is the region outside the outermost pole. (1 mark)
3. For a left-sided (anti-causal) sequence, the ROC is the region inside the innermost pole. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Determine the Z-transform and ROC of the causal sequence $x(n) = (0.5)^n u(n) + 2^n u(-n-1)$. Comment on the stability of the system. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the two components.
- $x_1(n) = (0.5)^n u(n)$ — causal right-sided
- $x_2(n) = 2^n u(-n-1)$ — anti-causal left-sided

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute Z-transform of each using standard pairs.

$$X_1(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.5$$

For $x_2(n) = 2^n u(-n-1)$:

$$X_2(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{-1} 2^n z^{-n} = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} 2^{-k} z^{k} = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \left( \frac{z}{2} \right)^k = \frac{z/2}{1 - z/2} = \frac{-1}{1 - 0.5 z}$$

Convert to $z^{-1}$ form: $X_2(z) = \dfrac{-1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{z} \cdot z = \dfrac{-1}{1 - 0.5 z} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 2z^{-1}}$ after sign adjustment. Final form:

$$X_2(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 2z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert < 2$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply linearity.

$$X(z) = X_1(z) + X_2(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{1}{1 - 2 z^{-1}}$$

Common denominator expansion gives:

$$X(z) = \frac{2 - 2.5 z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 2 z^{-1})}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Determine the ROC.
- $\text{ROC}_1: \vert z \vert > 0.5$
- $\text{ROC}_2: \vert z \vert < 2$
- Combined ROC: $0.5 < \vert z \vert < 2$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Stability comment. The ROC is a non-empty annulus that **does not include the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$ (since $1$ is excluded because it coincides with the pole at $z=2$ boundary). Wait — $1$ is strictly between $0.5$ and $2$, so the unit circle **is included** $\implies$ the system is **stable**. *(Corrected comment: Stable.)*

---

**(b)** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{z^2}{(z - 1)(z - 0.5)^2}, \quad \vert z \vert > 1$ using partial fraction expansion. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Express as $X(z)/z$:

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z}{(z - 1)(z - 0.5)^2}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply PFE:

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{A}{z - 1} + \frac{B}{z - 0.5} + \frac{C}{(z - 0.5)^2}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute residues.

$$A = \left. \frac{z}{(z - 0.5)^2} \right|_{z = 1} = \frac{1}{(0.5)^2} = 4$$

$$C = \left. \frac{z}{z - 1} \right|_{z = 0.5} = \frac{0.5}{-0.5} = -1$$

$$B = \left. \frac{d}{dz} \left[ \frac{z}{z - 1} \right] \right|_{z = 0.5} = \left. \frac{(z-1) - z}{(z-1)^2} \right|_{z = 0.5} = \frac{-1}{(-0.5)^2} = -4$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Reconstruct $X(z)$:

$$X(z) = \frac{4}{1 - z^{-1}} - \frac{4}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} - \frac{0.5}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Use standard pairs to invert:

- $\dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} \longleftrightarrow u(n)$
- $\dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} \longleftrightarrow (0.5)^n u(n)$
- $\dfrac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} \longleftrightarrow n a^n u(n)$ — for $a = 0.5$, the form $\dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})^2}$ corresponds to $\dfrac{n (0.5)^{n-1}}{0.5} u(n) = 2n (0.5)^{n-1} u(n) = 4n(0.5)^n u(n) \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot$ — careful manipulation gives $0.5/(1-0.5z^{-1})^2 \longleftrightarrow (n+1)(0.5)^n u(n)$.

$$\boxed{x(n) = \left[ 4 - 4 (0.5)^n - (n+1)(0.5)^n \right] u(n)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** State and prove the time-shifting property of the Z-transform. Use it to find $\mathcal{Z}\{u(n - 3)\}$. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Statement. If $X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{x(n)\}$, then $\mathcal{Z}\{x(n - n_0)\} = z^{-n_0} X(z)$.

**Step 2 (4 marks):** Proof (write as shown in Derivation 4 of Section 3).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Application. Since $u(n) \longleftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$, we have:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{u(n - 3)\} = z^{-3} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1$$

---

**(b)** A discrete-time LTI system is described by the difference equation $y(n) - 0.6 y(n-1) + 0.08 y(n-2) = x(n)$. Find the transfer function $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ assuming zero initial conditions. Determine if the system is stable. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Take Z-transform of both sides using the time-shift property (zero initial conditions).

$$Y(z) - 0.6 z^{-1} Y(z) + 0.08 z^{-2} Y(z) = X(z)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Factor $Y(z)$:

$$Y(z) [1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2}] = X(z)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Solve for transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Find poles by setting denominator to zero: $1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2} = 0$. Multiply by $z^2$: $z^2 - 0.6 z + 0.08 = 0$. Using quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{0.6 \pm \sqrt{0.36 - 0.32}}{2} = \frac{0.6 \pm 0.2}{2} \implies z_1 = 0.4, \; z_2 = 0.2$$

Both poles satisfy $\vert z \vert < 1$, so the system is **stable**. (Causal ROC is $\vert z \vert > 0.4$ which includes unit circle.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ROC** — Half-mark deduction per instance; sometimes full mark loss in "compute the Z-transform" questions where ROC is mandatory.
> 2. **Not specifying sequence type (causal/anti-causal/two-sided)** before writing the ROC — the ROC depends on the support of $x(n)$.
> 3. **Sign error in anti-causal exponentials** like $-a^n u(-n-1)$ — students often drop the negative sign.
> 4. **In PFE problems, forgetting to multiply back by $z$** when using the cover-up method on $X(z)/z$. Marks are typically 2–3 for this step alone.
> 5. **Confusing stability with causality** — a system can be stable but non-causal; a causal system is stable only if all poles are inside the unit circle.
> 6. **In inverse Z-transform using long division** — students often forget to verify the sequence starts at $n=0$ (causal case).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-Full-Revision Cheat Sheet (Read 5 minutes before exam):**

- **Definition:** $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) z^{-n}$ — always specify ROC.
- **One-sided Z-transform** is used for **causal** sequences; start the sum from $n = 0$.
- **Standard Pairs (must memorize):** $\delta(n) \leftrightarrow 1$, $u(n) \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$, $a^n u(n) \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$, $n u(n) \leftrightarrow \dfrac{z^{-1}}{(1 - z^{-1})^2}$, $n a^n u(n) \leftrightarrow \dfrac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$.
- **ROC Rules:** Outside outermost pole (causal), inside innermost pole (anti-causal), annular ring (two-sided), no poles allowed inside ROC.
- **Stability Rule (KTU Favorite):** Causal + BIBO stable $\iff$ all poles strictly inside $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- **Properties to memorize for marks:** Linearity, Time Shift, Time Reversal, Scaling ($a^n x(n)$), Differentiation in $z$ ($n x(n)$), Convolution.
- **Inverse Z-transform methods (ranked by frequency in KTU papers):** PFE > Long Division > Residue Method.
- **PFE Strategy:** Express $X(z)/z$ in partial fractions when the order of numerator $\ge$ order of denominator, then multiply by $z$ back and use standard pairs.
- **Initial Value Theorem:** $x(0) = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ (only for causal sequences with proper rational $X(z)$).
- **Final Value Theorem:** $\lim_{n \to \infty} x(n) = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$ (only valid if all poles of $(1-z^{-1})X(z)$ are strictly inside the unit circle).
- **Convolution in time = Multiplication in $z$-domain** — this single property is the foundation of digital filter design in robotics.
- **Always** draw a **pole-zero plot** alongside the ROC for full marks in ESE problems.
- **Common KTU traps:** Negative exponential sign errors, ROC vs. system function confusion, missing the "causal" qualifier.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Z Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inverse Z Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Inverse Z-Transform (IZT)** is the mathematical procedure used to recover the original discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ from its Z-domain representation $X(z)$. It is the reverse operation of the Z-Transform and is formally expressed as:

$$x[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{X(z)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z) \, z^{n-1} \, dz$$

where $C$ is a closed contour encircling the origin and lying entirely within the Region of Convergence (ROC) of $X(z)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Inverse Z-Transform is **NOT unique** on its own — a sequence $x[n]$ is uniquely determined only when **both** its Z-transform $X(z)$ **and** its ROC are specified.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **smoothie** (the Z-transform $X(z)$). This smoothie is made by blending many different fruits in exact proportions (the sequence values $x[n]$). The **Inverse Z-Transform** is the process of carefully un-blending the smoothie and identifying each fruit and its exact quantity — that is, reconstructing $x[n]$.

- The **fruits** $\longrightarrow$ individual time-domain samples $x[n]$
- The **smoothie recipe (ratio)** $\longrightarrow$ $X(z)$
- The **type of blender / blending radius** $\longrightarrow$ Region of Convergence (ROC)

> [!NOTE]
> In **robotics**, just as the smoothie recipe doesn't tell you *when* each fruit was added, the algebraic expression $X(z)$ alone does not tell you whether the signal is causal, anti-causal, or two-sided. The ROC carries this critical temporal information.

### Physical & Engineering Context

In robotic systems, the Inverse Z-Transform is the bridge from **digital controller design** (done in the Z-domain using $H(z)$) to **real-time actuator commands** $h[n]$. Without the IZT, a designed filter cannot be implemented on a microcontroller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants & Symbols**:
> - Sampling period: $T$ (seconds)
> - Sampling frequency: $f_s = 1/T$ (Hz)
> - Discrete time index: $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ (integer)
> - Unit circle: $\vert z \vert = 1$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The "Why" Behind the Inverse Z-Transform

The forward Z-Transform compresses a sequence $x[n]$ into a rational function $X(z)$. We need the inverse because:

1. **Filter Realization**: We design $H(z)$ in Z-domain, but microcontrollers run difference equations in time-domain.
2. **Stability Analysis**: IZT combined with ROC reveals if a system is causal and stable.
3. **System Identification**: Recovering impulse response $h[n]$ from measured $H(z)$.

### The Four Standard Methods of Computing the Inverse Z-Transform

#### Method 1: Long Division (Power Series) Method

**How it works**: If $X(z)$ is a rational polynomial, expand it into a power series by performing polynomial long division (numerator $\div$ denominator). The coefficients of $z^{-n}$ give $x[n]$.

- **Best for**: First few samples of causal sequences.
- **Limitation**: Cannot yield a closed-form expression for $x[n]$.

#### Method 2: Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE) Method

**How it works**: Express $X(z)$ as a sum of simple first-order terms using partial fractions, then look up each term in the standard Z-transform table and sum them.

- **Best for**: Most KTU exam problems.
- **Limitation**: Requires correct factoring of the denominator.

#### Method 3: Residue (Contour Integration) Method

**How it works**: Apply the Cauchy Residue Theorem to the contour integral directly. The result is:

$$x[n] = \sum \text{Residues of } X(z) \, z^{n-1} \text{ at poles inside } C$$

- **Best for**: Analytical/theoretical problems.
- **Limitation**: Mathematically heavy.

#### Method 4: Inspection / Z-Transform Table Lookup

**How it works**: Match $X(z)$ directly with a known transform pair, possibly after algebraic manipulation (e.g., multiplying and dividing by $z$).

- **Best for**: Simple standard forms.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Definition (Contour Integral) | $x[n] = \dfrac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z) \, z^{n-1} \, dz$ | Master formula for IZT |
| Long Division Result | $X(z) = x[0] + x[1]z^{-1} + x[2]z^{-2} + \cdots$ | Read off $x[n]$ as coefficients |
| PFE — Distinct Poles | $X(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \dfrac{A_k}{1 - a_k z^{-1}}$ | $A_k$ are residues |
| Residue Formula (Distinct Pole) | $A_k = (1 - a_k z^{-1}) \, X(z) \,\Big\vert_{z=a_k}$ | Compute numerator residue |
| Residue Formula (Repeated Pole of order $m$) | $A_k = \dfrac{1}{(m-1)!} \lim_{z\to a_k} \dfrac{d^{m-1}}{dz^{m-1}} \left[ (z-a_k)^m X(z)z^{n-1} \right]$ | Multiple-order pole case |
| Time Reversal | $x[-n] \leftrightarrow X(z^{-1}); \quad \text{ROC}: 1/r_1 < \vert z \vert < 1/r_2$ | Bidirectional sequences |
| Multiplication by $a^n$ | $a^n x[n] \leftrightarrow X(z/a); \quad \text{ROC}: \vert a \vert r_1 < \vert z \vert < \vert a \vert r_2$ | Modulation in Z-domain |
| Differentiation Property | $n \, x[n] \leftrightarrow -z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | Used in PFE for $nz^{-n}$ forms |
| Convolution Theorem | $x[n] * h[n] \longleftrightarrow X(z) \, H(z)$ | Filter output computation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard Z-Transform Pairs you MUST memorize:
> - $\delta[n] \leftrightarrow 1$ (all $z$)
> - $u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > 1$
> - $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$
> - $-a^n u[-n-1] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$
> - $n a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$

### Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Robotics**: Realizing a digital PID controller $H(z)$ back as a difference equation running on a robot's onboard computer.
- **Audio Processing**: Reconstructing time-domain audio samples from equalizer transfer functions.
- **Biomedical**: Recovering ECG signal patterns after digital filtering.
- **Communications**: Matched filtering in digital receivers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Inverse Z-Transform via Partial Fraction Expansion (Canonical KTU Example)

**Problem**: Find $x[n]$ for:

$$X(z) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - z^{-1})(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})}, \quad \text{ROC}: \vert z \vert > 1$$

**Step 1: Identify the form.** Since the ROC is the exterior of the outermost pole ($z=1$), the sequence $x[n]$ is **right-sided (causal)**.

**Step 2: Set up the partial fraction expansion.** Because both poles are at the origin after substitution, but here we have poles at $z=1$ and $z=0.5$, we use the negative-power form:

$$X(z) = \dfrac{A}{1 - z^{-1}} + \dfrac{B}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3: Solve for A.**

$$A = (1 - z^{-1}) X(z) \,\Big\vert_{z^{-1} = 1} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5(1)} = \dfrac{1}{0.5} = 2$$

**Step 4: Solve for B.**

$$B = (1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) X(z) \,\Big\vert_{z^{-1} = 2} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 2} = -1$$

**Step 5: Verify.** $A + B = 2 - 1 = 1$ ✓ (matches the constant term in numerator).

**Step 6: Substitute back.**

$$X(z) = \dfrac{2}{1 - z^{-1}} - \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 7: Apply standard pairs with ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$ (causal).**

$$\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}\right\} = u[n]$$

$$\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}\right\} = (0.5)^n u[n]$$

**Step 8: Final Answer (closed form).**

$$x[n] = \left[ 2 - (0.5)^n \right] u[n]$$

**Step 9: Verify by computing first 3 samples.**
- $n=0: \quad 2 - 1 = 1$
- $n=1: \quad 2 - 0.5 = 1.5$
- $n=2: \quad 2 - 0.25 = 1.75$

Cross-check via long division (numerator = 1, denominator = $1 - 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.5 z^{-2}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= 1 \div (1 - 1.5z^{-1} + 0.5z^{-2}) \\
     &= 1 + 1.5z^{-1} + 1.75z^{-2} + 1.875z^{-3} + \cdots
\end{aligned}$$

This matches $x[0]=1$, $x[1]=1.5$, $x[2]=1.75$ ✓

---

### Derivation 2: Inverse Z-Transform Using the Residue Method

**Problem**: Find $x[n]$ for $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > 1$.

**Step 1: Apply the residue formula.**

$$x[n] = \sum \text{Res} \left[ X(z) \, z^{n-1}, \; z = p_k \right]$$

$$\Rightarrow x[n] = \sum \text{Res} \left[ \dfrac{z \cdot z^{n-1}}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}, \; z = p_k \right] = \sum \text{Res} \left[ \dfrac{z^n}{(z-1)(z-0.5)} \right]$$

**Step 2: Identify poles.** $p_1 = 1, \; p_2 = 0.5$. Both are inside the contour for causal ROC.

**Step 3: Compute residue at $z = 1$.**

$$R_1 = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) \cdot \dfrac{z^n}{(z-1)(z-0.5)} = \dfrac{1^n}{1 - 0.5} = 2$$

**Step 4: Compute residue at $z = 0.5$.**

$$R_2 = \lim_{z \to 0.5} (z-0.5) \cdot \dfrac{z^n}{(z-1)(z-0.5)} = \dfrac{(0.5)^n}{0.5 - 1} = -2(0.5)^n$$

**Step 5: Sum residues.**

$$x[n] = R_1 + R_2 = 2 - 2(0.5)^n = 2\left[1 - (0.5)^n\right] u[n]$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both methods (PFE and Residue) yield **identical answers**, but Residue works even when $X(z)$ has non-rational components like $\ln(1+z^{-1})$.

---

### Derivation 3: Inverse Z-Transform of a Two-Sided Sequence (ROC is an Annulus)

**Problem**: Find $x[n]$ for $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 2 z^{-1})}$, ROC: $0.5 < \vert z \vert < 2$.

**Step 1: Interpret the ROC.** Outer boundary is $\vert z \vert < 2$, so the pole at $z=2$ corresponds to a **left-sided** component. Inner boundary is $\vert z \vert > 0.5$, so the pole at $z=0.5$ corresponds to a **right-sided** component.

**Step 2: Expand.**

$$X(z) = \dfrac{A}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \dfrac{B}{1 - 2 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3: Compute A.**

$$A = (1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) X(z) \,\Big\vert_{z^{-1} = 2} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 2(2)} = -\dfrac{1}{3}$$

**Step 4: Compute B.**

$$B = (1 - 2 z^{-1}) X(z) \,\Big\vert_{z^{-1} = 0.5} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5(0.5)} = \dfrac{1}{0.75} = \dfrac{4}{3}$$

**Step 5: Assign sidedness based on ROC.**

$$X(z) = -\dfrac{1/3}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \dfrac{4/3}{1 - 2 z^{-1}}$$

- Pole $0.5$, causal $\Rightarrow (0.5)^n u[n]$
- Pole $2$, anti-causal $\Rightarrow -(2)^n u[-n-1]$

**Step 6: Final Answer.**

$$x[n] = -\dfrac{1}{3}(0.5)^n u[n] - \dfrac{4}{3}(2)^n u[-n-1]$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Functional Block Architecture of the Inverse Z-Transform Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input X of z in Z domain"] --> B{"Is X of z Rational Polynomial?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Choose Method"]
    B -- No --> D["Use Residue Method"]
    C --> E["Long Division Method"]
    C --> F["Partial Fraction Expansion"]
    C --> G["Table Lookup Inspection"]
    E --> H["Identify ROC"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    D --> H
    H --> I{"ROC Type?"}
    I -- "|z| greater than pole_max" --> J["Right sided causal x of n"]
    I -- "|z| less than pole_min" --> K["Left sided anti causal x of n"]
    I -- "Annulus between poles" --> L["Two sided sequence x of n"]
    J --> M["Output discrete time sequence x of n"]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N["Feed into Robotic Controller"]
```

### Diagram 2: Method Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Need to compute x of n from X of z"] --> Q1{"Looking for closed form?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Single pole or repeated?"}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3["Use Long Division"]
    Q2 -- Simple distinct --> PFE["Partial Fraction Expansion"]
    Q2 -- Complex repeated --> RES["Residue Method with derivatives"]
    Q2 -- Already a standard pair --> TBL["Direct Table Lookup"]
    PFE --> Solve["Solve for A_k residues"]
    RES --> Solve
    Solve --> Check["Verify ROC and sidedness"]
    TBL --> Check
    Check --> Out["Output x of n sequence"]
```

### Diagram 3: Relationship Between ROC and Sequence Sidedness

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Pole at z = a"] --> B{"Where is ROC relative to a?"}
    B -- "|z| greater than mod a" --> C["Right sided a to the n u of n"]
    B -- "|z| less than mod a" --> D["Left sided minus a to the n u of minus n minus 1"]
    B -- "ROC excludes a" --> E["Two sided mixed sequence"]
    C --> F["Causal and stable if mod a less than 1"]
    D --> G["Anti causal"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Define the Inverse Z-Transform. Why is the specification of the Region of Convergence (ROC) essential for a unique IZT?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Inverse Z-Transform is the mathematical operation that recovers the discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ from its Z-domain representation $X(z)$. It is given by the contour integral:

$$x[n] = \dfrac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z) \, z^{n-1} \, dz$$

ROC is essential because two **different sequences** can have the **same** $X(z)$ expression but different ROCs. For example, $a^n u[n]$ and $-a^n u[-n-1]$ both have $\dfrac{1}{1-a z^{-1}}$, but with ROCs $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ and $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ respectively. Without ROC, the IZT is non-unique. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *List any four methods of evaluating the Inverse Z-Transform.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Long Division (Power Series) Method
2. Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE) Method
3. Residue (Contour Integration) Method
4. Inspection / Table Lookup Method
5. (Bonus, optional) Convolution Method

Each method has specific advantages based on the nature of $X(z)$ and the desired output format. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Module 3]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Determine the inverse Z-transform of:
> $$X(z) = \dfrac{1 + 2z^{-1}}{1 - 0.6z^{-1} + 0.08z^{-2}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.4$$
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Using the residue method, find $x[n]$ for $X(z) = \dfrac{z^2}{(z-1)(z-0.5)(z-0.8)}$ assuming causal sequence.

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Partial Fraction Method:

**Step 1: Factor the denominator.**
$$1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2} = (1 - 0.4 z^{-1})(1 - 0.2 z^{-1})$$

Poles at $z = 0.4$ and $z = 0.2$.

**Step 2: Setup PFE.**
$$X(z) = \dfrac{1 + 2z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.4 z^{-1})(1 - 0.2 z^{-1})} = \dfrac{A}{1 - 0.4 z^{-1}} + \dfrac{B}{1 - 0.2 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3: Solve for A.**
$$A = (1 - 0.4 z^{-1}) X(z) \,\Big\vert_{z^{-1} = 2.5} = \dfrac{1 + 2(2.5)}{1 - 0.2(2.5)} = \dfrac{6}{0.5} = 12$$

**Step 4: Solve for B.**
$$B = (1 - 0.2 z^{-1}) X(z) \,\Big\vert_{z^{-1} = 5} = \dfrac{1 + 2(5)}{1 - 0.4(5)} = \dfrac{11}{-1} = -11$$

**Step 5: Verify.** $A + B = 12 - 11 = 1$ ✓

**Step 6: Apply table (causal, ROC $|z| > 0.4$).**
$$x[n] = \left[12(0.4)^n - 11(0.2)^n\right] u[n] \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Residue Method:

**Step 1:** Apply residue formula with poles at $z = 1, 0.5, 0.8$.

$$x[n] = \sum \text{Res}\left[ \dfrac{z^2 \cdot z^{n-1}}{(z-1)(z-0.5)(z-0.8)}\right] = \sum \text{Res}\left[ \dfrac{z^{n+1}}{(z-1)(z-0.5)(z-0.8)}\right]$$

**Step 2: Residue at $z=1$:**
$$R_1 = \dfrac{1^{n+1}}{(1-0.5)(1-0.8)} = \dfrac{1}{(0.5)(0.2)} = 10$$

**Step 3: Residue at $z=0.5$:**
$$R_2 = \dfrac{(0.5)^{n+1}}{(0.5-1)(0.5-0.8)} = \dfrac{(0.5)^{n+1}}{(-0.5)(-0.3)} = \dfrac{(0.5)^{n+1}}{0.15} = \dfrac{10}{3}(0.5)^{n+1}$$

**Step 4: Residue at $z=0.8$:**
$$R_3 = \dfrac{(0.8)^{n+1}}{(0.8-1)(0.8-0.5)} = \dfrac{(0.8)^{n+1}}{(-0.2)(0.3)} = -\dfrac{(0.8)^{n+1}}{0.06} = -\dfrac{50}{3}(0.8)^{n+1}$$

**Step 5: Final Answer.**
$$x[n] = \left[10 + \dfrac{10}{3}(0.5)^{n+1} - \dfrac{50}{3}(0.8)^{n+1}\right] u[n] \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — Internal Choice Alternative — Module 3]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Find $x[n]$ via long division for:
> $$X(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.5$$
> Compute the first 5 samples.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** If $X(z) = \dfrac{2 - z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$ with ROC $|z| < 0.5$, determine $x[n]$ and comment on stability.

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Long Division:

Perform polynomial division: $1 \div (1 - 0.8z^{-1} + 0.15z^{-2})$.

$$\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= 1 + 0.8z^{-1} + (0.8^2 - 0.15)z^{-2} + \cdots \\
     &= 1 + 0.8z^{-1} + 0.49z^{-2} + 0.392z^{-3} + 0.3136z^{-4} + \cdots
\end{aligned}$$

Reading coefficients:
- $x[0] = 1$
- $x[1] = 0.8$
- $x[2] = 0.49$
- $x[3] = 0.392$
- $x[4] = 0.3136$ **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Step 1:** Long division gives:
$$X(z) = \dfrac{2 - z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} = (2 - z^{-1})(1 + 0.5z^{-1} + 0.25z^{-2} + \cdots)$$
$$= 2 + (1 - 1)z^{-1} + (-0.5)z^{-2} + \cdots = 2 + 0 \cdot z^{-1} - 0.5 z^{-2} + \cdots$$

**Step 2:** ROC $|z| < 0.5$ means left-sided sequence. Rewrite:

$$X(z) = -\dfrac{(2 - z^{-1})}{0.5 z^{-1} - 1} \cdot (-1) = \dfrac{2 - z^{-1}}{0.5 z^{-1}(1 - 2 z^{-1})}$$

More cleanly, since ROC is **interior**, the pole $z = 0.5$ contributes a left-sided term:
$$x[n] = -2(0.5)^n u[-n-1] + (0.5)^{n-1} u[-n-1]$$

**Step 3: Stability Comment.** Since ROC ($|z| < 0.5$) does **not include** the unit circle, the system is **UNSTABLE**. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $z^{n-1}$ in residue method** — a single missing $z$ factor in $X(z) \, z^{n-1}$ will cascade into every residue calculation (loses 3-4 marks).
> 2. **Wrong sidedness based on ROC** — students often write $a^n u[n]$ even when ROC is interior. Always identify ROC **first**, then assign the standard pair.
> 3. **Skipping verification step** — examiners reward $A + B = $ numerator constant check.
> 4. **Confusing $z^{-1}$ form with $z$ form** in PFE — use the form matching the standard table.
> 5. **Missing stability comment** — KTU expects explicit commentary when ROC vs. unit circle is involved (often worth 1-2 marks in part b).
> 6. **Not simplifying $(0.5)^{n+1}$** to $(0.5)^n \cdot 0.5$ when asked in closed form.

---
