# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the transfer function of a discrete-time LTI system. Explain the significance of the Region of Convergence (ROC) in determining causality and stability.**

**Model Answer:**

The transfer function $H(z)$ of a discrete-time LTI system is defined as the ratio of the Z-transform of the output $y[n]$ to the Z-transform of the input $x[n]$, assuming zero initial conditions:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)}$$

The Region of Convergence (ROC) is the set of all values of $z$ in the complex plane for which the Z-transform converges.

**Significance of ROC:**
- **Causality:** If the ROC is the region **outside** the outermost pole (i.e., $\vert z \vert > \vert p \vert_{\max}$), the system is **right-sided (causal)**.
- **Stability:** A system is BIBO stable **if and only if** the ROC **includes the unit circle** ($\vert z \vert = 1$).
- A system is **stable and causal** only when **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle**.

> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + ROC definition (1) + Causality & Stability connection (1) = **3 marks**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between FIR and IIR systems in terms of impulse response, transfer function structure, and phase response.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | FIR System | IIR System |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Impulse response $h[n]$ | Finite length ($h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ and $n > M$) | Infinite length (theoretically) |
| Transfer function | Denominator $A(z) = 1$; all $a_k = 0$ | Denominator $A(z)$ has at least one non-trivial factor |
| Recursion | Non-recursive (no feedback) | Recursive (output fed back) |
| Phase response | Can be exactly **linear phase** if $h[n]$ is symmetric | Generally **non-linear** phase |
| Stability | Always stable (if coefficients are bounded) | Stable only if **all poles inside** the unit circle |

> **Marks Distribution:** Impulse response distinction (1) + Transfer function distinction (1) + Phase distinction (1) = **3 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks Total)

### **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]**
**Question A:**

**(a)** For the LTI system described by the difference equation:

$$y[n] - 0.9 y[n-1] + 0.2 y[n-2] = x[n] + x[n-1]$$

**(i)** Determine the transfer function $H(z)$.
**(ii)** Sketch the pole-zero plot.
**(iii)** Determine whether the system is causal and stable. Justify your answer.
**(iv)** Find the impulse response $h[n]$ assuming the system is causal. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** For the same system, obtain the magnitude response $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ and plot it qualitatively. Identify the type of filter (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, etc.). **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Step 1 — Take Z-transform of the difference equation:**

$$Y(z) - 0.9 z^{-1} Y(z) + 0.2 z^{-2} Y(z) = X(z) + z^{-1} X(z)$$

**Step 2 — Factor and form the transfer function:**

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - 0.9 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^2$ for positive powers:**

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2 + z}{z^2 - 0.9 z + 0.2} = \frac{z(z + 1)}{z^2 - 0.9 z + 0.2}$$

**Step 4 — Find the poles from the denominator quadratic $z^2 - 0.9z + 0.2 = 0$:**

Using the quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{0.9 \pm \sqrt{0.81 - 0.8}}{2} = \frac{0.9 \pm \sqrt{0.01}}{2} = \frac{0.9 \pm 0.1}{2}$$

Therefore:
- $p_1 = \frac{0.9 + 0.1}{2} = 0.5$
- $p_2 = \frac{0.9 - 0.1}{2} = 0.4$

**Step 5 — Identify zeros:**

The numerator $z(z+1)$ gives zeros at $z = 0$ and $z = -1$.

**Step 6 — Sketch pole-zero plot:**

```
            Imaginary axis
                  │
              × ──┼── ×     ← zeros at z = -1 (left) and... wait, 0 and -1
                  │
       ──────────┼──────────  Real axis
                  │
              ●──●           ← poles at 0.4 and 0.5
```

Both poles are at $0.4$ and $0.5$ on the **positive real axis**, well inside the unit circle.

**Step 7 — Determine causality and stability:**

- **Causal:** ROC is $\vert z \vert > 0.5$ (exterior of outermost pole) ✓
- **Stable:** Both $\vert p_1 \vert = 0.5 < 1$ and $\vert p_2 \vert = 0.4 < 1$, so ROC includes the unit circle ✓
- **Conclusion:** The system is **causal and stable**.

**Step 8 — Find impulse response via partial fractions:**

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 0.4 z^{-1})} = \frac{A}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - 0.4 z^{-1}}$$

Solving for $A$ (cover up $(1 - 0.5z^{-1})$, set $z^{-1} = 2$):

$$A = \frac{1 + 2}{1 - 0.4(2)} = \frac{3}{0.2} = 15$$

Solving for $B$ (cover up $(1 - 0.4z^{-1})$, set $z^{-1} = 2.5$):

$$B = \frac{1 + 2.5}{1 - 0.5(2.5)} = \frac{3.5}{-0.25} = -14$$

Verification: $A + B = 15 - 14 = 1$ ✓ (matches the leading coefficient)

Applying the inverse Z-transform:

$$\boxed{h[n] = \bigl[15 (0.5)^n - 14 (0.4)^n\bigr] u[n]}$$

---

### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Step 1 — Evaluate $H(z)$ on the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$:**

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 + e^{-j\omega}}{(1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega})(1 - 0.4 e^{-j\omega})}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega}$ to clear half the exponentials (numerator becomes $1 + e^{j\omega}$):**

Actually, a cleaner form: let $z = e^{j\omega}$, so $z^{-1} = e^{-j\omega}$.

**Numerator magnitude:** $\vert 1 + e^{-j\omega} \vert = \sqrt{(1 + \cos\omega)^2 + \sin^2\omega} = \sqrt{2 + 2\cos\omega} = 2\vert\cos(\omega/2)\vert$

This is maximum at $\omega = 0$ (value = 2) and zero at $\omega = \pm\pi$ (value = 0).

**Step 3 — Denominator magnitude analysis:** Each factor $(1 - p e^{-j\omega})$ has magnitude:

$$\vert 1 - p e^{-j\omega} \vert = \sqrt{(1 - p\cos\omega)^2 + p^2 \sin^2 \omega} = \sqrt{1 - 2p\cos\omega + p^2}$$

For $p = 0.5$: $V_1(\omega) = \sqrt{1.25 - \cos\omega}$
For $p = 0.4$: $V_2(\omega) = \sqrt{1.16 - 0.8 \cos\omega}$

**Step 4 — Final magnitude expression:**

$$\bigl\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \bigr\vert = \frac{2 \vert\cos(\omega/2) \vert}{\sqrt{(1.25 - \cos\omega)(1.16 - 0.8 \cos\omega)}}$$

**Step 5 — Qualitative behavior:**

- At $\omega = 0$: numerator = 2; denominator $\approx \sqrt{0.25 \cdot 0.36} = 0.3$; hence $\vert H \vert \approx 6.67$ (large)
- At $\omega = \pi$: numerator = 0; hence $\vert H \vert = 0$ (zero)
- At $\omega = \pi/2$: $\cos(\pi/4) \approx 0.707$, numerator ≈ 1.414; denominator $\approx \sqrt{1.25 \cdot 1.16} \approx 1.20$; hence $\vert H \vert \approx 1.18$

**Conclusion:** The magnitude is **large near $\omega = 0$** (DC) and **decreases monotonically** to zero at $\omega = \pi$ (Nyquist). This is the signature of a **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)**.

```
|H(e^jω)|
    │
  7 ┤●
    │ \
    │  \
  3 ┤   \
    │    \___
  1 ┤        \___
    │            \_____
  0 ┤________________●_____► ω
    0      π/2       π
```

---

## 5.3 Part B Question — Choice B (Alternative, 14 Marks Total)

### **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]**
**Question B:**

**(a)** A causal LTI system is described by the transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.8 e^{j\pi/4} z^{-1})(1 - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4} z^{-1})}$$

**(i)** Identify the poles and zeros of the system.
**(ii)** Sketch the pole-zero plot and comment on stability.
**(iii)** Determine if this is a low-pass, high-pass, or band-pass filter. Justify.
**(iv)** Compute the impulse response $h[n]$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A discrete-time signal $x[n] = 2\cos(0.5\pi n)$ is applied to the same system. Using the DTFT, find the steady-state output $y_{\text{ss}}[n]$. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Step 1 — Identify poles:**

The poles are at:

$$p_1 = 0.8 e^{j\pi/4}, \quad p_2 = 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4}$$

These are complex conjugates with magnitude $\vert p_1 \vert = \vert p_2 \vert = 0.8 < 1$.

**Step 2 — Identify zeros:**

There are no finite zeros. However, for the causal system with $M = 0$ and $N = 2$, there are $N - M = 2$ zeros at $z = 0$ (origin).

**Step 3 — Pole-zero plot:**

```
              Imaginary
                  │
              ×   │   ×        ← zeros at origin (×2, multiplicity 2)
                  │
       ──────────┼──────────  Real
            ●    │    ●        ← poles at 0.8e^(±jπ/4)
                  │
```

Both poles are inside the unit circle. The system is **stable and causal**.

**Step 4 — Determine filter type:**

The poles lie at angles $\pm\pi/4$ from the positive real axis. As $\omega$ sweeps the unit circle from $0$ to $2\pi$, the denominator term becomes smallest (and hence $\vert H \vert$ largest) when $\omega$ is **closest** to the pole angle, i.e., near $\omega = \pi/4$ and $\omega = -\pi/4$ (or equivalently $7\pi/4$).

Therefore, this is a **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** centered around $\omega_0 = \pi/4$.

**Step 5 — Compute impulse response:**

Multiply out the denominator:

$$(1 - 0.8 e^{j\pi/4} z^{-1})(1 - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4} z^{-1}) = 1 - 0.8(e^{j\pi/4} + e^{-j\pi/4}) z^{-1} + 0.64 z^{-2}$$

$$= 1 - 1.6 \cos(\pi/4) \, z^{-1} + 0.64 z^{-2} = 1 - \frac{1.6}{\sqrt{2}} z^{-1} + 0.64 z^{-2}$$

$$= 1 - 0.8\sqrt{2}\, z^{-1} + 0.64 z^{-2}$$

Using partial fractions:

$$H(z) = \frac{A}{1 - 0.8 e^{j\pi/4} z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4} z^{-1}}$$

where $A = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4} \cdot 0.8 e^{j\pi/4}} \cdot \text{(adjustment)}$

A cleaner approach: use the standard inverse pair. The impulse response of a system with complex-conjugate poles $p = r e^{\pm j\theta}$ is:

$$h[n] = \frac{r^n \sin[(n+1)\theta]}{\sin\theta} u[n]$$

Here $r = 0.8$, $\theta = \pi/4$, so:

$$\boxed{h[n] = \frac{(0.8)^n \sin\!\left[(n+1)\pi/4\right]}{\sin(\pi/4)} u[n] = \sqrt{2}\,(0.8)^n \sin\!\left[(n+1)\pi/4\right] u[n]}$$

---

### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Step 1 — Recall the DTFT property:** A sinusoidal input $x[n] = A\cos(\omega_0 n)$ produces steady-state output:

$$y_{\text{ss}}[n] = A \vert H(e^{j\omega_0}) \vert \cos\bigl(\omega_0 n + \angle H(e^{j\omega_0})\bigr)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $H(z)$ at $z = e^{j0.5\pi}$:**

$$H(e^{j0.5\pi}) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.8 e^{j\pi/4} e^{-j0.5\pi})(1 - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4} e^{-j0.5\pi})}$$

Simplify exponents:
- $e^{j\pi/4} \cdot e^{-j\pi/2} = e^{-j\pi/4}$
- $e^{-j\pi/4} \cdot e^{-j\pi/2} = e^{-j3\pi/4}$

$$H(e^{j0.5\pi}) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4})(1 - 0.8 e^{-j3\pi/4})}$$

**Step 3 — Compute magnitudes:**

$\vert 1 - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4} \vert = \sqrt{(1 - 0.8\cos(\pi/4))^2 + (0.8\sin(\pi/4))^2} = \sqrt{1 - 1.6\cos(\pi/4) + 0.64}$

$$= \sqrt{1 - 0.8\sqrt{2} + 0.64} = \sqrt{1.64 - 1.1314} = \sqrt{0.5086} \approx 0.713$$

$\vert 1 - 0.8 e^{-j3\pi/4} \vert = \sqrt{1 - 1.6\cos(3\pi/4) + 0.64} = \sqrt{1.64 + 1.1314} = \sqrt{2.7714} \approx 1.665$

$$\vert H(e^{j0.5\pi}) \vert = \frac{1}{0.713 \times 1.665} \approx \frac{1}{1.187} \approx 0.842$$

**Step 4 — Compute phases:**

$\phi_1 = \angle(1 - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4}) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.8 \sin(\pi/4)}{1 - 0.8 \cos(\pi/4)}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.5657}{0.4343}\right) \approx 0.917$ rad

$\phi_2 = \angle(1 - 0.8 e^{-j3\pi/4}) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.8 \sin(3\pi/4)}{1 - 0.8 \cos(3\pi/4)}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.5657}{1.5657}\right) \approx 0.347$ rad

$\angle H(e^{j0.5\pi}) = -(\phi_1 + \phi_2) \approx -(0.917 + 0.347) = -1.264$ rad $\approx -72.4°$

**Step 5 — Write the steady-state output:**

$$x[n] = 2\cos(0.5\pi n), \quad A = 2$$

$$\boxed{y_{\text{ss}}[n] \approx 1.684 \cos\!\left(0.5\pi n - 1.264\right)}$$

The input amplitude of 2 is scaled by $\vert H \vert \approx 0.842$ and phase-shifted by about $-72.4°$.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify ROC:** A transfer function without ROC is **incomplete** in KTU valuation. You may lose **2 marks** if you state $H(z)$ but don't mention the ROC.
>
> 2. **Confusing causal stability criteria:** Students often write *"system is stable because all poles are inside the unit circle"*. This is **only true if the system is also causal**. Always state both conditions explicitly.
>
> 3. **Sign errors in partial fractions:** The cover-up method requires you to substitute the **reciprocal of the pole's $z^{-1}$ value**. Forgetting the inversion step is a frequent source of **arithmetic error**.
>
> 4. **DC gain mistakes:** When asked for "DC gain," substitute $z = 1$ (or $\omega = 0$), **not** $z = 0$.
>
> 5. **Phase wrap-around:** The $\arctan$ function only returns values in $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$. For full phase, use the `atan2` formulation and add $\pm\pi$ as needed to maintain **continuity**. Examiners deduct marks for discontinuous phase plots.
>
> 6. **Inverse DTFT confusion:** The inverse DTFT is $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}$, not $\frac{1}{\pi}$ and not $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi}$. The latter two yield **incorrect** time-domain signals.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Discrete-Time LTI System Analysis**

- **System function** $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ uniquely characterizes a causal LTI system **together with its ROC**.
- **Causal system:** ROC is $\vert z \vert > \vert p \vert_{\max}$ (exterior of outermost pole).
- **Stable system (BIBO):** ROC **must include the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- **Stable + Causal:** All poles satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ (strictly inside unit circle).
- **FIR filter:** Denominator = 1; only zeros, no poles (except possibly at origin).
- **IIR filter:** Has at least one non-trivial pole; impulse response is theoretically infinite.
- **Frequency response:** $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z)\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$ — valid **only** for stable systems.
- **Magnitude response** $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$: tells you which frequencies pass/are attenuated.
- **Phase response** $\angle H(e^{j\omega})$: tells you the delay introduced at each frequency.
- **Group delay** $\tau_g(\omega) = -d\angle H / d\omega$: **constant** for linear-phase (FIR symmetric) filters.
- **Steady-state response to sinusoid:** $A\cos(\omega_0 n) \to A \vert H(e^{j\omega_0}) \vert \cos(\omega_0 n + \angle H(e^{j\omega_0}))$.
- **Geometric series identity:** $\frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} \;\longleftrightarrow\; a^n u[n]$ (ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$).
- **Resonance occurs** when frequency $\omega$ aligns with pole angle $\theta_p$.
- **Notches occur** when frequency $\omega$ aligns with zero angle $\theta_z$.
- **Pole-zero pairing rule:** Poles close to the unit circle → sharp resonance peaks (high-Q filter).
- **All-pass filter:** $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 1$ for all $\omega$ — magnitude is unity, but phase varies.
- **Convolution-multiplication duality:** Time-domain convolution $\Leftrightarrow$ Z-domain multiplication.
- **Robotics applications:** Pole placement in closed-loop motor control, IIR notch filters for powerline interference, FIR filters for audio preprocessing, all-pass equalizers for phase correction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** is a fundamental mathematical transformation that converts a finite-length, discrete-time signal $x[n]$ of length $N$ from the **time domain** into its corresponding representation in the **frequency domain**, producing a finite-length, discrete-frequency sequence $X[k]$ of length $N$.

The DFT is formally defined as:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn/N}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

The corresponding **Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT)** is given by:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j2\pi kn/N}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

The term $e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ is commonly referred to as the **twiddle factor**, denoted as $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$, so we can write $W_N^{kn} = e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Analogy (Robotics-Context)**
> Imagine a **robotic manipulator** receiving a continuous stream of vibration signals from a motor (the time domain). The engineer wants to understand **which frequencies of vibration are present** (e.g., is there a 50 Hz hum? A 200 Hz resonance?).
> - The **time-domain signal** is like a smoothie — a blended mix of many fruits (frequencies) that you cannot separate visually.
> - The **DFT** is like a sieve that separates the smoothie back into individual fruits, telling you **exactly how much of each frequency component** is present in the signal.
> - The output $X[k]$ tells you the **"amount" (magnitude)** and **"timing offset" (phase)** of each frequency ingredient $k$ in the original signal.

In robotics applications, the DFT is the **mathematical backbone** of:
- **Spectral analysis** of IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) sensor data
- **Vibration monitoring** of robotic joints
- **Feature extraction** from LiDAR and camera signals
- **Frequency-domain filtering** for noise reduction in control loops

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in DFT-Based Signal Processing for Robotics**
> - **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$):** measured in **Hertz (Hz)**; typical values: 1 kHz to 100 kHz.
> - **Signal Length ($N$):** dimensionless integer; total number of samples.
> - **Frequency Resolution ($\Delta f$):** $\Delta f = f_s / N$ in **Hz** per bin.
> - **Twiddle Factor Magnitude:** $\vert W_N^{kn} \vert = 1$ (always lies on the unit circle).
> - **Normalization Constant:** $1/N$ appears in the IDFT for energy conservation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of DFT Operation

The DFT operation can be understood as a **bank of $N$ parallel correlators**, each tuned to a specific discrete frequency $k$.

- **Step 1: Input Sampling**
  The continuous-time signal from a robot sensor (e.g., accelerometer, encoder) is sampled at $f_s$ Hz to produce $N$ discrete samples $x[0], x[1], \dots, x[N-1]$.

- **Step 2: Frequency Bin Generation**
  Each output index $k$ represents a discrete frequency: $f_k = k \cdot f_s / N$. The DFT computes $N$ frequency bins from $k=0$ to $k=N-1$.

- **Step 3: Complex Exponential Multiplication**
  For each bin $k$, the signal $x[n]$ is multiplied by the complex exponential $W_N^{kn}$. This is essentially projecting the signal onto a complex sinusoid of frequency $f_k$.

- **Step 4: Summation (Correlation)**
  The products are summed over all $N$ samples. This is equivalent to computing the **inner product** of the signal with the complex sinusoid — a measure of similarity.

- **Step 5: Output Formation**
  The result $X[k]$ is a **complex number** containing both magnitude and phase information at frequency $f_k$.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind the DFT

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the DFT work?**
> Any finite-length discrete signal $x[n]$ can be expressed as a **linear combination** of $N$ orthogonal complex exponentials $\{e^{j2\pi kn/N}\}_{k=0}^{N-1}$. The DFT simply finds the **coefficients** of this expansion — analogous to how the Fourier Series finds coefficients for periodic signals.

**Key engineering insight for robotics:**
- The DFT operates on a **finite, periodic extension** of the signal. This is why a robotic vibration signal of length $N=1024$ is implicitly treated as **periodic with period 1024**.
- Spectral leakage occurs when the true signal frequency does not align exactly with a DFT bin, causing energy to "leak" into adjacent bins — a critical issue in precision robotic sensor analysis.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table — these are the most tested formulas in KTU ESE.**

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| DFT | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot W_N^{kn}$ | Forward transform |
| IDFT | $x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot W_N^{-kn}$ | Inverse transform |
| Twiddle Factor | $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ | Complex primitive $N$-th root of unity |
| Twiddle Property | $W_N^{k+N/2} = -W_N^{k}$ | Anti-symmetry at $N/2$ |
| Twiddle Periodicity | $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^{k}$ | Periodicity in $k$ |
| Frequency Bin | $f_k = k \cdot f_s / N$ | Hz corresponding to bin $k$ |
| Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = f_s / N$ | Spacing between bins |
| Nyquist Frequency | $f_{Nyq} = f_s/2$ | Maximum representable frequency |
| Magnitude Spectrum | $\vert X[k] \vert = \sqrt{\text{Re}^2\{X[k]\} + \text{Im}^2\{X[k]\}}$ | Amplitude vs. frequency |
| Phase Spectrum | $\angle X[k] = \arctan\left(\text{Im}\{X[k]\} \,/\, \text{Re}\{X[k]\}\right)$ | Phase vs. frequency |
| Parseval's Theorem | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$ | Energy conservation |
| Circular Convolution | $x[n] \circledast h[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \cdot h[(n-m) \mod N]$ | Time-domain circular convolution |
| DFT of Circular Convolution | $\text{DFT}\{x[n] \circledast h[n]\} = X[k] \cdot H[k]$ | Convolution theorem |
| DFT of Linear Convolution | $Y[k] = X[k] \cdot H[k]$ (after zero-padding to length $\geq N_1 + N_2 - 1$) | Achieved via zero-padding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTATION RULE (For Examiners):**
> In KTU answer scripts, always use the **exponential form** $e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ for derivations, and the **twiddle form** $W_N^{kn}$ for compactness. Examiners explicitly reward this notation discipline.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Robotics:** Vibration analysis of robot arms, sensor denoising via frequency-domain filtering, speech recognition for human-robot interaction.
- **Audio Processing:** MP3 compression, equalizers, noise-cancelling headphones.
- **Biomedical:** ECG and EEG signal analysis (heart rate variability, seizure detection).
- **Communications:** OFDM modulation in 4G/5G, spectrum sensing in cognitive radio.
- **Image Processing:** JPEG compression (2D-DFT), edge detection, denoising.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Deriving the IDFT from the DFT

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This derivation is a KTU **favourite** for 7-mark sub-questions. Mastering this is essential.

**Starting Point — The Forward DFT:**

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$$

**Goal:** Recover $x[n]$ from the set $\{X[k]\}_{k=0}^{N-1}$.

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides of the DFT equation by $e^{j2\pi km/N}$ and sum over $k$ from $0$ to $N-1$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j2\pi km/N} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn/N} \right] e^{j2\pi km/N}$$

**Step 2:** Swap the order of summation (a standard double-sum manipulation):

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j2\pi km/N} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j2\pi k(m-n)/N}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the inner sum — this is the **key orthogonality step**:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j2\pi k(m-n)/N} = \begin{cases} N, & \text{if } (m-n) \mod N = 0 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the Kronecker delta property. The inner sum is $N$ when $m = n$ (mod $N$), and $0$ otherwise. Therefore, only the term $n = m$ survives:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j2\pi km/N} = x[m] \cdot N$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by $N$ to obtain the IDFT:

$$x[m] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j2\pi km/N}$$

**Step 6:** Renaming $m \to n$ gives the standard IDFT formula:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j2\pi kn/N}$$

**Conclusion:** The orthogonality of complex exponentials is the **mathematical heart** of the DFT-IDFT pair.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a typical 14-mark KTU ESE question. Work through every step.**

**Problem:** Compute the 4-point DFT of the signal $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$, where $x[0]=1, x[1]=2, x[2]=3, x[3]=4$.

**Given:** $N = 4$, so $W_4 = e^{-j2\pi/4} = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$.

**Pre-compute all twiddle factors:**

$$W_4^0 = 1, \quad W_4^1 = -j, \quad W_4^2 = -1, \quad W_4^3 = j$$

**Step 1: Compute $X[0]$** (DC component, $k=0$):

$$X[0] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] \cdot W_4^{0 \cdot n} = 1\cdot 1 + 2\cdot 1 + 3\cdot 1 + 4\cdot 1 = 10$$

**Step 2: Compute $X[1]$** ($k=1$):

$$\begin{aligned} X[1] &= \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] \cdot W_4^{1 \cdot n} = x[0]W_4^0 + x[1]W_4^1 + x[2]W_4^2 + x[3]W_4^3 \\ &= (1)(1) + (2)(-j) + (3)(-1) + (4)(j) \\ &= 1 - 2j - 3 + 4j = -2 + 2j \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute $X[2]$** ($k=2$):

$$\begin{aligned} X[2] &= \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] \cdot W_4^{2 \cdot n} = (1)(1) + (2)(-1) + (3)(1) + (4)(-1) \\ &= 1 - 2 + 3 - 4 = -2 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute $X[3]$** ($k=3$):

$$\begin{aligned} X[3] &= \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] \cdot W_4^{3 \cdot n} = (1)(1) + (2)(j) + (3)(-1) + (4)(-j) \\ &= 1 + 2j - 3 - 4j = -2 - 2j \end{aligned}$$

**Final 4-point DFT:**

$$X[k] = \{10, \quad -2+2j, \quad -2, \quad -2-2j\}$$

**Verification (optional but high-value in KTU):** Note the conjugate symmetry $X[3] = X[1]^*$ since $x[n]$ is real-valued. ✓

**Magnitude and Phase Spectrum:**

| **Bin $k$** | **$X[k]$** | **$\vert X[k] \vert$** | **$\angle X[k]$ (radians)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 10 | 10.000 | 0.0000 |
| 1 | $-2+2j$ | $\sqrt{8} = 2.828$ | $\arctan(2/-2) = 3\pi/4$ |
| 2 | $-2$ | 2.000 | $\pi$ |
| 3 | $-2-2j$ | $\sqrt{8} = 2.828$ | $-3\pi/4$ |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DFT Symmetry Properties

| **Property** | **Mathematical Form** | **Robotics Application** |
|---|---|---|
| Linearity | $\text{DFT}\{a \cdot x[n] + b \cdot y[n]\} = a \cdot X[k] + b \cdot Y[k]$ | Combining accelerometer + gyroscope signals |
| Time Shift | $\text{DFT}\{x[n-n_0]\} = X[k] \cdot W_N^{kn_0}$ | Time-aligning sensor samples |
| Frequency Shift | $\text{DFT}\{x[n] \cdot e^{j2\pi k_0 n/N}\} = X[(k-k_0) \mod N]$ | Shifting spectrum to baseband |
| Conjugate Symmetry (real $x[n]$) | $X[N-k] = X[k]^*$ | Half-spectrum suffices — saves computation |
| Circular Convolution | $\text{DFT}\{x[n] \circledast h[n]\} = X[k] \cdot H[k]$ | Fast filtering in robotic control loops |
| Parseval's Theorem | $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum \vert X[k] \vert^2$ | Computing signal power (vibration energy) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DFT Computation Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Sensor Signal xn] --> B[Sampler: ADC at fs Hz]
    B --> C[Frame Buffer: Collect N Samples]
    C --> D[For each k = 0 to N-1]
    D --> E[Initialize Sum = 0]
    E --> F[For each n = 0 to N-1]
    F --> G[Compute Twiddle W to the power of kn]
    G --> H[Accumulate Sum = Sum + xn times Wkn]
    H --> I{n equals N minus 1}
    I -->|No| F
    I -->|Yes| J[Store Xk = Sum]
    J --> K{k equals N minus 1}
    K -->|No| D
    K -->|Yes| L[Output Spectrum Xk for k = 0, 1, ..., N-1]
    L --> M[Compute Magnitude and Phase]
    M --> N[Frequency Domain Analysis for Robotics]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DFT-Based Robotic Vibration Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Robot Joint Motor] --> S2[Vibration Sensor Accelerometer]
    S2 --> S3[Analog Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    S3 --> S4[Analog to Digital Converter]
    S4 --> S5[Frame Buffer N Samples]
    S5 --> S6[DFT Computation Engine]
    S6 --> S7[Magnitude Spectrum]
    S6 --> S8[Phase Spectrum]
    S7 --> S9[Peak Frequency Detector]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Anomaly Detection Logic]
    S10 --> S11[Predictive Maintenance Alert]
    S10 --> S12[Adaptive Control Loop Adjustment]
```

## 4.3 Twiddle Factor Symmetry Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Twiddle Factor WN to the power of kn] --> B[Symmetry Property 1]
    A --> C[Symmetry Property 2]
    A --> D[Periodicity Property]
    B --> B1[WN to the power of k plus N/2 equals minus WN to the power of k]
    C --> C1[WN to the power of k equals WN to the power of N minus k complex conjugate]
    D --> D1[WN to the power of k plus N equals WN to the power of k]
    B1 --> E[Exploited in FFT Algorithms]
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> E
    E --> F[Reduces DFT Complexity from N squared to N log N operations]
```

## 4.4 DFT Spectrum Interpretation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DFT Output Xk] --> B[Is Signal Real Valued?]
    B -->|Yes| C[Conjugate Symmetry Exists]
    B -->|No| D[Full Spectrum Required]
    C --> E[Only First N/2 plus 1 Bins Needed]
    D --> F[All N Bins Independent]
    E --> G[Positive Frequencies Only: k = 0 to N/2]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Plot Magnitude Spectrum versus Frequency]
    G --> I[Plot Phase Spectrum versus Frequency]
    H --> J[Identify Dominant Frequencies]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Robotic Decision: Anomaly / Normal / Adjust Control]
```
