# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) of a sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$. State the IDFT expression also.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **DFT Definition (2 Marks):**
> The DFT of a finite-length sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$ is defined as:
> $$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn/N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$
> It transforms the discrete time-domain signal $x[n]$ into the discrete frequency-domain representation $X[k]$.

> **IDFT Expression (1 Mark):**
> $$x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j2\pi kn/N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$
> The IDFT recovers the original time-domain sequence from its DFT coefficients.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is a twiddle factor? State any two properties of twiddle factors used in DFT computation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Twiddle Factor Definition (1 Mark):**
> The twiddle factor $W_N$ is defined as $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$. It is a complex $N$-th root of unity and appears in the DFT/IDFT kernels: $W_N^{kn} = e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$.

> **Properties of Twiddle Factors (2 Marks — state any two):**
> 1. **Periodicity:** $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^k$
> 2. **Symmetry:** $W_N^{k+N/2} = -W_N^k$
> 3. **Conjugate Symmetry:** $W_N^{N-k} = W_N^{k*} = (W_N^k)^*$
> 4. **Magnitude:** $\vert W_N^k \vert = 1$ for all $k$ (lies on the unit circle in the complex plane).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the IDFT expression from the DFT definition, clearly showing the use of the orthogonality property of complex exponentials. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the 8-point DFT of the signal $x[n] = \{1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0\}$. Show all steps and state the magnitude spectrum. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part A(a): IDFT Derivation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Multiply both sides by $e^{j2\pi km/N}$ and sum over $k$ from $0$ to $N-1$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j2\pi km/N} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j2\pi kn/N}\right] e^{j2\pi km/N}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Exchange the order of summation:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] e^{j2\pi km/N} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j2\pi k(m-n)/N}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply the orthogonality principle:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j2\pi k(m-n)/N} = \begin{cases} N, & m = n \pmod{N} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Only the term $n = m$ survives on the right-hand side:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] e^{j2\pi km/N} = N \cdot x[m]$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Divide by $N$ and rename $m \to n$:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j2\pi kn/N}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** This is the IDFT. The orthogonality of complex exponentials ensures the inverse exists and is unique.

---

#### Model Solution to Part A(b): 8-Point DFT Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $x[n] = \{1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0\}$, $N=8$.

**Twiddle Factor (1 Mark):** $W_8 = e^{-j2\pi/8} = e^{-j\pi/4}$

**Key Observation (1 Mark):** Since $x[n] = \delta[n]$ (Kronecker delta), the DFT reduces to:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{7} \delta[n] \cdot W_8^{kn} = W_8^{k \cdot 0} = W_8^0 = 1$$

**General Formula (1 Mark):** For $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 7$:

$$X[k] = 1 \quad \text{for all } k$$

**Result (1 Mark):** $X[k] = \{1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1\}$

**Magnitude Spectrum (2 Marks):** $\vert X[k] \vert = 1$ for all $k = 0, 1, \dots, 7$.

**Engineering Interpretation (1 Mark):** A unit impulse in the time domain has a **flat (uniform) spectrum** in the frequency domain — it contains **equal energy at all frequencies**. This is the foundation of why impulse signals are used to test robotic system frequency response.

---

### Question B (Option 2): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** State and prove the **time-shifting property** of the DFT. Hence, compute the 4-point DFT of $x[n] = \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ using the result of the DFT of $y[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 0\}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **circular convolution** in DFT. Compute the circular convolution of $x[n] = \{1, 2, 0, 1\}$ and $h[n] = \{2, 2, 1, 1\}$ using the DFT method. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part B(a): Time-Shifting Property + DFT Computation (7 Marks)

**Statement (1 Mark):** If $\text{DFT}\{x[n]\} = X[k]$, then:
$$\text{DFT}\{x[(n-n_0) \mod N]\} = X[k] \cdot W_N^{k n_0}$$

**Proof (3 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned} \text{DFT}\{x[(n-n_0) \mod N]\} &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[(n-n_0) \mod N] \cdot W_N^{kn} \end{aligned}$$

Let $m = (n - n_0) \mod N$. Then $n = (m + n_0) \mod N$ and as $n$ ranges over $0, \dots, N-1$, so does $m$:

$$= \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \cdot W_N^{k(m+n_0)} = W_N^{kn_0} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] W_N^{km} = W_N^{kn_0} \cdot X[k]$$

**DFT Computation (3 Marks):**

**First:** Compute DFT of $y[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 0\}$ with $N=4$:

$$Y[0] = 1+2+3+0 = 6$$
$$Y[1] = (1)(1) + (2)(-j) + (3)(-1) + (0)(j) = -2 - 2j$$
$$Y[2] = (1)(1) + (2)(-1) + (3)(1) + (0)(-1) = 2$$
$$Y[3] = (1)(1) + (2)(j) + (3)(-1) + (0)(-j) = -2 + 2j$$

So $Y[k] = \{6, -2-2j, 2, -2+2j\}$.

**Now:** Notice that $x[n] = \{0, 1, 2, 3\} = y[(n+1) \mod 4]$. This is a **cyclic left shift by 1**. By the time-shifting property:

$$X[k] = W_4^{-k} \cdot Y[k]$$

With $W_4^k$ values: $W_4^0 = 1, W_4^1 = -j, W_4^2 = -1, W_4^3 = j$, so $W_4^{-k}$ is the conjugate:

$$X[0] = 1 \cdot 6 = 6$$
$$X[1] = j \cdot (-2-2j) = -2j - 2j^2 = 2 - 2j$$
$$X[2] = -1 \cdot 2 = -2$$
$$X[3] = -j \cdot (-2+2j) = 2j - 2j^2 = 2 + 2j$$

**Result:** $X[k] = \{6, 2-2j, -2, 2+2j\}$ ✓

---

#### Model Solution to Part B(b): Circular Convolution via DFT (7 Marks)

**Concept (2 Marks):** Circular convolution of two length-$N$ sequences is defined as:

$$y[n] = x[n] \circledast h[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \cdot h[(n-m) \mod N]$$

It can be computed efficiently using the DFT as $y[n] = \text{IDFT}\{X[k] \cdot H[k]\}$.

**Step 1: Compute DFT of $x[n] = \{1, 2, 0, 1\}$ (1 Mark):**

$$X[0] = 1+2+0+1 = 4$$
$$X[1] = (1)(1) + (2)(-j) + (0)(-1) + (1)(j) = 1 - j$$
$$X[2] = (1)(1) + (2)(-1) + (0)(1) + (1)(-1) = -2$$
$$X[3] = (1)(1) + (2)(j) + (0)(-1) + (1)(-j) = 1 + j$$

**Step 2: Compute DFT of $h[n] = \{2, 2, 1, 1\}$ (1 Mark):**

$$H[0] = 2+2+1+1 = 6$$
$$H[1] = (2)(1) + (2)(-j) + (1)(-1) + (1)(j) = 1 - j$$
$$H[2] = (2)(1) + (2)(-1) + (1)(1) + (1)(-1) = 0$$
$$H[3] = (2)(1) + (2)(j) + (1)(-1) + (1)(-j) = 1 + j$$

**Step 3: Multiply pointwise $Y[k] = X[k] \cdot H[k]$ (1 Mark):**

$$Y[0] = 4 \cdot 6 = 24$$
$$Y[1] = (1-j)(1-j) = 1 - 2j + j^2 = -2j$$
$$Y[2] = (-2)(0) = 0$$
$$Y[3] = (1+j)(1+j) = 1 + 2j + j^2 = 2j$$

**Step 4: Compute IDFT to get $y[n]$ (2 Marks):**

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=0}^{3} Y[k] \cdot W_4^{-kn}$$

$$y[0] = \frac{1}{4}(24 + (-2j)(1) + 0(1) + (2j)(1)) = \frac{1}{4}(24) = 6$$

$$y[1] = \frac{1}{4}(24 + (-2j)(j) + 0(-1) + (2j)(-j)) = \frac{1}{4}(24 + 2 + 2) = 7$$

$$y[2] = \frac{1}{4}(24 + (-2j)(-1) + 0(1) + (2j)(-1)) = \frac{1}{4}(24 + 2j - 2j) = 6$$

$$y[3] = \frac{1}{4}(24 + (-2j)(-j) + 0(-1) + (2j)(j)) = \frac{1}{4}(24 - 2 - 2) = 5$$

**Final Result:** $y[n] = x[n] \circledast h[n] = \{6, 7, 6, 5\}$ ✓

**Robotics Insight:** Circular convolution enables **fast FIR filtering** of robotic sensor data — a single FFT, pointwise multiplication, and inverse FFT can replace $O(N^2)$ time-domain convolution with $O(N \log N)$ complexity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Twiddle Factor Index Error:** Students often write $W_N^{nk}$ instead of $W_N^{kn}$. Both are mathematically equivalent, but KTU expects $W_N^{kn}$ to match the standard textbook convention. (Lose 0.5 marks)
> 2. **Missing $1/N$ in IDFT:** Forgetting the $1/N$ normalization constant is the **#1 most common mistake** in IDFT questions. This will cost you a full mark.
> 3. **Conjugate Symmetry Mistake:** For real $x[n]$, students write $X[k] = X[N-k]$ instead of $X[k] = X[N-k]^*$. The asterisk (conjugate) is **mandatory**.
> 4. **Linear vs Circular Convolution:** Students often compute linear convolution when the question asks for circular convolution. Always read carefully.
> 5. **Index Out of Range:** DFT indices are $0$ to $N-1$. Showing $k=N$ or $n=N$ indicates a fundamental misunderstanding and attracts negative marking.
> 6. **Magnitude without $j$ handling:** Writing $\vert -2+2j \vert$ as $-2+2j$ (forgetting the modulus) is a very common error in 3-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — DFT**

- **DFT Formula:** $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ — memorize both the exponential and twiddle forms.
- **IDFT Formula:** $x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] e^{j2\pi kn/N}$ — **never forget the $1/N$ factor**.
- **Twiddle Factor:** $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$, with three critical properties: periodicity, symmetry, and conjugate symmetry.
- **Frequency Bin Mapping:** $f_k = k \cdot f_s / N$ — the cornerstone for relating bin index to physical frequency.
- **Nyquist Limit:** Frequencies above $f_s/2$ cause **aliasing** — always low-pass filter before sampling in robotic systems.
- **Conjugate Symmetry:** Real-valued signals have $X[N-k] = X[k]^*$, reducing storage and computation by half.
- **DFT Circularity:** DFT inherently treats the signal as **periodic**; this leads to **circular convolution** in the frequency domain.
- **Convolution Theorem:** $Y[k] = X[k] \cdot H[k]$ enables O($N \log N$) FIR filtering — critical for real-time robotics.
- **Parseval's Theorem:** $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum \vert X[k] \vert^2$ — useful for vibration energy computation in robotic joints.
- **Computational Cost:** Direct DFT is $O(N^2)$; FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) reduces it to $O(N \log N)$ — a 1000-point signal goes from 1,000,000 to 10,000 multiplications.
- **Magnitude Spectrum:** $\vert X[k] \vert$ — tells you **how much** of each frequency is present (used for anomaly detection).
- **Phase Spectrum:** $\angle X[k]$ — tells you the **timing offset** (used for synchronization in robotic sensor fusion).
- **Zero-Padding:** Extending a signal with zeros improves frequency resolution visualization but does **not** add new information.
- **Spectral Leakage:** Occurs when signal frequency does not align with bin frequency; mitigated using windowing functions (Hamming, Hanning).
- **Robotics Applications:** IMU signal denoising, joint vibration analysis, motor fault detection, LiDAR point-cloud spectral features, audio command recognition in HRI.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computational Complexity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computational Complexity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Signal Processing for Robotics (OERAT831)**, **Computational Complexity** is formally defined as the mathematical study of the resources—primarily **time** (number of elementary operations) and **space** (memory footprint)—required by an algorithm as a function of the size of its input. It provides an asymptotic framework to classify algorithms based on how their resource consumption *scales* with input size $n$, independent of the underlying hardware.

Mathematically, it is expressed through the **Landau Notation** (Big-O family):

$$
T(n) = O(f(n)) \quad \text{iff} \quad \exists \, c > 0, \, n_0 > 0 \text{ such that } T(n) \le c \cdot f(n) \, \forall n \ge n_0
$$

For roboticists, complexity is not academic—it is the **fundamental barrier** between a working prototype and a real-time, embedded system that must process LiDAR, IMU, camera, and encoder signals within a deterministic deadline (often $1\,\text{ms}$ to $10\,\text{ms}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Computational Complexity under Module 4 expects mastery of asymptotic notations, the derivation of FFT vs. DFT complexity, recurrence analysis using the **Master Theorem**, and the application of these principles to real-time robotic signal processing pipelines (filters, transforms, Kalman updates).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Search" Intuition

Imagine a librarian searching for a specific book in a library:

| Strategy | Librarian's Action | Complexity Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Knows the shelf** | Walks directly to the shelf | $O(1)$ |
| **Sorted catalog** | Binary search through index | $O(\log n)$ |
| **Sequential scan** | Checks every book one-by-one | $O(n)$ |
| **Duplicate + compare** | Compares every pair of books | $O(n^2)$ |
| **Brute permutations** | Tries every possible arrangement | $O(n!)$ |

A robot processing a signal of $N$ samples faces the same trade-off: a **smart algorithm** (FFT, $O(N \log N)$) finishes in microseconds, while a **naive algorithm** (DFT, $O(N^2)$) can miss its real-time deadline and cause the robot to "freeze" or oscillate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-Time Robotics Mandate:** A mobile robot navigating at $1\,\text{m/s}$ cannot afford to "lag" even $100\,\text{ms}$ in its perception loop. Thus, the choice of algorithm—governed by complexity analysis—**directly determines physical safety**.

## 1.3 The Three Primary Complexity Measures

1. **Time Complexity** $T(n)$ — Number of elementary operations (multiplications, additions, comparisons) executed as a function of input size $n$.
2. **Space Complexity** $S(n)$ — Total memory (in words or bytes) required for storing program state, intermediate buffers, and the input itself.
3. **I/O Complexity** — Number of memory accesses, relevant when data resides in cache vs. RAM vs. Flash (critical for embedded DSPs in robots like STM32H7 or Jetson Nano).

These three measures are linked through a fundamental **time-space trade-off**: memoization (caching) reduces $T(n)$ at the cost of $S(n)$, while iterative in-place algorithms reduce $S(n)$ at the cost of $T(n)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Asymptotic Notations

The Big-O family captures *bounds* on the growth rate of $T(n)$ as $n \to \infty$:

### Big-O — Upper Bound (Worst Case Ceiling)
$$
T(n) = O(f(n)) \iff \limsup_{n \to \infty} \frac{T(n)}{f(n)} < \infty
$$

### Big-Ω — Lower Bound (Best Case Floor)
$$
T(n) = \Omega(f(n)) \iff \liminf_{n \to \infty} \frac{T(n)}{f(n)} > 0
$$

### Big-Θ — Tight Bound (Exact Order)
$$
T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) \iff T(n) = O(f(n)) \text{ AND } T(n) = \Omega(f(n))
$$

### Little-o — Strict Upper Bound
$$
T(n) = o(f(n)) \iff \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{T(n)}{f(n)} = 0
$$

### Little-ω — Strict Lower Bound
$$
T(n) = \omega(f(n)) \iff \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{T(n)}{f(n)} = \infty
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** Most KTU questions that ask "the complexity is given by" expect the answer to be in **Big-O form** unless otherwise specified. When asked for *exact* order, use Big-Θ.

## 2.2 The Hierarchy of Complexity Classes (Ordered by Growth)

$$
O(1) \;<\; O(\log \log n) \;<\; O(\log n) \;<\; O(\sqrt{n}) \;<\; O(n) \;<\; O(n \log n) \;<\; O(n^2) \;<\; O(n^3) \;<\; O(2^n) \;<\; O(n!)
$$

Each step in this hierarchy represents an **exponentially harder** jump in resource consumption. For robotics:
- $O(1)$ — Register read, GPIO toggle
- $O(\log n)$ — Binary search in sorted buffer
- $O(n)$ — FIR filter tap evaluation
- $O(n \log n)$ — FFT-based spectral analysis
- $O(n^2)$ — Direct convolution, naive DFT
- $O(n^3)$ — Matrix inversion (Kalman filter state update)

## 2.3 Best, Average, and Worst-Case Analysis

For an algorithm with random or adversarial input, three measures are standard:

$$
T_{\text{best}}(n) = \min_{x \in \mathcal{I}_n} T(x), \qquad
T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \mathbb{E}_{x}[T(x)], \qquad
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \max_{x \in \mathcal{I}_n} T(x)
$$

where $\mathcal{I}_n$ is the set of all inputs of size $n$ and $T(x)$ is the running time on input $x$.

**Example — Quicksort partitioning:**
- Best Case: $T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/2) + \Theta(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ — Pivot is the median
- Worst Case: $T(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n) = \Theta(n^2)$ — Pivot is always the extreme element (sorted input)
- Average Case: $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ — Randomized pivot assumption

## 2.4 The Master Theorem (Divide-and-Conquer Solver)

For recurrences of the form:
$$
T(n) = a \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)
$$

where $a \ge 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ is asymptotically positive, we compare $f(n)$ against $n^{\log_b a}$:

$$
n^{\log_b a} = \begin{cases}
\text{Case 1:} \; f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \varepsilon}) & \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a}) \\[4pt]
\text{Case 2:} \; f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n) & \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n) \\[4pt]
\text{Case 3:} \; f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \varepsilon}) & \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(f(n))
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Master Theorem is the **single most-tested topic** in KTU Module 4 problems. Memorize the three cases and the critical exponent $\log_b a$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Algorithm / Operation | Time Complexity $T(n)$ | Space Complexity $S(n)$ | Robotics Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Direct DFT Computation | $\Theta(N^2)$ | $\Theta(N)$ | Spectrum of vibration sensor |
| 2 | Radix-2 FFT (Cooley-Tukey) | $\Theta(N \log_2 N)$ | $\Theta(N)$ | Real-time audio/sonar processing |
| 3 | Direct Linear Convolution | $\Theta(N \cdot M)$ | $\Theta(N+M)$ | FIR filter output |
| 4 | FFT-based Convolution | $\Theta(N \log N)$ | $\Theta(N)$ | Overlap-add / overlap-save filtering |
| 5 | FIR Filter (per output) | $\Theta(L)$ | $\Theta(L)$ | Moving average, low-pass on IMU |
| 6 | IIR Filter (per output) | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | Real-time noise shaping |
| 7 | Matrix Multiply (naive) | $\Theta(n^3)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Rotation, Jacobian in kinematics |
| 8 | Strassen Multiply | $\Theta(n^{2.807})$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Bulk pose-graph optimization |
| 9 | Gaussian Elimination | $\Theta(n^3)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Linear system solve for IK |
| 10 | LU / Cholesky Decomposition | $\Theta(n^3)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Kalman filter covariance update |
| 11 | Kalman Filter (full) | $\Theta(n^3)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Sensor fusion in localization |
| 12 | Binary Search | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | Lookup in sorted trajectory table |
| 13 | Bubble / Insertion Sort | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | Rarely used; educational baseline |
| 14 | Merge Sort / Heap Sort | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ / $\Theta(1)$ | Sorting LiDAR returns by angle |
| 15 | Quicksort (avg) | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | Median finding, partition-based SLAM |

> **Notation Note:** Above, $N$ denotes signal length, $L$ denotes FIR tap count, $M$ denotes filter length, $n$ denotes matrix dimension. Vertical bars in this table are rendered as standard delimiters; in equations, $\vert x \vert$ denotes absolute value.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Robotics Engineering

1. **Autonomous Vehicles (L4/L5):** A self-driving car's perception stack processes $\approx 2\,\text{GB/s}$ of sensor data. Each frame requires FFT-based obstacle detection within $33\,\text{ms}$ (30 Hz loop). Using $O(N^2)$ algorithms is **infeasible**—only $O(N \log N)$ passes the timing budget.
2. **Embedded SLAM on Drones:** A quadrotor running Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) must solve a $\Theta(n^3)$ bundle adjustment on $n \le 50$ feature points in $<5\,\text{ms}$ to maintain hover stability. The complexity bound dictates the **maximum feature count**.
3. **Brain-Computer Interface Prosthetics:** EMG signal classification must finish in $<1\,\text{ms}$. This excludes all polynomial-time algorithms beyond $O(n^2)$ on raw signal vectors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Direct DFT Complexity

The Discrete Fourier Transform of an $N$-point signal $x[n]$ is:

$$
X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot W_N^{kn}, \quad W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1
$$

**Step 1 — Count complex multiplications per output:**
Each computation of $X[k]$ requires $N$ complex multiplications of the form $x[n] \cdot W_N^{kn}$.

**Step 2 — Count total outputs:**
There are $N$ values of $k$, producing $N$ output bins.

**Step 3 — Total multiplications:**
$$
M_{\text{mult}} = N \times N = N^2
$$

**Step 4 — Count complex additions per output:**
Each summation involves $N-1$ complex additions (the first term needs zero additions).

**Step 5 — Total additions:**
$$
M_{\text{add}} = N \times (N-1) = N^2 - N
$$

**Step 6 — Combined time complexity:**
$$
T_{\text{DFT}}(N) = N^2 + (N^2 - N) = 2N^2 - N = \Theta(N^2)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{\text{DFT}}(N) = \Theta(N^2)\,}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Radix-2 FFT Complexity

The Cooley-Tukey FFT splits the DFT into even-indexed and odd-indexed subsequences using the *twiddle factor identity*:

$$
X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{kn} = \underbrace{\sum_{r=0}^{N/2-1} x[2r] W_{N/2}^{kr}}_{\text{Even part } E[k]} + W_N^k \underbrace{\sum_{r=0}^{N/2-1} x[2r+1] W_{N/2}^{kr}}_{\text{Odd part } O[k]}
$$

**Step 1 — Formulate the recurrence:**
Each sub-DFT has length $N/2$, and the combination step costs $N$ operations:

$$
T_{\text{FFT}}(N) = 2 \cdot T_{\text{FFT}}\!\left(\frac{N}{2}\right) + N
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Master Theorem parameters:**
Comparing with $T(n) = a \, T(n/b) + f(n)$:
$$
a = 2, \qquad b = 2, \qquad f(n) = N
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the critical exponent:**
$$
n^{\log_b a} = N^{\log_2 2} = N^1 = N
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Master Theorem, Case 2:**
Since $f(n) = \Theta(N) = \Theta(N^{\log_2 2} \cdot \log^0 N)$, we are in **Case 2** with $k = 0$:

$$
T_{\text{FFT}}(N) = \Theta(N \log N)
$$

**Step 5 — Verify by telescoping the recurrence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(N) &= 2T(N/2) + N \\
&= 2\bigl[2T(N/4) + N/2\bigr] + N \\
&= 4T(N/4) + 2N \\
&= 4\bigl[2T(N/8) + N/4\bigr] + 2N \\
&= 8T(N/8) + 3N \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= N \cdot T(1) + N \log_2 N
\end{aligned}
$$

After $\log_2 N$ recursions, the subproblem size reaches $1$. The geometric sum yields:

$$
T(N) = N \cdot T(1) + N \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 N - 1} 1 = N \cdot c + N \log_2 N = \Theta(N \log N)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{\text{FFT}}(N) = \Theta(N \log_2 N)\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speedup Factor of FFT over DFT

For a robot processing $N = 1024$ samples (a typical audio buffer):

**Step 1 — Compute DFT operation count:**
$$
T_{\text{DFT}}(1024) = 1024^2 = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ operations}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FFT operation count:**
$$
T_{\text{FFT}}(1024) = 1024 \cdot \log_2 1024 = 1024 \cdot 10 = 10{,}240 \text{ operations}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute speedup ratio:**
$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{DFT}}}{T_{\text{FFT}}} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{10{,}240} \approx 102.4 \times
$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic generalization:**
$$
\frac{T_{\text{DFT}}}{T_{\text{FFT}}} = \frac{N^2}{N \log_2 N} = \frac{N}{\log_2 N} \to \infty \text{ as } N \to \infty
$$

For $N = 10^6$ samples (a single channel of audio at $48\,\text{kHz}$ over $20\,\text{s}$):
$$
S = \frac{10^6}{\log_2 10^6} \approx \frac{10^6}{20} = 50{,}000 \times
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This speedup is the **single most important engineering result** in DSP and is the reason every modern radar, sonar, and spectrum analyzer uses FFT.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Convolution Complexity: Direct vs. FFT-Based

The linear convolution of two finite sequences $x[n]$ of length $N$ and $h[n]$ of length $M$ is:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k], \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N+M-2
$$

### Direct Convolution

**Step 1 — Output length:** $N + M - 1 \approx N + M$ samples.

**Step 2 — Multiplications per output:** $M$ multiplications.

**Step 3 — Total multiplications:**
$$
T_{\text{direct}} = (N + M) \cdot M \approx NM = \Theta(NM)
$$

For $N = M = L$: $T_{\text{direct}} = \Theta(L^2)$.

### FFT-Based Convolution (Overlap-Add / Overlap-Save)

Using the circular convolution theorem, $\Theta(N \log N)$ per FFT, with the *zero-padding* $L_{\text{FFT}} = N + M - 1$:

**Step 1 — Forward FFT of $x$:** $\Theta(L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}})$
**Step 2 — Forward FFT of $h$:** $\Theta(L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}})$
**Step 3 — Pointwise multiply:** $\Theta(L_{\text{FFT}})$
**Step 4 — Inverse FFT:** $\Theta(L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}})$

$$
T_{\text{FFT-conv}} = 3 \cdot \Theta(L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}}) + \Theta(L_{\text{FFT}}) = \Theta(L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}})
$$

For large $L_{\text{FFT}} \to \infty$, FFT-based convolution is **strictly faster** than direct convolution.

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{T_{\text{direct}}}{T_{\text{FFT-conv}}} = \frac{NM}{L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}}} \to \infty \text{ as } N, M \to \infty\,}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Kalman Filter Complexity

The Kalman filter prediction and update steps involve the following matrix operations on a state vector of dimension $n$:

**Step 1 — State prediction:** $\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1} + B u_k$ — Matrix-vector product: $\Theta(n^2)$.

**Step 2 — Covariance prediction:** $P_{k\vert k-1} = A P_{k-1} A^T + Q$ — Matrix-matrix products: $\Theta(n^3)$.

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance:** $S_k = H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R$ — $\Theta(n^3)$.

**Step 4 — Kalman gain:** $K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H^T S_k^{-1}$ — **Matrix inversion**: $\Theta(n^3)$ via Gaussian elimination.

**Step 5 — State update:** $\Theta(n^2)$.

**Total per iteration:**
$$
T_{\text{Kalman}}(n) = \Theta(n^3)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{\text{Kalman}}(n) = \Theta(n^3)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **extended Kalman filters (EKF)** on a humanoid robot with full-body state ($n \approx 30$–$50$ joints) cost $\approx 30^3 = 27{,}000$ FLOPs per iteration—still tractable on microcontrollers. However, full SLAM with map state $n > 10{,}000$ requires iterative methods or sparsity exploitation to remain real-time.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Asymptotic Proof: $n^2 \notin O(n)$

We prove by contradiction that $n^2$ does **not** belong to $O(n)$.

**Step 1 — Assume for contradiction:** $n^2 = O(n)$. Then by definition, there exist $c > 0$ and $n_0$ such that:
$$
n^2 \le c \cdot n \quad \forall n \ge n_0
$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by $n > 0$:**
$$
n \le c \quad \forall n \ge n_0
$$

**Step 3 — Take the limit:** The above requires $n$ to be bounded above by the constant $c$ for all $n \ge n_0$. But $n$ grows without bound, contradicting the inequality for any $n > c$.

**Conclusion:** $n^2 \notin O(n)$. $\blacksquare$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complexity Class Hierarchy for Robotic Signal Processing

The diagram below maps the growth-rate hierarchy of complexity classes and locates representative DSP operations within it. Lower-positioned nodes indicate algorithms that scale more favourably.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef constantClass fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef logClass fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef linearClass fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nlognClass fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef polyClass fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef expClass fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    s1["O OF 1 - Register Access - GPIO Toggle"]:::constantClass
    s2["O LOG N - Binary Search - Lookup Table"]:::logClass
    s3["O N - FIR Filter - IIR Sample Step"]:::linearClass
    s4["O N LOG N - Radix 2 FFT - Merge Sort"]:::nlognClass
    s5["O N SQUARED - Direct DFT - Naive Convolution"]:::polyClass
    s6["O N CUBED - Matrix Inversion - Kalman Update"]:::polyClass
    s7["O TWO TO N - Brute Force Subset Search - TSP"]:::expClass
    s8["O N FACTORIAL - Permutation Enumeration - Brute IK"]:::expClass

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8

    noteA["EMBEDDED REAL TIME OK"]:::constantClass
    noteB["MARGINAL - USE SPARSITY"]:::polyClass
    noteC["INFEASIBLE FOR N GREATER THAN 25"]:::expClass

    s4 -.-> noteA
    s6 -.-> noteB
    s7 -.-> noteC
```

## 4.2 Real-Time Signal Processing Pipeline in a Mobile Robot

The diagram below illustrates a canonical perception-and-control pipeline used in autonomous ground robots, with the computational complexity of each stage annotated.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef sensorClass fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procClass fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fusionClass fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrlClass fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#99004d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    sens1["LIDAR SENSOR - 10 HZ - 100K POINTS"]:::sensorClass
    sens2["CAMERA - 30 HZ - 2 MP FRAME"]:::sensorClass
    sens3["IMU - 200 HZ - 6 AXIS"]:::sensorClass

    procA["PRE PROCESSING - NOISE FILTER - O N"]:::procClass
    procB["FFT SPECTRUM - VIBRATION - O N LOG N"]:::procClass
    procC["FEATURE EXTRACT - CORNERS EDGES - O N"]:::procClass

    fusA["KALMAN FUSION - STATE VEC - O N CUBED"]:::fusionClass
    fusB["SLAM BACKEND - POSE GRAPH - O M CUBED"]:::fusionClass

    ctrlA["PATH PLANNER - A STAR - O E LOG V"]:::ctrlClass
    ctrlB["MOTOR CONTROLLER - PID - O 1"]:::ctrlClass

    sens1 --> procA --> fusA --> ctrlA --> ctrlB
    sens2 --> procC --> fusA
    sens3 --> procB --> fusA
    fusA --> fusB --> ctrlA
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Algorithm Selection for a Robotic DSP Task

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef decisionClass fill:#fffacd,stroke:#8b8000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resultClass fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rejectClass fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    q1{"IS N LESS THAN 50"}:::decisionClass
    q2{"IS REAL TIME LESS THAN 1 MS"}:::decisionClass
    q3{"IS THE PROBLEM DECOMPOSABLE"}:::decisionClass

    r1["USE O N CUBED - KALMAN ACCEPTABLE"]:::resultClass
    r2["USE O N LOG N - FFT OPTIMAL"]:::resultClass
    r3["USE DIVIDE AND CONQUER - REDUCE TO SUBPROBLEMS"]:::resultClass
    r4["REJECT BRUTE FORCE - USE HEURISTIC OR LEARNING"]:::rejectClass

    q1 -->|YES| r1
    q1 -->|NO| q2
    q2 -->|YES| r2
    q2 -->|NO| q3
    q3 -->|YES| r3
    q3 -->|NO| r4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define computational complexity. Distinguish between time complexity and space complexity with a suitable robotic signal processing example.**

**Model Answer:**

Computational complexity is the asymptotic measure of the resources—**time** (number of elementary operations) and **space** (memory footprint)—consumed by an algorithm as a function of the input size $n$, independent of hardware.

- **Time Complexity** $T(n)$: Quantifies the number of arithmetic/logic operations.
- **Space Complexity** $S(n)$: Quantifies the total auxiliary memory required.

**Robotic Example:** An FIR filter of length $L$ processing a buffer of $N$ samples has $T(n) = \Theta(L \cdot N)$ multiplications, but $S(n) = \Theta(L)$ words (coefficient storage). The time depends on signal length, while the space is fixed by filter order.

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State and explain Big-O, Big-Ω, and Big-Θ notations with a one-line definition each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Notation | Meaning | Mathematical Statement |
|---|---|---|
| $T(n) = O(f(n))$ | Upper bound (worst case) | $\exists c, n_0 : T(n) \le c \cdot f(n)$ for $n \ge n_0$ |
| $T(n) = \Omega(f(n))$ | Lower bound (best case) | $\exists c, n_0 : T(n) \ge c \cdot f(n)$ for $n \ge n_0$ |
| $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ | Tight bound (exact order) | Both $O$ and $\Omega$ hold simultaneously |

For example, $T(n) = 3n^2 + 5n = \Theta(n^2)$ since it is bounded above and below by constants times $n^2$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**(a)** Derive the time complexity of the **Direct Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** algorithm for an $N$-point signal. Show every step clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the time complexity of the **Radix-2 Cooley-Tukey FFT** using the Master Theorem. Compare the asymptotic complexity of FFT vs. DFT and compute the speedup factor for $N = 2048$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The $N$-point DFT is defined as:

$$
X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot W_N^{kn}, \quad W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1
$$

**Step 1 — Multiplications per output bin:** Each computation of a single $X[k]$ requires $N$ complex multiplications ($x[n] \cdot W_N^{kn}$ for $n = 0, \dots, N-1$).

**Step 2 — Total multiplications:** There are $N$ bins to compute:

$$
M_{\text{mult}} = N \times N = N^2
$$

**Step 3 — Additions per output:** Summing $N$ terms requires $N - 1$ complex additions.

**Step 4 — Total additions:**

$$
M_{\text{add}} = N \times (N - 1) = N^2 - N
$$

**Step 5 — Total operations and Big-O:**

$$
T_{\text{DFT}}(N) = N^2 + (N^2 - N) = 2N^2 - N = \Theta(N^2)
$$

**Conclusion:** The direct DFT exhibits **quadratic time complexity**.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Apply the twiddle-factor decomposition:**

$$
X[k] = \sum_{r=0}^{N/2 - 1} x[2r] W_{N/2}^{kr} + W_N^k \sum_{r=0}^{N/2 - 1} x[2r+1] W_{N/2}^{kr}
$$

**Step 2 — Formulate the divide-and-conquer recurrence:**

$$
T(N) = 2 \, T\!\left(\frac{N}{2}\right) + N
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Master Theorem parameters:** $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(N) = N$. Critical exponent: $\log_b a = \log_2 2 = 1$, so $N^{\log_b a} = N$.

**Step 4 — Apply Master Theorem Case 2:** Since $f(N) = \Theta(N) = \Theta(N^1 \log^0 N)$, we have $k = 0$, yielding:

$$
T_{\text{FFT}}(N) = \Theta(N \log N)
$$

**Step 5 — Comparative analysis:**

$$
\frac{T_{\text{DFT}}}{T_{\text{FFT}}} = \frac{N^2}{N \log_2 N} = \frac{N}{\log_2 N}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical speedup at $N = 2048$:**

$$
\frac{T_{\text{DFT}}}{T_{\text{FFT}}} = \frac{2048}{\log_2 2048} = \frac{2048}{11} \approx 186.2
$$

**Conclusion:** FFT is approximately **186 times faster** than DFT for $N = 2048$, demonstrating its indispensability for real-time robotic spectral analysis.

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**(a)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$ using the Master Theorem and interpret the result for an $n \times n$ image processed on a robotic vision system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with derivations the asymptotic complexity of **direct linear convolution** vs. **FFT-based convolution** for two sequences of length $N$ and $M$. State one robotic application where FFT-based convolution is preferred. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Identify Master Theorem parameters:** $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^2$.

**Step 2 — Compute critical exponent:**

$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 4} = n^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compare $f(n)$ to critical exponent:** $f(n) = n^2 = \Theta(n^2 \log^0 n)$. This matches **Case 2** of the Master Theorem with $k = 0$.

**Step 4 — Conclude:**

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^2 \log n)
$$

**Step 5 — Robotic Interpretation:** For an $n \times n$ image, a recursive image-processing algorithm that splits the image into 4 quadrants ($n/2$ each) with $\Theta(n^2)$ per-level work exhibits this complexity. Example: **quad-tree-based image filtering** or **hierarchical SLAM feature matching**. The $n^2 \log n$ factor indicates that doubling image resolution increases work by **more than 4×**, a critical scaling consideration for embedded vision processors like the Jetson Orin Nano.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Direct Convolution Complexity:**
For two sequences $x[n]$ (length $N$) and $h[n]$ (length $M$), direct convolution yields:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k], \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N+M-2
$$

- Output length: $N + M - 1 \approx N + M$.
- Multiplications per output: $M$.
- Total multiplications: $(N + M) \cdot M \approx \Theta(NM)$.

For $N = M = L$: $T_{\text{direct}} = \Theta(L^2)$.

**Step 2 — FFT-Based Convolution Complexity:**
Padding both sequences to length $L_{\text{FFT}} = N + M - 1$:

- Forward FFT of $x$: $\Theta(L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}})$
- Forward FFT of $h$: $\Theta(L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}})$
- Pointwise multiply: $\Theta(L_{\text{FFT}})$
- Inverse FFT: $\Theta(L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}})$

$$
T_{\text{FFT-conv}} = 3 \, \Theta(L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}}) + \Theta(L_{\text{FFT}}) = \Theta(L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}})
$$

**Step 3 — Crossover analysis:** For large $L_{\text{FFT}}$, FFT-based convolution wins because $\log L_{\text{FFT}} \ll L_{\text{FFT}}$. Specifically:

$$
\frac{T_{\text{direct}}}{T_{\text{FFT-conv}}} \approx \frac{NM}{L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}}} \to \infty \text{ as } N, M \to \infty
$$

**Step 4 — Robotic Application:** **Real-time FIR noise cancellation in hearing-aid robots or active-noise-control drones.** With $M > 256$ taps and $N > 4096$-sample audio buffers, FFT-based overlap-save convolution is the **only feasible choice** for sub-millisecond latency on an ARM Cortex-M7 DSP.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dominant term:** Writing $T_{\text{DFT}} = 2N^2 - N$ without simplifying to $\Theta(N^2)$ loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Misapplying Master Theorem:** Students often confuse Case 2 with $f(n) \le n^{\log_b a}$. The correct condition is $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$. Forgetting the $\log^k$ factor yields wrong cases.
> 3. **Confusing little-o with Big-O:** $o(n)$ means **strictly** smaller than $n$, not just bounded by it.
> 4. **Ignoring base cases:** The Master Theorem requires the base case $T(1) = \Theta(1)$. Stating $T(N) = N \log N$ without justifying $T(1)$ loses partial credit.
> 5. **Wrong log base:** KTU expects $\log_2$ for FFT (since $b = 2$). Writing $\log_{10}$ loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting space complexity:** "Time complexity" alone is incomplete for Module 4 questions; always mention auxiliary space if asked about "computational complexity" broadly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Computational complexity** quantifies resource scaling via asymptotic notations: $O$ (upper), $\Omega$ (lower), $\Theta$ (tight), $o$ (strict upper), $\omega$ (strict lower).
- **Big-O** is the most commonly tested bound; use it unless the question explicitly asks for exact order ($\Theta$).
- **Hierarchy:** $O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(n^3) < O(2^n) < O(n!)$.
- **DFT** is $\Theta(N^2)$; **Radix-2 FFT** is $\Theta(N \log_2 N)$; **speedup ratio** $= N / \log_2 N$.
- **Direct convolution** of $(N, M)$ sequences costs $\Theta(NM)$; **FFT-based** costs $\Theta(L_{\text{FFT}} \log L_{\text{FFT}})$.
- **Matrix inversion** (and thus Kalman filter update) costs $\Theta(n^3)$ — the dominant cost in robotic state estimation.
- **Master Theorem** solves $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ via three cases comparing $f(n)$ to $n^{\log_b a}$.
- **Best, average, worst case** apply to randomized or input-dependent algorithms (e.g., Quicksort).
- **Time-space trade-off** is a foundational design principle — memoization trades memory for speed, iterative in-place algorithms trade speed for memory.
- **Real-time robotic constraint:** If the algorithm's $T(n)$ exceeds the sampling deadline (typically $1$–$10\,\text{ms}$ for control loops), the robot cannot operate safely regardless of algorithmic correctness.
- **Embedded DSP tip:** Always check the dominant term at the expected operating point $n = n_0$, not just asymptotic limit. An $O(n^2)$ algorithm with small constant factor may beat $O(n \log n)$ with large constants for $n < 100$.
- **Common KTU traps:** Misnaming $o$ vs $O$, confusing Big-$\Theta$ with average case, and ignoring the base case of recurrences.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circular Convolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT831 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Circular Convolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Circular Convolution** of two periodic sequences (or finite sequences of length $N$ treated as periodic with period $N$) is a discrete-time mathematical operation defined as the **time-domain dual of pointwise multiplication in the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) domain**. It is the cornerstone operation that allows linear filtering of periodic signals in the frequency domain.

For two $N$-point sequences $x[n]$ and $h[n]$, their **$N$-point circular convolution** is denoted by the operator $\circledast$ and is mathematically defined as:

$$y[n] = (x[n] \circledast h[n])_N = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \cdot h[(n-m) \bmod N], \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

where the modulo-$N$ operation $\bmod N$ ensures that the index $(n-m)$ is wrapped around to lie within the principal interval $[0, N-1]$. This wrap-around (or **time-aliasing**) behaviour is the defining characteristic that differentiates circular convolution from its linear counterpart.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> - $N$ is referred to as the **convolution length** or **block length**
> - $h[(n-m) \bmod N]$ is called the **circularly time-reversed and shifted** version of $h[n]$
> - $y[n]$ is **always an $N$-point sequence**, regardless of the physical length of the linear convolution output

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Robotic Windshield Wiper

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Intuition: The Spinning Radar Dish**
>
> Imagine a radar dish on an autonomous robot performing a $360^\circ$ sweep. The dish has **$N$ discrete angular sectors**, and at each sector it captures a sensor reading $x[m]$. The impulse response $h[k]$ represents how the radar's antenna "remembers" the previous sector.
>
> Now, when you compute the output for sector $n$, the dish doesn't just look at sector $n$ — it *wraps* around: it looks at sector $n$ in the **current rotation** AND remembers the contribution from sector $(n - N)$ which is **the same sector in the previous rotation**.
>
> This is exactly what the **modulo-$N$** operator does: when the index goes below $0$, it wraps back to the top of the circle. The result is a "blurred" output where the last samples influence the first ones — a feature of **all periodic/circular systems**.

In robotics, this periodic boundary behaviour is naturally exhibited in:
- **Panoramic camera images** (cylindrical wrap-around)
- **LiDAR scan data** (rotational wrap at $360^\circ$)
- **Audio signals in echoic enclosures** (reflections re-entering the frame)

## 1.3 Why Circular Convolution is Critical in Robotics

Modern robotic systems process signals in blocks (e.g., sliding window FFT for real-time control). The DFT/FFT algorithm — the computational engine behind most robotic vision and audio modules — is fundamentally based on **circular convolution**, not linear convolution. Understanding the distinction is mandatory for:
- Designing **digital filters** (FIR/IIR) in the frequency domain
- Performing **fast block convolution** via FFT (the *overlap-add* and *overlap-save* methods)
- Computing **cross-correlations** in SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)
- Implementing **template matching** in computer vision

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation — Step-by-Step Logic

To compute $y[n] = (x[n] \circledast h[n])_N$ for any $n \in \{0, 1, \dots, N-1\}$, the following structured procedure is applied:

**Step 1 — Fix the output index $n$:** Choose a specific $n$ for which the output sample $y[n]$ is to be computed.

**Step 2 — Time-reverse $h$:** Replace $h[k]$ with $h[-k]$, effectively mirroring the sequence about $k = 0$.

**Step 3 — Circular shift by $n$:** Shift the reversed sequence to the right by $n$ samples. If the shift pushes samples outside $[0, N-1]$, they **wrap around** to the beginning.

**Step 4 — Pointwise multiplication:** Multiply the original $x[m]$ sample-by-sample with the shifted, reversed $h$.

**Step 5 — Summation:** Sum all $N$ products to obtain a single scalar $y[n]$.

**Step 6 — Repeat for all $n$:** Iterate $n$ from $0$ to $N-1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Modulo Operator**
>
> Unlike linear convolution where the sequences have finite support on $[0, \infty)$, circular convolution assumes both sequences are **periodic with period $N$**. Hence, $h[(n-m) \bmod N]$ denotes the sample of the periodic extension of $h$ at the equivalent index inside one period.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every essential identity, condition, and result on circular convolution that is examinable under Module 4 of the OERAT831 syllabus.

| **#** | **Identity / Property** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Boundary / Validity Condition** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Definition (Modulo Form) | $y[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \cdot h[(n-m) \bmod N]$ | $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$ |
| 2 | Definition (Index Form) | $y[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[(n-m) \bmod N] \cdot h[m]$ | $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$ |
| 3 | **Circular Convolution Theorem** | $x[n] \circledast h[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DFT}} X[k] \cdot H[k]$ | $N$-point DFT/IDFT pair |
| 4 | **Circular Correlation Theorem** | $x[n] \circledast h^*[(-n) \bmod N] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DFT}} X[k] \cdot H^*[k]$ | Forged from CC Theorem |
| 5 | Time-Aliasing Relation | $y_c[n] = \sum_{r=-\infty}^{\infty} y_l[n + rN]$ | $y_c = $ circular, $y_l = $ linear |
| 6 | Equivalence with Linear | $y_c[n] = y_l[n]$ | IFF $N \geq L_x + L_h - 1$ |
| 7 | Commutativity | $x[n] \circledast h[n] = h[n] \circledast x[n]$ | Always holds |
| 8 | Associativity | $(x \circledast h) \circledast g = x \circledast (h \circledast g)$ | Always holds |
| 9 | Distributivity | $x \circledast (h + g) = (x \circledast h) + (x \circledast g)$ | Always holds |
| 10 | Identity Element | $x[n] \circledast \delta[n] = x[n]$ | $\delta[n] = $ unit impulse |
| 11 | Periodicity of Output | $y[n + N] = y[n]$ | By definition |
| 12 | Number of Output Samples | $\text{length}(y) = N$ | Block length is fixed |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Pitfall:** In the exam, the *time-aliased* linear convolution samples are **not** added mod-$N$; they are added **arithmetically** and then the modulo-$N$ index fold is performed on the *time* axis. Many students incorrectly reverse the order.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application** | **Role of Circular Convolution** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Robotic Audio Processing | Echo cancellation, beamforming | Fast block convolution via FFT |
| Computer Vision (CV) | Template matching, motion blur deconv. | Circular correlation in image kernels |
| Wireless Communication | OFDM symbol equalization | Cyclic prefix exploits circular nature |
| Radar & LiDAR | Pulse compression, matched filtering | Circular cross-correlation in FFT |
| Control Systems | Frequency-domain controller design | Convolution theorem for transfer function evaluation |

The **circular convolution theorem** is the gateway to **fast convolution**: filtering an $L$-point signal with an $M$-point filter takes $O(L \cdot M)$ operations directly, but only $O(N \log N)$ operations via FFT-based circular convolution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of the Circular Convolution Theorem

We seek to prove that the IDFT of the **pointwise product** of two DFTs equals the **circular convolution** of the original time-domain sequences.

**Statement:** If $X[k] = \text{DFT}\{x[n]\}$ and $H[k] = \text{DFT}\{h[n]\}$, both $N$-point, then

$$y[n] = \text{IDFT}\{X[k] \cdot H[k]\} = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \cdot h[(n-m) \bmod N] = (x \circledast h)_N[n]$$

**Proof:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IDFT}\{X[k] \cdot H[k]\} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left( X[k] \cdot H[k] \right) e^{j 2\pi k n / N}
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute the DFT expansions of $X[k]$ and $H[k]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
&= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[ \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \, e^{-j 2\pi k m / N} \right] \left[ \sum_{p=0}^{N-1} h[p] \, e^{-j 2\pi k p / N} \right] e^{j 2\pi k n / N}
\end{aligned}$$

Rearrange the order of summation (associativity of finite sums):

$$\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \sum_{p=0}^{N-1} x[m] \, h[p] \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (n - m - p) / N} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

Recognize the **orthogonality identity** of complex exponentials:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (n - m - p) / N} = 
\begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } (n - m - p) \text{ is a multiple of } N \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

This is non-zero only when $p = (n - m) \bmod N$. Substituting this constraint eliminates the sum over $p$:

$$\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \cdot h[(n - m) \bmod N] = (x \circledast h)_N[n]
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The IDFT of $X[k] \cdot H[k]$ is precisely the $N$-point circular convolution. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Concentric Circle (Graphical) Method

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** Compute the 4-point circular convolution of $x[n] = \{1, 2, 0, 1\}$ and $h[n] = \{2, 2, 1, 1\}$. Use the **concentric circle method**. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Plot the two sequences on concentric circles.**

The outer circle holds $x[m]$ with $N = 4$ samples equally spaced in the **counter-clockwise** direction. The inner circle holds $h[m]$.

```
   Outer Circle (x[m]) — counter-clockwise
   m=0: 1       m=1: 2
        \         /
         \       /
   m=3: 1 ----- m=2: 0
   
   Inner Circle (h[m]) — clockwise
   m=0: 2       m=3: 1
        \         /
         \       /
   m=1: 2 ----- m=2: 1
```

**Step 2 — Align $h$ with $x$ initially (for $n = 0$).**

The reference arrow on $h$ (at $m = 0$) is aligned with $x[0]$.

**Step 3 — Compute $y[0]$: Pair-wise product of overlapping samples.**

$$\begin{aligned}
y[0] &= x[0] \cdot h[0] + x[1] \cdot h[3] + x[2] \cdot h[2] + x[3] \cdot h[1] \\
y[0] &= (1)(2) + (2)(1) + (0)(1) + (1)(2) \\
y[0] &= 2 + 2 + 0 + 2 = 6
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Rotate the inner circle one step counter-clockwise to get $y[1]$.**

After rotation, $h[0]$ aligns with $x[1]$, $h[1]$ aligns with $x[2]$, $h[2]$ aligns with $x[3]$, $h[3]$ aligns with $x[0]$.

$$\begin{aligned}
y[1] &= x[1] \cdot h[0] + x[2] \cdot h[1] + x[3] \cdot h[2] + x[0] \cdot h[3] \\
y[1] &= (2)(2) + (0)(2) + (1)(1) + (1)(1) \\
y[1] &= 4 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 6
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Rotate the inner circle one more step to compute $y[2]$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
y[2] &= x[2] \cdot h[0] + x[3] \cdot h[1] + x[0] \cdot h[2] + x[1] \cdot h[3] \\
y[2] &= (0)(2) + (1)(2) + (1)(1) + (2)(1) \\
y[2] &= 0 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Rotate the inner circle one more step to compute $y[3]$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
y[3] &= x[3] \cdot h[0] + x[0] \cdot h[1] + x[1] \cdot h[2] + x[2] \cdot h[3] \\
y[3] &= (1)(2) + (1)(2) + (2)(1) + (0)(1) \\
y[3] &= 2 + 2 + 2 + 0 = 6
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$y[n] = (x[n] \circledast h[n])_4 = \{6, \; 6, \; 5, \; 6\}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Circulant Matrix Method (Most Common KTU Pattern)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** Compute the 4-point circular convolution of $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $h[n] = \{4, 3, 2, 1\}$ using the **circulant matrix method**. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Construct the $N \times N$ circulant matrix using $h[n]$ as the first row.**

The circulant matrix is built by **right-cyclic shifts** of the first row.

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{H} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
h[0] & h[N-1] & h[N-2] & h[N-3] \\
h[1] & h[0]   & h[N-1] & h[N-2] \\
h[2] & h[1]   & h[0]   & h[N-1] \\
h[3] & h[2]   & h[1]   & h[0]
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
4 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\
3 & 4 & 1 & 2 \\
2 & 3 & 4 & 1 \\
1 & 2 & 3 & 4
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Stack $x[n]$ as a column vector and form the matrix product $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H} \cdot \mathbf{x}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x} = 
\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Perform the matrix multiplication, row by row.**

$$\begin{aligned}
y[0] &= (4)(1) + (1)(2) + (2)(3) + (3)(4) = 4 + 2 + 6 + 12 = 24 \\
y[1] &= (3)(1) + (4)(2) + (1)(3) + (2)(4) = 3 + 8 + 3 + 8 = 22 \\
y[2] &= (2)(1) + (3)(2) + (4)(3) + (1)(4) = 2 + 6 + 12 + 4 = 24 \\
y[3] &= (1)(1) + (2)(2) + (3)(3) + (4)(4) = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 = 30
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the output vector.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H} \cdot \mathbf{x} =
\begin{bmatrix} 24 \\ 22 \\ 24 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$y[n] = (x[n] \circledast h[n])_4 = \{24, \; 22, \; 24, \; 30\}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Comparison: Linear vs Circular Convolution

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** For the sequences $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $h[n] = \{4, 3, 2, 1\}$ of the previous example, compute the **linear convolution** $y_l[n]$ and the **circular convolution** $y_c[n]$ for $N = 4$. Verify the time-aliasing relation. (4 marks)

**Step 1 — Linear convolution $y_l[n] = x[n] * h[n]$.**

The linear convolution length is $L_x + L_h - 1 = 4 + 4 - 1 = 7$ samples.

$$\begin{aligned}
y_l[0] &= x[0] \cdot h[0] = (1)(4) = 4 \\
y_l[1] &= x[0] h[1] + x[1] h[0] = (1)(3) + (2)(4) = 3 + 8 = 11 \\
y_l[2] &= x[0] h[2] + x[1] h[1] + x[2] h[0] = (1)(2) + (2)(3) + (3)(4) = 2 + 6 + 12 = 20 \\
y_l[3] &= x[0] h[3] + x[1] h[2] + x[2] h[1] + x[3] h[0] = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 = 30 \\
y_l[4] &= x[1] h[3] + x[2] h[2] + x[3] h[1] = 2 + 6 + 12 = 20 \\
y_l[5] &= x[2] h[3] + x[3] h[2] = 3 + 8 = 11 \\
y_l[6] &= x[3] h[3] = 4
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{y_l[n] = \{4, \; 11, \; 20, \; 30, \; 20, \; 11, \; 4\}}$$

**Step 2 — Circular convolution $y_c[n]$ (already computed in Example 2).**

$$y_c[n] = \{24, \; 22, \; 24, \; 30\}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the time-aliasing relation $y_c[n] = \sum_{r} y_l[n + rN]$ for $N = 4$.**

Since $y_l$ is non-zero only on $[0, 6]$, only $r = 0$ and $r = 1$ contribute (and $r = -1$ for $n \geq 4$ — none apply here).

$$\begin{aligned}
y_c[0] &= y_l[0] + y_l[4] = 4 + 20 = 24 \quad \checkmark \\
y_c[1] &= y_l[1] + y_l[5] = 11 + 11 = 22 \quad \checkmark \\
y_c[2] &= y_l[2] + y_l[6] = 20 + 4 = 24 \quad \checkmark \\
y_c[3] &= y_l[3] + y_l[7] = 30 + 0 = 30 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

All four values match exactly, confirming the **time-aliasing principle**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The two results differ because the circular convolution treats $x$ and $h$ as periodic, "folding" the tail of the linear convolution back onto the head. They become equal **if and only if** $N \geq L_x + L_h - 1 = 7$ in this example.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Computation Methodology — Master Flowchart

The following block diagram maps the four principal methods for computing $N$-point circular convolution in the OERAT831 syllabus. Each method is suited to a different application context in robotics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START: Two N-point sequences x n and h n])
    inputNode[/Input: x n, h n, N/]
    decisionNode{QChoose Computation Method?}
    matrixNode[Method 1: Circulant Matrix]
    circleNode[Method 2: Concentric Circle Graphical]
    dftNode[Method 3: DFT IDFT Fast]
    directNode[Method 4: Direct Modulo Formula]
    resultNode[/Output: y n = x n circledast h n/]
    endNode([END: 14 mark question solved])
    
    startNode --> inputNode
    inputNode --> decisionNode
    decisionNode --> matrixNode
    decisionNode --> circleNode
    decisionNode --> dftNode
    decisionNode --> directNode
    matrixNode --> resultNode
    circleNode --> resultNode
    dftNode --> resultNode
    directNode --> resultNode
    resultNode --> endNode
```

## 4.2 Robotic Signal Processing Pipeline

The block-level functional architecture below illustrates how circular convolution is embedded in a real-time robotic control loop. The DFT-domain multiplication exploits the circular convolution theorem, enabling fast filtering of sensor streams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorNode[Robot Sensors Lidar IMU Camera]
    adcNode[ADC and Sampler]
    xnNode[Discrete Signal x n of length N]
    hnNode[Filter Impulse Response h n]
    dft1Node[DFT Block of x n]
    dft2Node[DFT Block of h n]
    multNode[Pointwise Multiplier]
    idftNode[IDFT Block of Y k]
    ynNode[Filtered Output y n]
    ctrlNode[Motor Controller]
    actNode[Robot Actuators Wheels Joints]
    
    sensorNode --> adcNode
    adcNode --> xnNode
    xnNode --> dft1Node
    hnNode --> dft2Node
    dft1Node --> multNode
    dft2Node --> multNode
    multNode --> idftNode
    idftNode --> ynNode
    ynNode --> ctrlNode
    ctrlNode --> actNode
```

## 4.3 Concentric Circle Operation — Algorithmic View

The graphical "concentric circle" method is best understood as the following iterative algorithmic loop, where the inner circle is rotated one step at a time.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initNode[/Initialize: Place x n on outer circle and h n on inner circle/]
    loopNode{For n equals 0 to N minus 1}
    alignNode[Align h 0 with x n]
    pairNode[Form N overlapping pairs x m and h n minus m mod N]
    prodNode[Compute N pointwise products]
    sumNode[Sum the N products to obtain y n]
    rotateNode[Rotate inner circle by one step counter clockwise]
    nextNode[Increment n]
    outNode[/Output y n/]
    doneNode([END])
    
    initNode --> loopNode
    loopNode --> alignNode
    alignNode --> pairNode
    pairNode --> prodNode
    prodNode --> sumNode
    sumNode --> rotateNode
    rotateNode --> nextNode
    nextNode --> loopNode
    loopNode --> outNode
    outNode --> doneNode
```

## 4.4 Equivalence Condition: Linear vs Circular

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1Node{Is N greater than or equal to L x plus L h minus 1?}
    yesNode[Yes: Circular Convolution equals Linear Convolution]
    noNode[No: Time aliasing occurs. y c n equals sum of time shifted y l samples]
    padNode[Remedy: Zero pad both sequences to length N greater than or equal to L x plus L h minus 1]
    
    q1Node --> yesNode
    q1Node --> noNode
    noNode --> padNode
```
